issues.title: Build item->agent matcher never binds implementation items to the generated python-implementer; every impl item falls back to general-purpose even after equip
issues.evidence:
build next [mcp-server]: #9 `app/core/mcp/server.py` — `build_mcp_server` constructs `FastMCP(...)` and registers tools + resources
  agent: general-purpose (fallback)
  subagent_type: general-purpose ... [assistant:] "the build's deterministic matcher still maps item #9 to `general-purpose` — the tuned agents exist now, but this particular item's text didn't bind to `python-implementer`'s capability keywords (server-wiring isn't a clean match)"
issues.what_happened: After /dummyindex-equip wrote python-implementer/python-tester/backend-reviewer and equipment.json (schema v2), `dummyindex context build --next` STILL returned `agent: general-purpose (fallback)` for every implementation item: #9, #10, #13, #14, #15, #18, #19, #20, #22, #23, #28 (11 post-equip items; 21 total fallback outputs across the session). Only test items (#17, #21, #24, #25 -> python-tester) and #26 (-> backend-reviewer) ever bound. Claude compensated by manually dispatching subagent_type=python-implementer for each impl item, ignoring the CLI's mapping — i.e. the deterministic routing layer that justifies equip contributed nothing for the dominant item type.
issues.root_cause_guess: The capability-keyword matcher in build's item->equipment resolution is too narrow: python-implementer's capability keywords (likely 'implement', stack words) don't match checklist items phrased as file-path + contract descriptions ('app/core/mcp/server.py — build_mcp_server constructs ...'). Tester/reviewer keywords ('tests', 'Docs') match incidentally. Matcher needs a default 'code-change items -> stack implementer' rule rather than keyword binding.

issues.title: Equip adopts 'Frontend Developer' registry specialist as the covering specialist for a pure-backend FastAPI repo and a security/auth-heavy MCP proposal
issues.evidence: equip plan (--dry-run, nothing written): ... adopt   agent  Frontend Developer  ->  (registry specialist) ... [assistant:] "One oddity worth flagging: it wants to **adopt \"Frontend Developer\"** as the covering specialist for this proposal — surprising for a security/auth-heavy *backend* MCP port (a Security specialist would fit better). ... The catalog made that call from the plan text; I won't override it." ... "Frontend Developer adopted as the proposal's covering specialist — manifest-only (empty `grounded_in`, no file). As flagged, it's a poor cover for a backend/auth port; I won't route MCP work to it"
issues.what_happened: `dummyindex context equip --for-proposal mcp-server` on a repo detected as stack=python frameworks=['FastAPI'] (no frontend at all) chose 'Frontend Developer' from the registry as the gap-covering specialist for an MCP server/auth/JWT proposal. It landed in equipment.json with subagent_type='Frontend Developer' and grounded_in=[]. Claude explicitly flagged it as a wrong match and never used it — dead/wrong wiring in the manifest.
issues.root_cause_guess: Coverage-gap resolution scores registry specialists by naive keyword overlap with plan text (MCP tools serving 'clients building UI' / design-tokens wording likely matched 'frontend'), and/or the documented 'frontend stays adopt-fallback' default fires regardless of detected stack. No stack-consistency check (python/FastAPI backend should exclude frontend adoption) and no security-specialist mapping for auth/JWT-heavy proposals.

issues.title: Build ran the first 9 items on general-purpose fallback because plan didn't auto-equip and build neither stopped nor suggested equipping
issues.evidence:
build next [mcp-server]: #0 `fastmcp` (v2) added to `pyproject.toml`...
  agent: general-purpose (fallback)
  subagent_type: general-purpose ... [assistant, when user finally ran /dummyindex-equip mid-build:] "the build CLI has been falling back to `general-purpose` because there's no `.context/equipment.json` — equipping will generate project-tuned implementer/tester/reviewer agents so the remaining items map to better-fitted agents."
issues.what_happened: The /dummyindex-plan (v0.15.0) flow produced the proposal without equipping the repo, and /dummyindex-build silently dispatched general-purpose agents for items #0–#8. Only when the user manually invoked /dummyindex-equip mid-build (after 9/42 items) did tuned agents exist. The unequipped state was never surfaced as a warning by the CLI or skill — the user had to know to equip.
issues.root_cause_guess: v0.15.0 plan/build skills had no equip integration: plan didn't run `equip apply --for-proposal` and build had no 'not equipped -> stop and warn' gate (both were added in later versions, confirming the gap). The CLI's silent fallback hides the missing toolkit.

issues.title: User had to interrupt the build to demand verification of already-built items — build loop was ticking boxes from subagent self-reports
issues.evidence: [user:] "before you move on u need to verufy already pre built sections" ... [assistant:] "Right call — before the architectural crux, let me run a full verification pass over everything built so far (items #0–#8). This also catches a real risk: installing `fastmcp` bumped `pydantic` (2.12.5) and `starlette` (1.0.0), so I need to confirm the **existing test suite still passes** with those transitive bumps."
issues.what_happened: During /dummyindex-build, Claude checked off items #0–#8 immediately after each subagent reported completion, without an independent verification pass. The user had to interrupt and explicitly demand verification of the pre-built sections; the resulting pass also surfaced a real risk (transitive dependency bumps) that the per-item flow had not re-validated against the whole suite.
issues.root_cause_guess: The 0.15.0 dummyindex-build skill says 'VERIFIES the result, and only then ticks the box' but gives no concrete verification procedure, so verification degraded to trusting the subagent's own report. Later skill versions stress independent verification — this session is the evidence for why.

issues.title: /dummyindex skill arg-routing can't handle the CLI's own `usage` verb — `/dummyindex usage chat` treated as a bogus path scope, resolved only via user round-trip
issues.evidence: [assistant:] "`/dummyindex` treats its arguments as a **scope path** to index. \"usage chat\" isn't a path, and it's not a recognized flag" ... [AskUserQuestion:] "`/dummyindex usage chat` doesn't map to a valid index scope (no `usage chat` path) or a dummyindex flag. What did you intend?" ... [tokens skill output:] "For history across all projects, mention `dummyindex usage daily | session | monthly | blocks`."
issues.what_happened: The user typed `/dummyindex usage chat` wanting token-usage stats. The SKILL.md instructs treating tokens after /dummyindex strictly as a literal scope path (or fail as 'ambiguous scope'), even though the CLI itself ships a `usage` subcommand family. Claude correctly avoided launching an expensive full ingest but had to probe the filesystem and ask the user a clarifying question before redirecting to /tokens. A dangerous near-miss: a less careful reading of the skill would have kicked off a full council re-index over the repo.
issues.root_cause_guess: SKILL.md scope-resolution rules predate / ignore the CLI's non-indexing verbs; the skill should recognize known CLI subcommands (usage, install, context ...) in args before path interpretation.

issues.title: No discoverable read-only status entry point — Claude guessed `dummyindex --status` and got 'error: unknown command'
issues.evidence:
build status [mcp-server]: 42/42 done
error: unknown command '--status'
Usage: dummyindex <command> [args]

Commands:
  install [--scope user|project] [--dir PATH] [--skill-only] ...
issues.what_happened: While disambiguating `/dummyindex usage chat`, Claude wanted a read-only overview of the index state and ran `dummyindex --status` (with `dummyindex context status` as fallback) — the flag doesn't exist and the CLI dumped its full usage text. Only `context build --proposal X --status` exists, which requires knowing a proposal slug. Single occurrence.
issues.root_cause_guess: There is no top-level `status`/`context status` verb summarizing index freshness/equipment/proposals, so the model improvises plausible-but-wrong flags. Error message also doesn't suggest the nearest valid command.

issues.title: equip apply summary miscounts and under-reports: 'wrote 6 item(s)' while listing only 4 writes; adopt + hook lines silently dropped from apply output
issues.evidence:
equip: stack=python frameworks=['FastAPI']
  write   python-implementer  ->  .claude/agents/python-implementer.md
  write   python-tester  ->  .claude/agents/python-tester.md
  write   backend-reviewer  ->  .claude/agents/backend-reviewer.md
  write   backend-verify  ->  .claude/skills/backend-verify/SKILL.md
equip: wrote 6 item(s), skipped 0, preserved 0 user-modified, kept 0 evolved
issues.what_happened: The dry-run plan showed 6 actions (4 writes + 1 registry adopt + 1 settings.json hook) but the apply output printed only the 4 file writes yet claimed 'wrote 6 item(s)' — the manifest-only adoption and the settings.json hook wiring were counted as 'written' but not echoed. Claude had to run a separate python inspection of equipment.json and settings.json to confirm what actually got wired (it had: DUMMYINDEX_EQUIP and DUMMYINDEX_AUTO_REFRESH both present, so wiring itself was correct).
issues.root_cause_guess: Apply-path logging only prints file-render actions while the counter includes all manifest entries (adopts, hooks); dry-run and apply renderers diverge.

issues.title: Docs-writing checklist item mapped to backend-reviewer; Claude overrode the CLI mapping with general-purpose
issues.evidence: build next ... #26 agent=backend-reviewer | Docs updated: `.context/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` (mount + `core...` ... [actual dispatch:] {"description": "Update docs for MCP feature", "subagent_type": "general-purpose", "prompt": "Update this backend's canonical docs to record the newly-built MCP server feature... This is DOC-WRITING (targeted additions), not code."}
issues.what_happened: The one item the matcher confidently bound to a tuned non-tester agent (#26, a documentation-update task) was bound to the review agent backend-reviewer — a critic persona, not a writer. Claude judged it a mis-fit and dispatched general-purpose instead. So in this session the CLI's mapping was overridden by the orchestrating model in BOTH directions (impl items: fallback->implementer; docs item: reviewer->general-purpose).
issues.root_cause_guess: Keyword 'Docs'/'reviewed' style matching binds doc tasks to the reviewer; v0.15.0 has no docs specialist (added later in v0.17.0), and the matcher has no notion of agent role (writer vs critic).

issues.title: Post-build close-the-loop is deterministic-only: new MCP feature gets rebuild --changed but no curated enrichment, left as a manual expensive --recouncil
issues.evidence: [AskUserQuestion option:] "Re-council the new MCP feature — Run the dummyindex council (`--recouncil`) on the just-built `core/mcp` area to enrich its `.context/features/` docs (spec/plan/concerns) beyond the deterministic `rebuild --changed` already done. Costs several subagent invocations." ... build check: [x] #28 `dummyindex context rebuild --changed` run ... context rebuild: 23 added, 3 modified, 5 removed
issues.what_happened: The build's final checklist step only runs `dummyindex context rebuild --changed` (deterministic backbone). The newly built core/mcp feature therefore has no curated spec/plan/concerns docs in .context/features/ unless the user separately pays for a full --recouncil — which was offered as an option at session end but never run. Also `.context/_enrich_plan.json` (an internal working artifact) appeared in git status as a tracked add.
issues.root_cause_guess: v0.15.0 predates the reconcile/reconcile-stamp loop (added v0.15.3+); the build skill's closing step was rebuild-only by design, leaving curated taxonomy stale for new features. The session is evidence that prompted the later commit-anchored reconcile work.

issues.title: CLI --check mutates checklist.md underneath the session, causing an Edit conflict
issues.evidence: Error: File has been modified since read, either by the user or by a linter. Read it again before attempting to write it. [while editing .context/proposals/mcp-server/checklist.md immediately after running `dummyindex context build --proposal mcp-server --check 7`]
issues.what_happened: Claude alternates between `dummyindex context build --check N` (CLI rewrites checklist.md to tick the box) and direct Edits to the same file (rewording items). Once, the CLI's mutation invalidated Claude's read-state and the Edit was rejected, forcing a re-read. One occurrence, recovered immediately, but it is an inherent shared-mutable-file hazard of the design (the index-based --check addressing also silently depends on item order that Claude's own edits could shift).
issues.root_cause_guess: Build state lives only in the human/agent-edited markdown checklist; the CLI offers no advisory output mode or stable item IDs, so concurrent CLI and editor mutation of the same file races.

summary: A 13-hour session (2026-06-07/08, dummyindex 0.15.0) running the full plan -> build -> equip cycle to port a TypeScript brand-knowledge MCP server into a FastAPI backend; all 42 checklist items completed with green gates, and propose/build/equip/rebuild CLI verbs all executed without crashes. The dominant struggle was the equip/agent-routing layer: the build matcher mapped every implementation item to 'general-purpose (fallback)' even after equipping (python-implementer was never selected once), equip adopted a 'Frontend Developer' registry specialist for a backend-only security-heavy proposal, and the repo started the build unequipped with no warning — Claude manually overrode the CLI's agent mapping throughout. No plugin discover/install activity exists in this transcript (0.15.0 predates the plugin manager), so the closest plugin-wiring evidence is the wrong specialist adoption plus several CLI-UX gaps (unknown `--status` command, `/dummyindex usage chat` arg-routing ambiguity, miscounted equip summary) and one user correction demanding verification of already-built items.

issues.title: Plan ignored installed Supabase MCP plugin; user had to manually wire it in
issues.evidence: USER: "in the plan also add points to update supabase using the supbase mcp" ... ASSISTANT: "Good call — the Supabase MCP is exactly the missing schema-apply mechanism from Task 0 (its `apply_migration` gives us the versioned migration tool the repo lacks, and `get_advisors` directly checks the RLS/index posture the council flagged)."
issues.what_happened: /dummyindex-plan produced a 16-task migration proposal whose Task 0 was a hard GATE: 'No committed bootstrap.sql or migration tool exists in the repo ... Decide: Alembic / Atlas / migra' — even though the Supabase MCP server (apply_migration, create_branch, get_advisors, list_migrations) was already installed and available in the session and trivially covered that exact capability. The plan only got plugin-annotated after the user explicitly asked; the assistant then admitted the MCP 'is exactly the missing schema-apply mechanism'. The user had to intervene a second time ('does it make sense to use a migration manager?' / 'supabase cli makes sense') to settle the mechanism. No plugin/MCP inventory or `equip discover` was ever run.
issues.root_cause_guess: The repo's dummyindex-plan skill was the v0.15.0 vintage, which has no plugin-awareness step: no inventory of installed plugins/MCP servers, no '— via <tool>' task annotation, no auto `equip discover` for uncovered capabilities. Planning is grounded only in .context/ symbols, so capabilities provided by installed plugins are invisible and get re-litigated as open gates.

issues.title: Stale 0.15.0 dummyindex deployed in repo; version check only detects CLI/skill mismatch, not staleness
issues.evidence: > **Installed from dummyindex `0.15.0`.** Run `dummyindex --version` to confirm the CLI matches. If they diverge, re-run `dummyindex install --scope user`. ... [tool_result] 0.15.0 | --- | context propose: /mnt/windows-ssd/Projects/opensession/BOS-Mono/backend/.context/proposals/brand-centric-migration (4 files)
issues.what_happened: The repo's .claude/skills/dummyindex-plan and the CLI (installed inside the project's own .venv — `./.venv/lib/python3.13/site-packages/dummyindex/cli/bootstrap.py` shows up in repo file searches) were both pinned at 0.15.0 while upstream dummyindex had moved several minor versions ahead (equip, plugin manager, critique panel, wave checklists, reconcile loop). The skill's only guard is 'confirm the CLI matches' — both layers were equally stale so the check passed and the session ran the old behavior: flat checklist (serial build), no critique panel, no equip/equipment.json, closing step is `dummyindex context rebuild --changed` rather than the reconcile procedure.
issues.root_cause_guess: Per-repo skill copies + a per-project venv CLI install mean a frozen snapshot with no freshness signal; the version-match check can never detect 'both stale'. Needs an upstream-latest check / update nudge (what /dummyindex-update later became) and ideally not vendoring the CLI into the project venv.

issues.title: Proposal cites symbols.json grep line numbers as if they were source-file locations
issues.evidence: Grounding cmd output: "=== key symbols (path:line) === | 358: \"name\": \"_build_owner_filter\", | ... | 5870: \"name\": \"ContainerService\"," → plan.md: "6. **Remove containers.** Delete `app/core/workspaces/containers.py` (`ContainerService` :5870)" and summary: "`ContainerService` (`workspaces/containers.py:5870`, delete), `OwnerRef`/`owner_fks` (`resources/models.py:5272/5298`...)"
issues.what_happened: Following the skill instruction to 'cite reused symbols from .context/map/symbols.json', Claude grepped the monolithic symbols.json and harvested grep's line numbers (358, 956, 969, 5272, 5298, 5870, 6000 — offsets inside the JSON file) then wrote them into spec.md/plan.md as if they were source-file line numbers (containers.py is nowhere near 5870 lines). Build agents executing this checklist would chase bogus locations. Repeated across ~8 symbol citations in the written proposal.
issues.root_cause_guess: symbols.json is one giant JSON blob and the skill gives no retrieval verb (e.g. `dummyindex query symbol <name>` returning path:line), so the model improvises grep and confuses JSON offsets with source locations. Provide a symbol-lookup command or embed path:line next to each name in the docs the skill tells the model to read.

issues.title: SessionStart drift report ignored; session ended without rebuild/reconcile despite overlapping feature edits
issues.evidence: SessionStart hook: "## .context/ drift report\n\n4 source files across 4 features have been edited since the matching `.context/features/<id>/` docs were last touched. ... - **app-infra-tools** — app/main.py - **auth-enums** — app/config.py - **integrations-oauth** — app/config.py - **utils-config-fragment** — app/config.py"
issues.what_happened: The drift hook fired correctly at session start (exit 0, 1.7s) and flagged integrations-oauth among 4 drifted features. The session then spent hours rewriting integration-ownership semantics across .context/docs/* and committed, but never updated the drifted feature docs, never ran `dummyindex context rebuild --changed` or any reconcile, and deferred reindexing to Task 16 of a future code migration. The report is advisory-only ('If your current task overlaps any of these features, review and update...') and nothing later in the session re-surfaces it, so the drift persisted across the whole session and the new target-state docs now also diverge from `.context/features/*` (still INFERRED with containers).
issues.root_cause_guess: Drift report is a one-shot SessionStart suggestion with no in-session re-check or Stop-time gate (the auto-council drift gate was built only after this session, 2026-06-11); easy for a long session to scroll it out of context.

issues.title: Unrelated WIP work committed onto dummyindex's leftover chore/dummyindex-context-index branch in a submodule
issues.evidence: ASSISTANT: "I'll commit the documentation changes. First let me see the exact working-tree state (we're inside the `backend` submodule, on `chore/dummyindex-context-index` — not the default branch, so no new branch needed)." ... "[chore/dummyindex-context-index 1b158de] docs: WIP brand-centric domain model rewrite + DB design-review findings | 9 files changed, 790 insertions(+), 295 deletions(-)"
issues.what_happened: The backend (a git submodule of BOS-Mono) was still checked out on the branch dummyindex created for its index commit. The entire session — domain-model doc rewrite, council findings, the brand-centric-migration proposal — happened and was committed there; the assistant even treated 'not the default branch' as license to keep committing. The commit also swept up three pre-existing staged changes the assistant 'didn't author'. The index branch is now entangled with unrelated product-doc work inside a submodule, complicating merge of either.
issues.root_cause_guess: dummyindex's ingest/index flow creates a chore/dummyindex-context-index branch and leaves it checked out with no follow-up (merge, switch back, or warn), which is extra hazardous in submodules where branch state is easy to overlook.

issues.title: User asked for an 'llm council' — Claude hand-rolled an ad-hoc workflow instead of dummyindex's council/audit machinery
issues.evidence: USER: "i need you to invoke an llm coucil and debate the db setup" ... ASSISTANT: "The user explicitly asked for a multi-agent council — that's a clear opt-in to orchestration. I'll convene a 5-member expert council..." ... later USER: "can you log these concerns in correct places"
issues.what_happened: Despite the repo having a full dummyindex .context with council artifacts (features/*/council/01-dev-draft.md etc.) and dummyindex owning the 'council' vocabulary, the request was satisfied with a bespoke background Workflow (11 agents, ~1M tokens). It worked, but its findings landed only in chat/tmp output; the user then had to separately ask for them to be logged 'in correct places', which the assistant did by hand-editing BACKLOG.md/DECISIONS.md/SCHEMA.md. None of it flowed through dummyindex's council/concerns.md pipeline.
issues.root_cause_guess: The 0.15.0 install shipped no on-demand audit/council entry point in the repo (dummyindex-audit came later), and the council trigger phrases didn't route anywhere, so 'council' requests bypass dummyindex entirely and results must be manually folded into .context.

issues.title: Curated .context frontmatter owner silently changed @karim → @ahmed by something other than the session
issues.evidence: git diff --cached .context/conventions/folder-organization.md: "-owner: '@karim' | +owner: '@ahmed'" ... ASSISTANT: "plus three I didn't author — an `owner: @karim→@ahmed` rename in `folder-organization.md`, a generated `bos-domain-model-graph.html`, and the pre-existing `mcp-design-tokens-from-db/spec.md` edit"
issues.what_happened: When committing, the assistant found already-staged modifications it had not made, including an owner-frontmatter rename on a curated dummyindex conventions file (whose sibling docs all say owner: '@karim'). It flagged them but committed them anyway as part of the WIP checkpoint, so the unexplained metadata change is now in history. Single occurrence.
issues.root_cause_guess: Most plausibly a prior dummyindex rebuild/ingest run stamping the frontmatter owner from the current git user, clobbering the curated value (confidence low — could also be a manual edit in an earlier session); worth checking whether deterministic rebuild rewrites owner fields on curated taxonomy files.

summary: Long session (2026-06-09) in the BOS-Mono/backend submodule: the user rewrote the curated .context/docs to a brand-centric domain model, ran an ad-hoc 5-expert DB council, committed, then invoked /dummyindex-plan to scaffold the brand-centric-migration proposal. dummyindex's deterministic plumbing worked — the SessionStart drift hook ran cleanly and `dummyindex context propose` succeeded with sensible related-features — but the deployment was a stale 0.15.0 snapshot, so all newer safeguards (plugin annotation, equip/discover, critique panel, wave checklists, reconcile) were absent: the flagship struggle is that the plan declared 'no migration tooling exists' as a blocking gate while the installed Supabase MCP already covered it, and the user had to wire that plugin in manually. Secondary problems: bogus line-number citations harvested from symbols.json, the ignored drift report with no reconcile at session end, and unrelated work committed onto dummyindex's leftover chore/dummyindex-context-index branch. No CLI crashes, tracebacks, or usage errors occurred.

issues.title: equip install cannot resolve a marketplace Claude Code already knows (user's own plugin uninstallable via equip)
issues.evidence: error: canvas-to-code@canvas-to-code-marketplace not found in known marketplaces ... === try alt marketplace id forms equip might accept === --- @canvas-to-code --- error: canvas-to-code@canvas-to-code not found in known marketplaces --- @opensesh/canvas-to-code --- error: canvas-to-code@opensesh/canvas-to-code not found in known marketplaces
issues.what_happened: User invoked /dummyindex-equip with their own plugin repo URL. `dummyindex context equip install canvas-to-code@canvas-to-code-marketplace` failed 4+ times across the session (first attempt and a later retry with three alternate id forms), even though the marketplace was registered in ~/.claude/plugins/known_marketplaces.json AND in the project's committed .claude/settings.json extraKnownMarketplaces, and the clone existed at ~/.claude/plugins/marketplaces/canvas-to-code-marketplace/. Claude eventually hand-edited .context/equipment.json to fake the install record. User later said 'canvas-to-code is my plugin', expecting it to just work.
issues.root_cause_guess: equip's installer resolves <plugin>@<marketplace> only against its own seeded marketplace list / catalog cache (canvas-to-code was confirmed absent from plugin-catalog-cache.json); it never reads Claude Code's native known_marketplaces.json, installed_plugins.json, or the repo's settings.json extraKnownMarketplaces, and offers no verb to register an arbitrary GitHub marketplace.

issues.title: equip discover cannot take a GitHub repo URL and returns irrelevant results for the query
issues.evidence:
=== discover with repo URL ===
equip discover: no matching plugins found.

=== discover by name ===
equip discover (dry-run — nothing written):
  native design@knowledge-work-plugins  covers: security, performance, search ...
  native wix@claude-plugins-official  covers: frontend ...
  native aws-serverless@claude-plugins-official  covers: frontend, test ...
issues.what_happened: When install failed, Claude tried `equip discover "https://github.com/opensesh/canvas-to-code"` (no matches) and `equip discover "canvas-to-code design system"` (returned a wall of unrelated official-catalog plugins like wix, aws-serverless, clickhouse-best-practices). Discover could neither resolve a direct repo URL nor surface the low-profile org repo via GitHub search, forcing Claude off the documented path entirely. A related fragility surfaced when installing impeccable: 'warning: skipping az9713/impeccable-tutorial: marketplace name impeccable already registered by a higher-priority source' — name-based live-GitHub-search resolution risks collisions.
issues.root_cause_guess: discover only matches its catalog seeds + popularity-ranked GitHub search; there is no direct add-by-URL/add-by-repo path, so first-party/private/low-star plugins are invisible, and keyword matching against catalog descriptions yields noise.

issues.title: Plugin wiring declared successful but plugin was never actually loaded — no install verification
issues.evidence: Diagnosed. It's **declared but not actually loaded** ... ❌ **It's not in `~/.claude/plugins/installed_plugins.json`** — that's the registry the *running app* consults, and it has no canvas-to-code entry. So no commands. ... ⚠️ The local clone is also **stale**: `lastUpdated 2026-06-04`, but the repo was pushed `2026-06-06` ... it's **missing `update.md`** that exists upstream.
issues.what_happened: After wiring canvas-to-code into settings.json (enabledPlugins + extraKnownMarketplaces), Claude declared 'No changes needed — it's already done ... your project-scope install is in place.' The user then found no commands available. Diagnosis showed the settings entry alone never registers the plugin in installed_plugins.json, the cached marketplace clone was stale/missing files, and loading only happens at session start. The user ultimately had to drive the native /plugin UI themselves to actually install it.
issues.root_cause_guess: equip's 'native' mechanism = writing settings.json entries only; it has no post-install verification step (check installed_plugins.json / clone freshness / command availability), so success is claimed when the plugin is merely declared, not loaded.

issues.title: Native plugin fetch failed with SSH Permission denied, surfacing only later in the /plugin UI
issues.evidence:
canvas-to-code @ canvas-to-code-marketplace (project)
Failed to download/cache plugin canvas-to-code: Failed to clone repository: Cloning into '/home/ahmed/.claude/plugins/cache/temp_github_1781107125738_k88w8h'...
git@github.com: Permission denied (publickey).
fatal: Could not read from remote repository.
issues.what_happened: The plugin equip had wired could not be downloaded: Claude Code's plugin cloner used the SSH URL (git@github.com:) while the user's machine is HTTPS-only (gh token, no SSH key). The user pasted the /plugin Errors screen; Claude admitted its earlier 'just restart' diagnosis was wrong and applied a global `git config url."https://github.com/".insteadOf "git@github.com:"` workaround, then proved a probe clone worked. This blocked BOTH canvas-to-code and impeccable until the workaround.
issues.root_cause_guess: Claude Code's plugin fetcher hard-codes SSH clone URLs; dummyindex's wiring path neither preflights clone transport nor warns about it, so an environment-level auth mismatch silently breaks every plugin it wires.

issues.title: equip install leaves no .context grounding/orchestration for installed plugins — user complained three times
issues.evidence: USER: "i dont see any changes in .context on how dummyindex should use canvas to code" ... USER: "i dont see any changes in the .claude or .context folder locally" ... USER: "i dont see any cahnges in .context or .claude, dummyindex is supposed to be the orchasetrator whuch runs them as and when it sees fit"
issues.what_happened: Three separate user pushbacks in one session. The user's mental model is that equipping a plugin should teach dummyindex (via .context/) when/how to run it. The tool only wrote settings.json entries; nothing in .context referenced the plugins. Claude acknowledged 'that's a real gap' each time and hand-authored .context/playbooks/canvas-to-code.md, impeccable.md, design-workflow.md plus a HOW_TO_USE.md edit — all improvised, none produced by the tool.
issues.root_cause_guess: equip's plugin-install verb has no step that writes plugin usage grounding (playbook/HOW_TO_USE entry/routing doc) into .context/, so the 'orchestrator runs them as it sees fit' promise has no artifact backing it; the skill docs don't instruct this either.

issues.title: Installed plugins are not routable by /dummyindex-build; equipment.json plugin schema half-baked, fixed by hand-edits
issues.evidence: Right now the plugin rows carry `subagent_type: null`, so build can't pick them. ... [Edit equipment.json] "kind": "agent", "name": "canvas-to-code@canvas-to-code-marketplace", "path": ".claude/settings.json", ... "subagent_type": null, "version": null, "origin_hash": null ... equip status: pristine impeccable@impeccable  (v-)
issues.what_happened: When the user asked 'so are the new plugins ready to be used by dummyindex', the honest answer was no for /dummyindex-build: equip records a plugin as a single kind:'agent' row whose path is settings.local.json, with subagent_type/version/origin_hash all null (status renders '(v-)'). Claude had to hand-edit equipment.json twice — once to fabricate the canvas-to-code entry equip refused to create, and again to set subagent_type to canvas-to-code:canvas-to-code-pm so build could dispatch it. impeccable was left subagent_type=None (not build-routable). Plugin sub-agents (7 canvas-to-code agents) aren't mapped as specialists at all.
issues.root_cause_guess: equipment.json schema v3 models plugins as a degenerate 'agent' item without resolving their actual agents/commands into routable specialists; there is no equip verb to map a plugin's agents to subagent_types, so build's specialist routing can never reach plugin capabilities without manual JSON surgery.

issues.title: Deterministic backbone over-segmented 1022 files into 734 communities; documented council would cost ~1000+ agent dispatches
issues.evidence: The deterministic backbone shattered this 1022-file repo into **734 Leiden communities** (650 trivial, 84 "non-trivial" ...). Yet the actual product structure is only **6 feature slices** ... Following the documented council *strictly* means ~84 per-feature councils + **650 individual trivial-consolidation agent passes** ... ≈ **1000+ agent dispatches** — wildly out of proportion with the "standard ≈ $6–10" the onboarding implied (that estimate assumed N≈14, not 734).
issues.what_happened: On this Next.js frontend the graph-community detection produced 734 features for what is really a ~13-feature repo. The documented council pipeline scaled with raw community count, contradicting the cost estimate shown at onboarding. Claude had to stop and ask; the user instructed 'be smart about ti first do a llm council pass and reduce the segments before moving into actual enrichment', and Claude improvised an off-script taxonomy collapse (ad-hoc bucketing scripts, an architect agent, scaffold/remove surgery).
issues.root_cause_guess: Leiden community detection with no post-clustering consolidation pass is unsuited to feature-sliced frontend repos (many small import islands); the council docs and cost model assume N≈14 features and provide no built-in 'reduce segments first' stage.

issues.title: No batch verbs for taxonomy surgery — 734 sequential features-remove calls took a multi-minute background loop
issues.evidence:
=== features-merge ===
error: --from <id> and --into <id> are both required ...
=== features-remove ===
error: --feature <id> is required
... "removing 734 community features..." [background loop] ... "all communities removed at iter 46" (polling at 6s intervals)
issues.what_happened: Every taxonomy verb (features-merge, features-rename, assign-files, scaffold-feature, features-remove, mark-enriched) accepts exactly one item per invocation, and each call regenerates INDEX.md + graph.json + graph.html. Collapsing 734 communities required a Python subprocess loop run in the background, with Claude polling for ~5 minutes across two wait loops before it finished. mark-enriched likewise required a 13-iteration shell loop.
issues.root_cause_guess: CLI verbs are single-target with per-call full index regeneration (O(n^2) overall); no --from-json/batch mode or deferred-reindex flag exists for bulk restructuring, which the over-segmentation issue makes routine.

issues.title: reconcile-stamp REFUSED because of dummyindex's own installed files; --force warns they will be re-reported forever
issues.evidence:
context reconcile-stamp: REFUSED — un-reconciled work remains (advancing the anchor would silently forget it):
  unassigned new files: .claude/CLAUDE.md, .claude/commands/tokens.md, .claude/skills/dummyindex-audit/SKILL.md, ... .claude/skills/dummyindex-equip/templates/db-specialist-agent.md.tmpl ... [then after --force] WARNING: --force advanced the anchor past un-reconciled work, but did NOT resolve it ... It WILL be re-reported next reconcile until you place/enrich it or stop tracking it
issues.what_happened: At the end of the council run, reconcile-stamp refused to advance the anchor because ~50 'unassigned new files' existed — virtually all of them dummyindex's OWN install artifacts (.claude/CLAUDE.md, the dummyindex* skill family, equip .tmpl templates, .dummyindex_version). Claude had to use --force, and the tool itself warned the same files will keep being re-reported on every future reconcile. Claude waved it off as 'expected and harmless'.
issues.root_cause_guess: The reconcile new-file tracker has no exclusion list for dummyindex-managed paths (.claude/skills/dummyindex*, managed CLAUDE.md, equip templates), so the tool's installer output permanently poisons its own drift/reconcile gate.

issues.title: Subcommands reject --help and there is no status verb; usage only discoverable by provoking errors
issues.evidence: error: unknown command '--status' ... error: unknown argument(s) for `conventions-write`: ['--help'] ... error: unknown argument(s) for `equip discover`: ['--help'] ... error: unknown argument(s) for `build`: ['--help']
issues.what_happened: At least 4 distinct occurrences: `dummyindex --status` (no such command), and `--help` rejected by conventions-write, equip discover, and build. Each time Claude had to run the verb bare to harvest the 'required args' error, or grep the top-level help, burning turns. The orchestrator skill repeatedly guesses flags the CLI doesn't have.
issues.root_cause_guess: Hand-rolled argparse-style dispatcher without per-subcommand --help support or a status command; SKILL.md doesn't embed full per-verb usage, so the model probes interactively.

issues.title: Council subagent reported the dummyindex CLI 'not installed in this environment' and bypassed atomic placement
issues.evidence: The `dummyindex context conventions-write` CLI is not installed in this environment; the file was written directly to `/mnt/windows-ssd/Projects/opensession/BOS-Mono/frontend/.context/conventions/data-access.md`.
issues.what_happened: One of four parallel conventions-author subagents could not find the dummyindex binary on its PATH (the other three succeeded), so it wrote the conventions file directly instead of via the CLI's atomic conventions-write. The orchestrator noticed and defensively re-placed the file through the CLI afterwards. Silent improvised fallback could have left malformed/unsectioned conventions docs.
issues.root_cause_guess: Subagent shells don't always inherit the uv-tool PATH where dummyindex is installed; council persona prompts mandate CLI use but include no PATH guard or absolute-binary fallback, so dispatched agents quietly route around the tool.

issues.title: Throwaway scaffold-feature test feature leaked back into the index after non-force removal failed
issues.evidence: error: feature '_zztest' still owns 2 file(s) that exist on disk (e.g. shared/lib/api/fetc... [later] The `_zztest` throwaway leaked back in (my earlier non-force cleanup failed). Let me force-remove it to land on exactly 13 features
issues.what_happened: Claude scaffolded a `_zztest` feature to learn the verb's behavior. Plain features-remove refused because the feature 'owns files that exist on disk' — true of essentially every real feature — so the cleanup silently failed and `_zztest` resurfaced in features/INDEX.json after the big restructure, requiring a second --force removal. The index briefly read 14 features instead of 13.
issues.root_cause_guess: features-remove's no-force guard (refuse if owned files exist on disk) is inverted for the common case; failure exit didn't make the earlier compound command abort, so stale state survived into refresh-indexes.

issues.title: Equip skill ships duplicate .tmpl template copies into the repo that the renderer never reads
issues.evidence: Good news — the dry-run rendered fine, so the CLI bundles its own templates (the `.claude/skills/.../templates/*.tmpl` I deleted were distribution copies, not what the renderer reads).
issues.what_happened: The user asked to delete the 9 .claude/skills/dummyindex-equip/templates/*.md.tmpl files. Claude warned this would disable /dummyindex-equip until reinstall — then discovered equip rendered fine anyway because the CLI bundles its own templates. The repo copies are dead weight that (a) misled the assistant about blast radius, and (b) were among the unassigned files that blocked reconcile-stamp.
issues.root_cause_guess: dummyindex install copies equip templates into every repo's .claude/skills tree as documentation/distribution artifacts even though rendering uses package-internal templates; nothing marks them as inert.

issues.title: Initial install built .context/ but never persisted onboarding config, forcing onboarding on the next run
issues.evidence: No `config.json` yet — onboarding never persisted, even though `.context/` exists. ... Onboarding hasn't run, so I need your preferences before the council proceeds. One note on scale: there are **734 raw graph-community detections** right now
issues.what_happened: When /dummyindex ran, .context/ already existed (meta.json created the same day at install time, 1022 files indexed at HEAD) but features were all raw community-N stubs with null summaries and there was no .context/config.json — the install-time project init built the deterministic backbone without running or persisting onboarding, leaving the repo in a half-initialized state the skill had to detect and repair. The onboarding cost estimate it then showed ('standard ≈ $6–10') was wrong for the actual 734-community state.
issues.root_cause_guess: `dummyindex install` project-init builds the backbone but defers onboarding to the skill's first interactive run without flagging the half-built state; the cost copy in onboarding assumes a typical N≈14 feature count rather than reading the actual index.

summary: Session (~2026-06-10, dummyindex 0.19.2) ran a full /dummyindex council build of a 1022-file Next.js frontend submodule, committed .context/, ran /dummyindex-equip (toolkit generation worked cleanly), then tried to wire two plugins — the user's own opensesh/canvas-to-code and pbakaus/impeccable. The context-build side mostly succeeded but only after heavy improvisation: the backbone over-segmented into 734 communities, the user had to redirect ("reduce the segments first"), and single-target CLI verbs plus a reconcile gate poisoned by dummyindex's own installed files made finalization painful. The plugin-manager side was the clear failure mode the owner suspected: equip install could not resolve a marketplace Claude Code already knew, discover couldn't take a repo URL and returned irrelevant results, wiring was declared done while the plugin never loaded (SSH clone failure + no installed_plugins.json registration), nothing was written into .context/ about how to use the plugins (three explicit user complaints), and making them build-routable required hand-editing equipment.json. Claude papered over most gaps with manual workarounds (playbooks, JSON edits, git insteadOf), which kept the user moving but means the tool's documented paths failed at nearly every plugin step.

issues.title: Reality-check resolves bare-basename citations against the wrong file, producing 21 false contradictions
issues.evidence:
=== assets-resources
- ``__init__.py:98`` (plan.md) — file has 0 line(s), claim cites line 98
- ``__init__.py:574`` (plan.md) — file has 0 line(s), claim cites line 574 ... [final report:] "Honesty note: the initial run flagged 21 \"contradictions\" — all were bare-basename citations (`__init__.py:152`) the checker resolved against the wrong file, plus one stdlib-API claim. Fixed by fully-qualifying citations (verified against source, none factually wrong)"
issues.what_happened: `dummyindex context reality-check --demote` flagged 22 'contradicted' claims across 8 of 11 features. Every single one was a false positive: the checker resolved citations like `__init__.py:152` against some other (often empty) file with the same basename instead of the file named in surrounding prose. Remediation cost: a citation-hygiene subagent (79 tool uses, 84.8k tokens, 6.5 min), a second micro-agent for one straggler (`router.py:128`), and three reality-check re-runs before reaching 172/172 verified. Recurs in every feature with files named __init__.py/router.py/enums.py/schemas.py — i.e., any Python repo.
issues.root_cause_guess: The claim extractor stores only the basename as `subject` and resolves it via a naive (possibly first-match or wrong-dir) lookup in map/symbols.json instead of disambiguating via the feature's `files` list or the surrounding prose; packages full of identically-named __init__.py/router.py files break it.

issues.title: reality-check --demote has no inverse: restoring confidence after false positives required hand-editing feature.json and INDEX.json
issues.evidence: "All 11 features now fully verified (193 claims checked, 0 contradicted). Restoring `INFERRED` on the 8 features the earlier false positives demoted" ... python script: "# Restore INFERRED only where the latest reality-check is clean ... fj['confidence'] = 'INFERRED' ... # Reconcile INDEX.json"
issues.what_happened: The false-positive contradictions (see previous issue) caused `--demote` to flip 8 features from INFERRED to AMBIGUOUS. After the citations were fixed and re-checks came back clean, the CLI offered no 'promote'/'restore' verb, so the orchestrator wrote a raw python loop mutating each feature.json AND .context/features/INDEX.json by hand to undo the demotion — exactly the kind of manual JSON surgery the atomic-verb design (features-rename, council-log) is supposed to prevent.
issues.root_cause_guess: Asymmetric CLI surface: demotion is automated but promotion/recovery is not; a clean re-run of reality-check should auto-restore (or a `--restore`/promote verb should exist).

issues.title: Flow auto-detection is mostly noise: 60 of 75 detected flows discarded (enum-class roots, private helpers, BFS over-reach)
issues.evidence: "workspace-org-core: kept 0, discarded 6. ... **flow-002** (`OrganizationMemberRole`): enum class entry point, 324 steps across 50 files — same pattern" ... "**flow-038** (`ExecStatus`): Enum class entry point (not callable); trace exploded to 100+ steps across 37 unrelated files (breadth mis-" ... "**flow-066** (`build_version_snapshot`): Misdetected breadth — the trace is a reverse call-graph showing which asset/doc service methods call this utility, not a forward execution sequence." Final report: "Flows: 15 kept, 60 discarded (enum-class roots, `_`-private helpers, BFS over-reach)"
issues.what_happened: The deterministic flow extractor treated enum classes and `_`-prefixed private helpers as entry points and produced traces of 100-324 steps across 37-50 unrelated files; at least one trace was a reverse call-graph rather than a forward execution path. Multiple features (ai-chat-streaming, workspace-org-core, app-infra-tools) discarded ALL 6 of their flows. 11 flow-refinement subagent dispatches were spent largely refuting deterministic noise (80% discard rate).
issues.root_cause_guess: Entry-point heuristic doesn't filter non-callable symbols (Enum classes) or private names, and the trace builder does unbounded BFS over the call graph (sometimes following reverse edges) with no breadth/depth cutoff.

issues.title: Entry-point detection misses real FastAPI HTTP handlers, leaving 5 of 11 features with zero flows
issues.evidence: "Caveat: 5 of 11 features kept **0** flows because AST entry-point detection missed the real HTTP handlers (`chat()`, `accept_invitation()`, …) — behaviour for those lives in their `spec.md`/`plan.md`, not `flows/`"
issues.what_happened: The flip side of the flow-noise problem: while inventing flows from enums, the AST extractor failed to detect the decorated FastAPI route handlers that are the actual entry points of this backend, so nearly half the features shipped with no flow narratives at all — a permanent coverage gap in the committed index acknowledged in the final report.
issues.root_cause_guess: Entry-point detection doesn't recognize framework decorators (@router.get/post etc.) as roots; it likely keys off naming/call-graph in-degree only, which misses decorator-registered handlers.

issues.title: Repo's canonical docs in .context/docs/ self-exclude from the source-docs catalog, even when passed explicitly via --docs
issues.evidence: [after `dummyindex ingest ... --docs .../backend/.context/docs --docs .../BOS-Mono/.context/docs`] "backend canon docs: 0" ... Final report: "Note: the backend's own `.context/docs/` canon self-excludes from the catalog — I compensated by injecting explicit canon pointers (CONVENTIONS/DECISIONS/SCHEMA) into every dispatch, which worked"
issues.what_happened: The team keeps its canon (ARCHITECTURE.md, CONVENTIONS.md, DECISIONS.md, SCHEMA.md, BACKLOG.md) in backend/.context/docs/. First ingest auto-discovered only 2 docs (CLAUDE.md, README.md). Re-ingest with the directory passed explicitly via --docs still catalogued 0 backend canon docs (the mono-root's 246 docs were catalogued fine) because the scanner blanket-excludes .context/ as its own output dir. The orchestrator had to hand-inject canon pointers into all ~56 subagent prompts — grounding that future sessions navigating only via the index will NOT get.
issues.root_cause_guess: The ingest doc scanner excludes the .context/ directory wholesale (to avoid indexing its own output) and the exclusion is applied even to explicitly user-supplied --docs roots that happen to live under a .context/ path.

issues.title: Reality-check misparses stdlib/external-API references as repo-symbol claims; docs had to be reworded to dodge the parser
issues.evidence: "- ``_LlmClient._ensure_initialised()` uses `os.environ.setdefault`` (plan.md) — symbol object not found in map/symbols.json" ... orchestrator's fix instruction: "rephrase to avoid the `` `X` uses `Y` `` backtick pattern for non-repo symbols — e.g. \"`_LlmClient._ensure_initialised()` seeds provider keys via the stdlib os.environ.setdefault call\" (no backticks around the stdlib reference). Meaning must not change."
issues.what_happened: A factually correct doc sentence citing the stdlib (`os.environ.setdefault`) was extracted as a repo-symbol relationship claim and marked contradicted because the symbol isn't in map/symbols.json. The sanctioned fix was to deliberately contort the doc's markdown formatting (drop backticks) so the checker's pattern wouldn't fire — degrading docs to satisfy the tool. The stage-4 brief even started warning authors that 'bare names break' the checker.
issues.root_cause_guess: Claim extraction is a backtick-pattern regex with no allowlist/check for non-repo (stdlib/third-party) symbols before classifying a claim as verifiable-against-symbols.json.

issues.title: `dummyindex ingest --help` fails with an error that names the wrong subcommand
issues.evidence:
$ dummyindex ingest --help
error: unknown argument(s) for `init`: ['--help']
issues.what_happened: While trying to confirm the --docs flag semantics mid-run, the orchestrator asked for subcommand help and got an argparse-style error instead — and the error message refers to `init` (the internal alias target) rather than `ingest` (what was typed). It had to fall back to the top-level `dummyindex --help` wall of text.
issues.root_cause_guess: Hand-rolled arg parsing without per-subcommand --help support; the ingest→`context init` aliasing leaks the internal command name into the error message.

issues.title: Graph partitioner leaks a parser-artifact rationale node into its own single-node community (spurious feature)
issues.evidence: "**Outcome: C — keep as standalone with parser-artifact note. Builder-bug leak recorded.** Evidence: `config_rationale_169` is a `file_type: rationale` node (the `_split_origins` docstring at `app/config.py` L169). The graph partitioner placed it alone into community 14 while every real `config.py` symbol ... landed in communities 1 and 4 ... The node has no edges, no call sites, and no entry points: it was never a real feature member. The `_is_parser_artifact` filter should have dropped `file_type: rationale` nodes before community assignment; one leaked through."
issues.what_happened: Leiden community detection produced a junk feature (`utils-config-fragment`) consisting of a single orphaned docstring-rationale node. It survived the trivial filter into the council, costing a dedicated architect consolidation dispatch (~36k subagent tokens) just to diagnose it as a builder bug, and ends up as a permanent parser-artifact stub feature in the committed index.
issues.root_cause_guess: The `_is_parser_artifact` pre-filter runs incompletely or after edge construction; `file_type: rationale` nodes with zero edges aren't dropped before community assignment, so an isolated node forms its own community.

issues.title: Structural review flags splits the tooling cannot apply — oversized mixed features ship in the final index
issues.evidence: "merges: 0 | splits flagged: 7" ... dispatch brief: "splits are surfaced as flags only — they can't be applied automatically; note them" ... final report: "**7 split flags** the structural review couldn't auto-apply (e.g. `workspace-org-core` spans 4 subdomains) — in `.context/features/_structural-plan.json` for human review"
issues.what_happened: The architect's structural review found 7 communities spanning multiple distinct subdomains (workspace-org-core alone is 210 members / 26 files across 4 subdomains), but the CLI offers `features-rename` and `features-merge` with no `features-split` verb, so all 7 were deferred as flags. The committed index permanently carries known-overgrouped features whose docs blend unrelated domains.
issues.root_cause_guess: Missing CLI verb: splitting requires re-partitioning members/files/flows across two feature folders plus JSON ref updates, which was never implemented; the skill papers over the gap by declaring splits 'flags only'.

issues.title: Ephemeral _enrich_plan.json left in .context/ and not gitignored — commit friction and user confusion
issues.evidence: USER: ".context/_enrich_plan.json why wasnt this committed?" ... .context/.gitignore generated by dummyindex covers only: "cache/" and "_doc_backups/" ... assistant: "If you'd rather the enrich plan never reappear as untracked noise, I can add it to `.context/.gitignore` — though dummyindex regenerates that file, so the entry might get overwritten"
issues.what_happened: `dummyindex context enrich-plan` writes a 384KB scratch work-queue into .context/ but the generated .context/.gitignore doesn't cover it. `git add .context` staged it with everything else; the assistant judged it scratch and unstaged it, leaving it as untracked clutter that prompted a user question and a two-turn explanation. Worse, the obvious fix (add it to .gitignore) is unsafe because dummyindex regenerates that .gitignore and may clobber manual entries.
issues.root_cause_guess: enrich-plan's output path was added after the .gitignore template was written; ephemeral artifacts aren't consistently namespaced (e.g. under cache/) or listed in the generated ignore file, and the generated .gitignore has no merge/patch seam for user entries.

issues.title: feature.json data contract mismatches the skill's documented trivial-filter fields; flow_count never populated
issues.evidence:
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 8, in <module>
    print(f"{fid:24s} members={mc:4} files={fc:3} entry_points={ep:3} flows={fl:3} conf={d.get('confidence')}")
TypeError: unsupported format string passed to NoneType.__format__ ... [final state check] "ai-chat-streaming        conf=INFERRED  flows=None" (all 14 features show flows=None despite 15 kept flow narratives on disk)
issues.what_happened: Following 18-filter-trivial.md (which keys the filter on member_count/entry-point/flow counts), the orchestrator's first stats pass over feature.json crashed because expected count fields were None; it had to rewrite the script schema-tolerantly (falling back to len(members)/len(files)). `flow_count` remained None in every feature.json through the entire run — even after flow refinement kept 15 flows and refresh-indexes ran — so the field documented as a filter input is never maintained.
issues.root_cause_guess: The skill's council docs describe a feature.json schema (count fields) that the deterministic builder doesn't fully populate or update; flow refinement/refresh-indexes don't write flow_count back.

issues.title: Onboarding runs after the first full ingest, so external doc roots require a second full re-ingest
issues.evidence: [first ingest] "context init: wrote 218 files ... files: 151" → [source-docs after it] "total docs catalogued: 2\n- CLAUDE.md ... - README.md" → [after onboarding answers] "dummyindex ingest /mnt/.../backend --docs .../backend/.context/docs --docs .../BOS-Mono/.context/docs" → "AST extraction: 151/151 files (100%) | context init: wrote 218 files"
issues.what_happened: The skill's pipeline ingests first, then asks the onboarding questions — including 'any prose-doc roots outside this repo?'. The user's answer (mono-root docs) was persisted to config.json via `context onboard`, but applying it meant re-running the entire ingest with explicit --docs flags (full 151-file AST re-extraction and 218-file rewrite). ingest apparently doesn't read external_docs from the just-written config.json either, since the flags had to be passed manually.
issues.root_cause_guess: Phase ordering in the skill (ingest before onboarding) plus ingest not consuming .context/config.json's external_docs; doc cataloguing isn't an incremental step separable from the full ingest.

issues.title: Resumption state (per-feature source hashes) must be hand-rolled in python; orchestrator unsure of its own file-list schema
issues.evidence:
"Reconciled. Writing the per-feature source hashes (resumption contract per `19-resume.md`), then a final state check:" ... python3 - <<'EOF' ... files = d.get('files') or []
    # files may be list of dicts or strings
    paths = []
issues.what_happened: The skill's resumption contract (19-resume.md) requires per-feature source hashes so re-runs can skip unchanged features, but no CLI verb writes them; the orchestrator implemented hashlib loops over feature.json by hand, including a defensive comment betraying uncertainty about whether `files` holds dicts or strings. Hand-written stamps risk diverging from whatever format the resume logic expects.
issues.root_cause_guess: Missing CLI verb (e.g. `context stamp-sources`) for the resumption contract, combined with an underspecified feature.json files schema in the skill docs.

summary: The session was a first-time `/dummyindex .` full ingest + council run (dummyindex v0.15.0) on the BOS-Mono `backend` FastAPI submodule, ending with committing 214 .context/.claude files on a branch. The pipeline did complete — 14 council-reviewed features, 172/172 claims eventually verified — but only after Claude papered over substantial deterministic-tooling defects: a reality-checker that produced 21/22 false contradictions and demoted 8 features with no restore path, flow extraction that was 80% noise while missing the real HTTP handlers, the repo's canonical docs being silently un-cataloguable because they live under .context/docs/, plus CLI help/gitignore/schema rough edges that forced repeated manual JSON surgery and prompt-level workarounds. Notably, this session contains NO plugin discover/install/wire activity (it predates the equip plugin-manager), so it provides no direct evidence on the plugin-wiring struggles; the issues here are all context-build, council, and CLI-UX. User frustration was mild — one confused question about why _enrich_plan.json wasn't committed.

issues.title: Bare `dummyindex install` (run to elicit usage text) executed a full install and started AST-scanning the entire 1426-file monorepo from the wrong root
issues.evidence:
=== install usage (trigger usage text) ===
  memory skill     ->  /home/ahmed/.claude/skills/dummyindex-remember/SKILL.md
  ...
  skill installed  ->  /home/ahmed/.claude/skills/dummyindex/SKILL.md
  AST extraction: 100/1426 files (7%)
  AST extraction: 1000/1426 files (70%)
[next turn] "That was a mistake — bare `dummyindex install` doesn't print help, it **executed a full install**: it re-registered user-scope skills and began an AST scan of the entire monorepo (1426 files) from the mono root."
issues.what_happened: After `dummyindex install --help` errored, Claude ran bare `dummyindex install` expecting usage text. Instead it immediately performed a full install: re-registered all user-scope skills and began AST extraction of the whole BOS-Mono monorepo (1426 files) from the mono root (not the backend submodule), creating a stray untracked `.context/cache/` at the mono root that Claude then had to verify and `rm -rf`. Damage was limited only because the scan didn't finish writing meta.json/features/.
issues.root_cause_guess: `install` has no `--help` handler and no zero-arg usage/confirmation guard; it treats a bare invocation as 'install here now' and defaults its index root to cwd, which in a monorepo is the superproject rather than the intended submodule.

issues.title: Stop reconcile-gate blocks session claiming `.context/ is stale` when drifted_features is empty — triggered by dummyindex's own skill-install artifact
issues.evidence:
blockingError: "dummyindex reconcile gate: `.context/` is stale after a substantial session. ... for each drifted feature below, re-run its council enrichment (`/dummyindex --recouncil <feature>`); ... Do NOT skip silently — this is the per-session reconcile gate. New unplaced files: .claude/skills/dummyindex-update/SKILL.md."
[reconcile --json]: "drifted_features": [], "unassigned_new_files": [".claude/skills/dummyindex-update/SKILL.md"], "has_drift": true
issues.what_happened: After the 0.25.0 skill upgrade, the Stop hook blocked the session demanding a council reconcile of 'drifted features' — but `reconcile --json` showed zero drifted features. The sole trigger was `.claude/skills/dummyindex-update/SKILL.md`, a file dummyindex's OWN `install --skill-only` had just written. The same class of false positive had already occurred earlier with equip-generated `.claude/agents/backend-*-specialist.md` files. Resolution each time was `reconcile-stamp --force` busywork. Directly led the user to disable the gate.
issues.root_cause_guess: The gate's staleness predicate is `has_drift`, which counts any git-added file not owned by a feature — including dummyindex/equip's own `.claude/**` tooling output. dummyindex should exempt its own managed paths (`.claude/skills/dummyindex*`, generated agents) from the unassigned-files set, or at least not block when drifted_features is empty.

issues.title: No supported way to exclude `.claude/**` (or any path) from the reconcile delta — ignore files only gate the index build
issues.evidence: "Definitive finding: **there's no supported way to make the canonical gate ignore `.claude/**`.** ... `.dummyindexignore` / `.codeindexignore` exist — but they only gate the **index build** ... They do *not* touch the gate. The gate's `unassigned_new_files` comes from `compute_reconcile_report` → raw **`git diff` added paths**, filtered only by `_is_context_path` ... The config schema (`scope`, `mode`, `model`, `auto_refresh_hook`, `external_docs`, `auto_council`) has **no exclude/ignore field**."
issues.what_happened: When the user asked to stop the `.claude/**` nag while keeping the gate, Claude traced the installed package source (reconcile.py, git_delta.py, config.py) and proved no exclusion mechanism exists for the reconcile path. The only remedies were: do nothing (accept recurring nags on every tooling change), disable the gate entirely via auto_council:false, or resurrect a hand-rolled filter hook the frontend repo had just retired.
issues.root_cause_guess: Reconcile delta computation was built directly on raw git diff with only a `.context/**` filter; the ignore-file mechanism was wired only into the AST/structure walk. Config schema never gained an exclude/ignore knob, so the gate is all-or-nothing.

issues.title: No subcommand `--help` anywhere: reconcile, council-batch, and install all reject `--help` as an unknown argument
issues.evidence:
error: unknown argument(s) for `reconcile`: ['--help']
...
===council-batch help===
error: unknown argument(s) for `council-batch`: ['--help']
...
=== dummyindex install --help ===
error: unknown install argument '--help'
issues.what_happened: Three separate times in one session, asking a subcommand for help produced an argparse-style error instead of usage text. Claude had to fall back to dumping the full `dummyindex context` usage blob and grepping it. Worst consequence: the `install --help` failure led directly to running bare `dummyindex install`, which executed a real install (see installer issue).
issues.root_cause_guess: Hand-rolled argument parsing per subcommand without a standard `--help` flag; unknown-argument errors instead of help output. Make `--help` (and bare invocation for destructive verbs) print usage.

issues.title: No CLI path to re-council drifted-but-already-enriched features; council-batch frontier omits them and confidence must be hand-edited
issues.evidence: "council-batch surfaces un-enriched features (`icon-catalog`, `utils-*`), not my drifted ones — its frontier is the per-feature council log, so the already-enriched-but-drifted features need explicit scoped re-council." ... "To re-council the drifted features I need to re-open their council logs. Let me find the reset mechanism" ... "Now: flip `icon-catalog` to INFERRED (no CLI for it; it's genuinely enriched now with full council/ dir)"
issues.what_happened: The core task — fold 5 drifted features back through the council — is unsupported by the CLI. `council-batch --next` only surfaces features with no council log (it offered icon-catalog plus trivial utils-* stubs instead). Claude had to hand-orchestrate the whole pipeline: manually dispatch dev/architect/critic subagents per drifted feature, manually `cp` plan.md to council/01-dev-draft.md, and manually edit feature.json to flip confidence EXTRACTED→INFERRED because no verb exists to mark a feature enriched or reset its log.
issues.root_cause_guess: The council frontier is keyed solely on the per-feature council log, with no awareness of the reconcile report's drifted_features; missing verbs: a council-log reset/re-open, a confidence/mark-enriched setter, and a `--recouncil <feature>` CLI primitive matching what the skill docs reference.

issues.title: Committed generated .context/ artifacts cause merge conflicts on every rebase (3 of 4 commits conflicted)
issues.evidence:
CONFLICT (content): Merge conflict in .context/meta.json
error: could not apply fba707a... chore: update indexed_commit and updated_at in meta.json
[next commit]
CONFLICT (content): Merge conflict in .context/conventions/naming.md
CONFLICT (content): Merge conflict in .context/features/INDEX.json
CONFLICT (content): Merge conflict in .context/features/INDEX.md
CONFLICT (content): Merge conflict in .context/features/app-infra-tools/spec.md
CONFLICT (content): Merge conflict in .context/features/symbol-graph.json
CONFLICT (content): Merge conflict in .context/source-docs/INDEX.json
issues.what_happened: A routine `git rebase origin/development` conflicted on 3 of 4 replayed commits — every conflict was in generated `.context/` files (timestamps, file counts, symbol snapshots, generated-at lines). Claude resolved all with `checkout --ours` and regenerated afterward, but this consumed the first ~25% of the session and is structural: dummyindex both mandates committing the index ('dedicated commits') and regenerates volatile per-machine content into tracked files.
issues.root_cause_guess: Volatile fields (updated_at, indexed_commit, '_Generated <timestamp>_' lines, counts) live inside tracked artifacts with no merge driver/.gitattributes strategy and no 'regenerate on conflict' guidance; concurrent sessions on diverged branches inevitably collide.

issues.title: Five dummyindex installs at four different versions; repo skill wiring silently drifts releases behind the CLI
issues.evidence: "Three installs exist: backend `.venv` (0.24.0), the global `~/.local/bin/dummyindex`, and user-scope skill `~/.claude` (0.24.0) — plus the two repo skills (backend 0.22.0, frontend 0.19.2)." ... "=== which + version === /mnt/windows-ssd/Projects/opensession/BOS-Mono/backend/.venv/bin/dummyindex | 0.24.0"
issues.what_happened: The user had to explicitly demand 'both dummyindex need to be on 0.25.0'. Claude found a fragmented install matrix: backend venv dev-dep CLI shadowing a global pip --user CLI, plus three skill copies at 0.24.0/0.22.0/0.19.2. Upgrading required a manual multi-step plan (pip --user upgrade, pyproject floor bump + uv lock/sync, then three `install --skill-only` runs) instead of one update command. The dedicated /dummyindex-update skill was never used — its documented detection covers uv tool/pipx/pip --user but not a project-venv dev-dep install.
issues.root_cause_guess: No single source of truth or self-sync between CLI and the per-repo/per-user skill copies; repo skills only refresh when someone re-runs install. Version-skew detection exists only as a note in SKILL.md, and the update skill's install-detection misses the venv dev-dep pattern.

issues.title: meta.json dummyindex_version frozen at 0.15.0 — non-destructive rebuild path never re-stamps it; fixed by hand-editing
issues.evidence:
.context/meta.json:6:  "dummyindex_version": "0.15.0",
[user]: "no it should all show the current version"
[assistant]: "the `dummyindex_version` field only re-stamps on a *destructive* full rebuild (which re-clusters and wipes curated features — never run here). The non-destructive `rebuild --changed` path deliberately preserves it, so it had been frozen at its original `0.15.0` creation stamp."
issues.what_happened: After upgrading everything to 0.25.0, the user asked whether everything was up to date; meta.json still said 0.15.0 and the user pushed back ('no it should all show the current version'). Claude traced rebuild.py/incremental.py to confirm only a destructive --full re-cluster re-stamps the version, then hand-edited meta.json and verified the edit survives refresh-indexes and rebuild --changed.
issues.root_cause_guess: The version stamp is treated as a creation-time field; the safe incremental path intentionally preserves all of meta.json instead of updating the tool-version field, leaving no non-destructive way to record which version maintains the index.

issues.title: reconcile-stamp refuses on intentionally-unowned tooling files, forcing --force with a misleading 'WILL be re-reported / stop tracking it' warning
issues.evidence:
context reconcile-stamp: anchor advanced to 303dad1bb17d
  WARNING: --force advanced the anchor past un-reconciled work, but did NOT resolve it (markers and untracked files persist). It WILL be re-reported next reconcile until you place/enrich it or stop tracking it:
  unassigned new files: .claude/agents/backend-docs-specialist.md, .claude/agents/backend-security-specialist.md
issues.what_happened: Twice in the session (equip-generated agent files, then the dummyindex-update SKILL.md) a plain `reconcile-stamp` would not advance the anchor because of `.claude/**` files no feature can own, requiring `--force`. The warning is doubly wrong: it threatens re-reporting that does not actually happen for committed files (Claude verified has_drift:false immediately after), and it suggests a 'stop tracking it' remedy that the code provides no mechanism for (no ignore/exclude in the reconcile path).
issues.root_cause_guess: Stamp validation treats all unassigned files as unresolved work with no 'intentionally unowned' classification, and the --force warning text was written generically without checking whether the files are committed behind the new anchor or whether a stop-tracking mechanism exists.

issues.title: Docs/CLI mismatch: documented update path `dummyindex context reconcile` is only a read-only report; the actual reconcile is an undocumented-at-CLI skill orchestration
issues.evidence:
[assistant]: "There's no single `context reconcile` CLI command — the LLM council reconcile is orchestrated by the `dummyindex` skill (the `--recouncil` flow). The CLI only exposes the primitives."
[user, earlier]: "was there a recouncil?" ... "i need .context to be up to date with llm coucil and everything done to date"
issues.what_happened: Project CLAUDE.md instructs 'run `dummyindex context reconcile`' to fold changes in, and the Stop-gate message likewise names `/dummyindex --recouncil <feature>`. But `context reconcile` is just a JSON delta report and `--recouncil` has no CLI form — the real procedure is a multi-stage skill orchestration in council/65-reconcile.md. The user clearly believed updating .context was a single command ('was there a recouncil?'), and Claude spent several turns of CLI archaeology (failed --help, dumping usage, reading 6 council docs) before reconstructing the actual path.
issues.root_cause_guess: Naming collision between the CLI report verb and the skill-level procedure, plus instructions (CLAUDE.md, gate text) that present the skill workflow as if it were a CLI command.

issues.title: tree.json never enriched: all 1117 nodes still deterministic stubs from the original ingest
issues.evidence:
context enrich-plan: wrote .context/cache/_enrich_plan.json
  total nodes: 1117  stubs: 1117  by_kind: {'project': 1, 'dir': 48, 'file': 176, 'class': 49, 'method': 257, 'function': 586}
  batches: 177
[assistant]: "All 1117 tree nodes are stubs — `tree.json` was never enriched in the original run. That's a **whole-repo, pre-existing gap** (177 file-batches)"
issues.what_happened: During the reconcile, Claude discovered the PageIndex-style tree.json — the artifact future sessions navigate by — was 100% deterministic stubs (1117/1117 nodes), meaning the original full ingest never completed (or never ran) Phase 4.5 tree enrichment. It was flagged as out-of-scope and left unfixed, so retrieval over the tree still reads auto-generated stubs, not prose.
issues.root_cause_guess: The original ingest either skipped tree-enrich silently or was interrupted with no completion check; nothing in status/preflight/drift reporting surfaces 'tree enrichment 0% complete', so the gap persisted invisibly across versions 0.15→0.25.

issues.title: Gate UX drove auto_council flip-flop: user toggled it false then back to true within the same session
issues.evidence:
[user]: "Option: auto_council: false corrct this"
[~30 events later, user]: "auto_council needs to be true and eveything needs to be updated and everything needs to be synced"
issues.what_happened: After the false-positive gate block and Claude's finding that nags can't be filtered, the user chose the nuclear option (auto_council:false, committed as 7033b07), losing the drift enforcement he had explicitly asked for earlier ('update .claude to run recoucil and update .context after each run'). One caveat-list later he reversed it (committed 2e8a282). Two dedicated commits and several turns spent toggling one boolean because the gate offers no middle ground.
issues.root_cause_guess: auto_council is the gate's only knob and it is binary; without an unassigned-file exemption or path filter, users oscillate between 'nagged about tooling files' and 'no enforcement at all'.

issues.title: Stale retired hook generations litter the repos: frontend kept dummyindex-backbone-refresh.sh after retiring its sibling, nearly causing the backend to adopt a dead pattern
issues.evidence: "the frontend has a dedicated `hooks/` directory with `dummyindex-backbone-refresh.sh` and `dummyindex-recouncil-on-stop.sh`" ... "=== commit 94945054 full message === chore(claude): use canonical dummyindex reconcile-gate for the stop gate — Replace the hand-rolled dummyindex-recouncil-on-stop.sh with the dummyindex-maintained `dummyindex context reconcile-gate` Stop hook"
issues.what_happened: Asked to improve the backend .claude from the frontend's, Claude found hand-rolled stop/refresh hook scripts in the frontend (from skill v0.19.2 era), concluded they were the desired automation, and was preparing to port them — only discovering mid-task via git history that the frontend had already retired the recouncil hook in favor of the canonical gate. `dummyindex-backbone-refresh.sh` still lingered in the frontend tree. Required an AskUserQuestion to untangle.
issues.root_cause_guess: dummyindex version upgrades and `install` re-runs don't clean up or migrate hook wiring from earlier generations, so repos accumulate mixed-generation .claude artifacts that mislead later sessions.

issues.title: Inconsistent council state files: enriched features missing .hash baselines; INDEX.json schema tripped naive parsing
issues.evidence:
=== .hash files for drifted features ===
assets-resources: hash=3666c16bf1b69ace... confidence=INFERRED
...
mcp-server: NO .hash  confidence=INFERRED
icon-catalog: NO .hash  confidence=EXTRACTED
[separately]
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
    ... json.load(open('.context/features/INDEX.json')) ...
KeyError: 'id'
issues.what_happened: While reconstructing enrichment state, Claude found mcp-server (fully enriched, INFERRED) had no `.hash` source-baseline file — the artifact the documented resume logic uses to detect drift — and icon-catalog likewise. Separately, a first attempt to read features/INDEX.json failed with KeyError because the entry schema doesn't use an 'id' key, requiring a second probing pass. Both are small but forced extra forensic turns.
issues.root_cause_guess: Hash baselines are written by some pipeline paths but not others (e.g., features arriving via merge or older versions), and the JSON index schemas are undocumented, so even the orchestrating agent must reverse-engineer them per session.

summary: Session in the BOS-Mono backend submodule: a git rebase onto origin/development (where generated .context/ files conflicted on 3 of 4 commits), followed by a user-driven dummyindex maintenance arc — re-council of 5 drifted features, wiring 'reconcile after each run', upgrading all five dummyindex layers to 0.25.0, and syncing via PR. dummyindex ultimately got the job done, but only because Claude hand-orchestrated everything: the CLI has no subcommand --help (which caused an accidental full install that AST-scanned the whole 1426-file monorepo from the wrong root), council-batch can't surface drifted features so re-council and confidence flips were manual, and the Stop reconcile-gate false-positive-blocked on dummyindex's own skill-install artifacts with no ignore mechanism, driving the user to toggle auto_council off and back on. Notably, no plugin discover/install/wire activity occurred in this session, so the owner's specific plugin-wiring concern isn't evidenced here — the struggles are concentrated in installer/CLI UX, reconcile gating, and council orchestration.
