issues.title: rebuild --changed on CLI 0.25.0 re-shatters curated 13-feature taxonomy into 838 community-N clusters
issues.evidence: "⚠️ **Stop — something went wrong.** The pre-commit's `dummyindex context rebuild --changed` (now on 0.25.0) appears to have **re-shattered the curated 13-feature taxonomy into hundreds of `community-N` clusters**" ... later controlled retest: "=== run rebuild --changed (stamp now 0.25.0 — does it re-shatter?) === context rebuild: 0 added, 5 modified, 0 removed → rebuilt ... community dirs created: 838 / curated features intact: 13/13 / untracked paths: 838"
issues.what_happened: A pre-commit hook wired to `dummyindex context rebuild --changed` (per user request to auto-update .context) polluted the working tree with 838 untracked .context/features/community-N/ dirs and rewrote 17 tracked .context files mid-commit, aborting the commit. Under 0.24.0 the same command had printed "enriched index preserved ... no re-cluster" (safe). The agent had to `git clean -fd .context/` + `git checkout HEAD -- .context/` to recover, then deliberately re-ran the command as a test on the now-0.25.0-stamped index and it re-shattered AGAIN (proving the regression is version-of-CLI, not index-stamp). All rebuild automation (Stop hook + pre-commit) was then reverted as unsafe. Occurred twice (incident + retest).
issues.root_cause_guess: 0.25.0 regression: rebuild --changed no longer takes the preserve-enriched/no-recluster path on a curated index — it runs full community clustering (likely the curated-index detection or migration path broke between 0.24.0 and 0.25.0), writing raw community-N feature dirs alongside the curated taxonomy.

issues.title: dummyindex install (0.25.0) rewrote .claude/settings.json and silently dropped a user-added Stop hook
issues.evidence: "Heads-up — the 0.25.0 `dummyindex install` **rewrote `settings.json` and dropped my `DUMMYINDEX_BACKBONE_REFRESH` Stop hook** (the canonical install only re-adds memory-nudge + reconcile-gate). That removed the session-end backbone refresh you asked for."
issues.what_happened: When the CLI moved 0.24.0→0.25.0 mid-session and re-ran install, it regenerated .claude/settings.json to the canonical hook set, deleting the custom Stop hook the user had explicitly asked to be wired this same session. The agent had to detect the loss by diffing and hand-restore it (then later removed it anyway because of the rebuild regression).
issues.root_cause_guess: install writes its canonical settings.json hook list non-additively — it replaces the hooks arrays instead of merging/preserving entries that don't carry its own sentinel comments, violating the 'never clobbering a user edit' contract.

issues.title: reconcile-gate Stop hook repeatedly blocks sessions over dummyindex's OWN tooling/skill files
issues.evidence: "dummyindex reconcile gate: `.context/` is stale after a substantial session. ... Do NOT skip silently — this is the per-session reconcile gate. New unplaced files: .claude/skills/dummyindex-equip/templates/db-specialist-agent.md.tmpl, ... .claude/skills/dummyindex-update/SKILL.md, .claude/skills/dummyindex/council/22-parallel-dispatch.md."
issues.what_happened: The Stop-hook gate fired at least 3 times in this session with drifted_features=[] — the only "staleness" was unassigned_new_files consisting of dummyindex's own artifacts (.claude/hooks/*.sh written this session, then 11 skill files written by the 0.25.0 upgrade). No feature source had changed, so no council was warranted, yet the gate instructed running the reconcile procedure. Each time the agent had to investigate, commit the tooling files, and run `reconcile-stamp --force` to clear it. The agent's own hand-rolled gate had the same false positive in its first smoke test (blocked on a clean index because of two new hook scripts).
issues.root_cause_guess: The gate's staleness predicate treats unassigned_new_files (including .claude/** tooling that is by convention feature-unowned, and files dummyindex itself just installed) the same as real feature drift; there is no exclusion list for self-generated/tooling paths.

issues.title: council-batch --next cannot be scoped to drifted features — forces whole-repo council, contradicting the reconcile doc
issues.evidence: "`council-batch --next` walks the frontier across **all** features (it's the unscoped, whole-repo path — note it pulled in chat, brain, projects, etc. which didn't change). The reconcile doc is explicit that I should scope to the drifted features only: *\"Always pass the feature id. Bare --recouncil re-runs the whole repo — the opposite of a scoped reconcile.\"*" ... "`council-batch` has no `--feature` scoping — it's whole-repo only, and the logs show stage 2 was never logged complete across the repo, so that loop would re-council all 13 features."
issues.what_happened: During the user-requested recouncil of 4 drifted features, the documented batch loop (`council-batch --next`) returned dispatch units for 8 unrelated, unchanged features (chat, brain, projects, home, account...). The skill's own reconcile doc demands scoping, but the CLI primitive it points at has no --feature flag, and council-log state was stale (stage 2 never logged complete repo-wide). The agent abandoned the CLI loop and hand-orchestrated 12 scoped agent dispatches manually.
issues.root_cause_guess: Mismatch between council/65-reconcile.md (scoped reconcile) and the council-batch CLI surface (whole-repo frontier only, no per-feature filter); council-log completion state also doesn't survive/reflect prior sessions, so the frontier restarts from scratch.

issues.title: reality-check files false contradictions on doc/manifest citations and auto-demotes features to AMBIGUOUS with no promote path
issues.evidence: "=== reality-check: tests === ## Contradicted ... - ``package.json:20`` (plan.md) — file not found - ``package.json:14`` (concerns.md) — file not found" ... "shared-lib: feature.json confidence = AMBIGUOUS ... tests: feature.json confidence = AMBIGUOUS" ... "`mark-enriched` only clears the pending marker; there's no explicit confidence setter, and reality-check only *demotes*, never promotes."
issues.what_happened: Fresh, correct council docs cited `spec.md:15` and `package.json:14/:20` (legitimate references to a doc line and npm scripts); reality-check --demote treated these as AST-verifiable source claims, reported "file not found", and demoted shared-lib and tests to confidence AMBIGUOUS in feature.json and INDEX.json. There is no CLI command to restore confidence, so the agent hand-edited feature.json + INDEX.json back to EXTRACTED and reworded the docs to strip the path:line cites before re-running reality-check clean.
issues.root_cause_guess: reality-check's claim extractor treats every `path:line` citation as a source-code claim, but its file resolver can't find non-indexed files (package.json, the spec.md doc itself); demote-on-contradiction is one-way with no corresponding promote/undo verb.

issues.title: CLI subcommands reject --help (and other discovery flags) with 'unknown argument(s)'
issues.evidence: "===== council-batch help ===== error: unknown argument(s) for `council-batch`: ['--help'] ===== brand-hub council-log ===== error: unknown argument(s) for `council-log`: ['--show']" ... "=== mark-enriched help === error: unknown argument(s) for `mark-enriched`: ['--help'] === section-write help === error: unknown argument(s) for `section-write`: ['--help']" ... "=== equip subcommand help === error: unknown argument(s) for `equip`: ['--help']"
issues.what_happened: Six separate help/discovery attempts failed: council-batch --help, council-log --show, mark-enriched --help, section-write --help, equip --help, plus `dummyindex --status` ("error: unknown command '--status'" — a skill-doc concept that isn't a CLI flag). Each failure forced the agent to grep the giant monolithic `dummyindex context --help` output instead, slowing every decision point.
issues.root_cause_guess: The argparse setup only implements --help at the top/context level; subcommand parsers treat unknown flags as errors rather than supporting per-subcommand help, and the skill docs reference verbs/flags (--status, --show) that don't exist in the CLI.

issues.title: Canonical reconcile-gate command undiscoverable from the skill — session hand-rolled a duplicate Stop-hook gate first
issues.evidence: "Big finding: the backend's Stop hook uses a **canonical dummyindex command I didn't know existed** — `dummyindex context reconcile-gate`. That's the purpose-built Stop-hook gate for exactly what I hand-rolled with `dummyindex-recouncil-on-stop.sh`."
issues.what_happened: Asked to automate recouncil-after-each-run, the agent (driving the dummyindex skill) wrote, tested, debugged (it false-blocked on a clean index) and committed a ~67-line bespoke `dummyindex-recouncil-on-stop.sh`, only discovering the built-in `dummyindex context reconcile-gate` by accident while browsing the sibling backend repo's settings.json. A whole build/test/commit cycle was then reverted in favor of the canonical command.
issues.root_cause_guess: reconcile-gate isn't mentioned in the user-scope skill docs / council procedure files the session read (it ships in newer per-repo wiring only), so the documented path for 'auto-keep .context current' doesn't surface the purpose-built primitive.

issues.title: reconcile-stamp --force warning is wrong/misleading about re-reporting
issues.evidence: "context reconcile-stamp: anchor advanced to 29d029b36ab4   WARNING: --force advanced the anchor past un-reconciled work, but did NOT resolve it (markers and untracked files persist). It WILL be re-reported next reconcile until you place/enrich it or stop tracking it: unassigned new files: .claude/skills/dummyindex-equip/templates/db-specialist-agent.md.tmpl, ... === verify unassigned cleared === anchor: 29d029b36ab4 | has_drift: False | unassigned: 0"
issues.what_happened: Three reconcile-stamp --force runs each emitted the scary 'WILL be re-reported next reconcile' warning, yet the very next `reconcile --json` showed has_drift: False / unassigned: 0 — files committed behind the anchor drop out, exactly as the user's memory notes predicted. The contradiction sent the agent down extra verification loops each time ("the --force warning was generic") before trusting the actual state.
issues.root_cause_guess: The warning text is emitted unconditionally on --force without checking whether the listed files are committed at/behind the new anchor (in which case they will NOT re-report).

issues.title: equip-generated agent files are not formatter-stable — repo prettier flips them to user-modified
issues.evidence: "user-modified frontend-docs-specialist  (v1.0.0) user-modified frontend-security-specialist  (v1.0.0)" ... "Root cause found: equip's render isn't prettier-stable, and the frontend's prettier hook formats `.md` — so every commit flips equip agents to `user-modified`. (The backend avoids this entirely: it uses `ruff`, which never touches `.md`.)" ... "=== does prettier WANT to change the pristine render? === [warn] Code style issues found in 2 files."
issues.what_happened: After `equip add-specialist security|docs` succeeded, committing the new agents through the repo's pre-commit prettier hook reformatted the .md files, so `equip status` classified them user-modified vs their origin-hash baseline. Even a fresh `equip reset` render immediately failed `prettier --check`, threatening a permanent reset↔prettier loop. The fix had to be done outside dummyindex: adding `.claude/` paths to .prettierignore.
issues.root_cause_guess: equip's hash-baseline integrity check assumes byte-identical files post-generation, but the rendered markdown isn't normalized to common formatter styles; equip neither formats its output with the repo's detected formatter nor excludes its artifacts from format hooks it knows about (it even wires a PostToolUse formatter hook itself).

issues.title: Committed .context generated artifacts cause repeated rebase merge conflicts and massive churn
issues.evidence: "CONFLICT (content): Merge conflict in .context/features/brand-hub/feature.json ... error: could not apply 7965b456... docs(context): reconcile illustrations into brand-hub + tests features" ... "[detached HEAD d6e9c6e5] chore(context): refresh deterministic backbone after rebase onto development  10 files changed, 36137 insertions(+), 34837 deletions(-)"
issues.what_happened: A 9-commit rebase onto origin/development conflicted 3 separate times, exclusively on dummyindex-generated files: feature.json (twice, requiring 8 manual union-merge edits of generated JSON member/file arrays), plus 7 deterministic backbone files (naming.md, INDEX.json, symbol-graph.json, map/*.json, meta.json) where hand-merging was pointless and the agent took --theirs then regenerated. Backbone refresh commits run to ~36k changed lines each, guaranteeing conflicts on any parallel branch.
issues.root_cause_guess: dummyindex's workflow commits huge order-sensitive generated JSON artifacts into git with no merge driver, no union strategy (.gitattributes), and no 'regenerate instead of merge' guidance — so any two branches that both reconcile will conflict.

issues.title: Version stamps disagree across layers; mid-session CLI upgrade churned tracked skill files
issues.evidence: "=== CLI on PATH === 0.25.0 === repo skill stamp === 0.25.0 === .context index stamp (meta.json) === dummyindex_version: 0.19.2 | schema_version: 1" ... "Full picture: the **CLI upgraded 0.24.0 → 0.25.0 mid-session** and rewrote the skill files — that's all 11 \"unplaced\" files plus the modified `SKILL.md`/council docs."
issues.what_happened: When the user asked "whats the dummyindex version in this repo?", three stamps disagreed: CLI 0.25.0, repo skill 0.25.0, index meta.json 0.19.2 (and earlier in the same session the skill header said 0.24.0). The mid-session 0.24.0→0.25.0 upgrade rewrote 11 new + ~12 modified tracked .claude/skills files, forcing an unplanned 'sync dummyindex skill to 0.25.0' commit, triggering the reconcile gate, and the stale 0.19.2 index stamp was initially (wrongly) blamed for the re-shatter regression. The index stamp only reached 0.25.0 as an incidental side effect of reconcile-stamp during incident recovery.
issues.root_cause_guess: No single-source version contract: rebuild doesn't update meta.json's dummyindex_version, installs rewrite per-repo skill copies without coordinating a commit, and nothing reconciles the three stamps, so diagnosing version-dependent behavior (like the re-shatter) is guesswork.

issues.title: feature.json confidence semantics are opaque — rebuild resets councilled features to EXTRACTED, enrichment signal hides in markdown headers
issues.evidence: "Now it's clear: **every** feature in this repo carries `feature.json confidence: EXTRACTED` — including fully-councilled ones (chat, auth, brain) ... The enrichment signal lives in the markdown headers (`spec.md: confidence: INFERRED`), not `feature.json`. So `EXTRACTED` is the correct normal state."
issues.what_happened: After rebuild --changed, all four re-enriched features showed confidence EXTRACTED in feature.json, prompting the user to ask "was there a recouncil?" and then demand "i need .context to be up to date with llm coucil and everything done to date". The agent spent multiple tool calls reverse-engineering that feature.json EXTRACTED is normal even post-council and the real enrichment flag is a `confidence: INFERRED` header inside spec.md/plan.md — two parallel confidence systems with different meanings.
issues.root_cause_guess: Dual confidence representations (feature.json/INDEX.json enum vs markdown front-line) that rebuild and council update independently; no status verb reports enrichment state coherently, so both user and agent doubt whether the council ran.

issues.title: Stale help text: equip help says equipment.json v2 while the actual manifest is schema_version 3
issues.evidence: "equip [apply] [path] ... record it in .context/equipment.json (v2): a <stack>-implementer + <stack>-tester agent..." while the manifest read seconds earlier shows: "{ \"schema_version\": 3, \"items\": [ { \"kind\": \"agent\", \"name\": \"typescript-implementer\", ..."
issues.what_happened: The 0.25.0 CLI's own `context --help` for equip still documents the manifest as v2, but the file equip writes/reads in this repo is schema_version 3. Harmless this session, but it is exactly the kind of doc/CLI drift that made other behaviors (council-batch scoping, --status) hard to trust.
issues.root_cause_guess: Help strings hardcode the schema version and weren't updated when the equipment.json schema bumped to v3 (plugin-manager fields: marketplace, origin_repo, origin_ref, mechanism).

summary: Session in the BOS-Mono frontend submodule: rebase a feature branch onto development (conflicts landed almost entirely in dummyindex-generated .context files), then a user-requested full LLM recouncil of 4 drifted features, then wiring .claude hooks to auto-recouncil/refresh .context after each run, followed by a mid-session CLI upgrade to 0.25.0 and an equip-specialist gap-fill. The scoped council itself completed (12 manual agent dispatches), but dummyindex caused serious trouble around it: a critical 0.25.0 regression where `rebuild --changed` re-shattered the curated taxonomy into 838 community-N clusters (twice), `dummyindex install` clobbering a user-added Stop hook in settings.json, the reconcile-gate repeatedly blocking sessions over dummyindex's own tooling/skill files, council-batch lacking the per-feature scoping its own reconcile doc requires, reality-check false-demoting freshly councilled features to AMBIGUOUS, and pervasive CLI/doc ergonomics failures (no subcommand --help, misleading --force warnings, undiscoverable reconcile-gate, stale help text). The agent recovered everything via git surgery and manual workarounds, but roughly half the session was spent fighting the tool rather than using it.

issues.title: reconcile-stamp repeatedly REFUSES over equip-generated agent files; agent forced into --force twice
issues.evidence:
context reconcile-stamp: REFUSED — un-reconciled work remains (advancing the anchor would silently forget it):
  unassigned new files: .claude/agents/backend-docs-specialist.md, .claude/agents/backend-security-specialist.md
  Reconcile them (place files, enrich features) or re-run with --force to anchor anyway. ... WARNING: --force advanced the anchor past un-reconciled work, but did NOT resolve it (markers and untracked files persist). It WILL be re-reported next reconcile until you place/enrich it or stop tracking it
issues.what_happened: `equip apply --for-proposal` legitimately wrote two new specialist agents into .claude/agents/ and recorded them in equipment.json. reconcile then counted them as 'unassigned new files' and reconcile-stamp refused to advance the anchor. Claude reasoned they were tooling, not feature source, and ran --force — twice in one session (planning Stop and post-build close-out), each time getting the 'WILL be re-reported next reconcile' warning. The files also kept appearing in every reconcile/rebuild output (4+ occurrences). dummyindex's own equip output poisons its own reconcile loop with no sanctioned 'stop tracking it' verb shown, training the agent to habitually --force past the integrity gate.
issues.root_cause_guess: reconcile's unassigned-new-file detection scans the whole tree (including .claude/) and does not exclude paths already owned by .context/equipment.json; no lightweight 'ignore/acknowledge file' verb exists short of feature assignment or --force.

issues.title: Plan skill hardcodes critique-panel subagent types that don't exist; 2 of 3 dispatches fail
issues.evidence: Agent type 'Software Architect' not found. Available agents: Accessibility Auditor, Agentic Identity & Trust Architect, ... Backend Architect, backend-reviewer, ... // Agent type 'Code Reviewer' not found. Available agents: ...
issues.what_happened: dummyindex-plan's SKILL.md table prescribes exact subagent_type values for its critique panel — `Software Architect`, `Code Reviewer`, `Test Results Analyzer`. In this environment only `Test Results Analyzer` resolved; the other two Task dispatches errored and had to be re-dispatched (general-purpose + the project's backend-reviewer). The fallback rule exists in the skill but is positioned as an afterthought, so the failure path is the default path: every /dummyindex-plan run in a repo without those agency-agents names burns two failed Task calls.
issues.root_cause_guess: SKILL.md ships fixed persona names copied from the agency-agents collection instead of resolving available agent types first (the way the council's dev-pick does) or defaulting to general-purpose with the mandate inlined.

issues.title: Stop-hook reconcile gate blocks a planning-only session where no source code changed
issues.evidence: dummyindex reconcile gate: `.context/` is stale after a substantial session. ... Do NOT skip silently — this is the per-session reconcile gate. New unplaced files: .claude/agents/backend-docs-specialist.md, .claude/agents/backend-security-specialist.md. // Claude: "I didn't touch any `app/` source this session — the work was a planning proposal under `.context/proposals/...` plus `equip apply`. So the code-behavior index hasn't actually drifted"
issues.what_happened: The /dummyindex-plan session only wrote proposal files under .context/ and ran equip apply, yet the Stop hook's reconcile gate blocked session end demanding the full council reconcile procedure. The only 'drift' was the two equip-generated agent files. Claude had to argue the gate down and force-stamp to end the session.
issues.root_cause_guess: reconcile-gate's substantial-session heuristic (subagent count + output tokens) fires regardless of whether the session changed feature source; combined with issue #1 (equip outputs counted as unplaced files) it produces a false-positive block.

issues.title: Reconcile gate's prescribed fix `/dummyindex --recouncil` is not a CLI verb; agent burns multiple calls discovering this
issues.evidence: Gate: "for each drifted feature below, re-run its council enrichment (`/dummyindex --recouncil <feature>`); place any new files; then `dummyindex context reconcile-stamp`" // Claude: "I've confirmed `recouncil` isn't a lightweight CLI step — it's the full council (multi-agent LLM) enrichment driven by the `/dummyindex` skill."
issues.what_happened: The gate message and reconcile output ('Run the council reconcile procedure (`/dummyindex --recouncil`)') present recouncil as if it were a runnable command. Claude grepped `dummyindex --help` and `dummyindex context --help` twice looking for a recouncil/enrich verb before concluding it's a skill-level multi-agent pass, then had to interrupt the user with a question because the only options were a heavy LLM council or --force. Recurred across both Stop-gate firings.
issues.root_cause_guess: Gate copy conflates the slash-skill invocation with a CLI subcommand and offers no middle-weight deterministic option; the message should distinguish skill vs CLI and state the cost of each.

issues.title: council-batch --next cannot be scoped to target features — unusable for a scoped recouncil
issues.evidence:
council-batch: stage 1 — 2 parallel units (dispatch concurrently, barrier, then re-run --next):
  utils-test-conftest: dev → SubagentType.AI [AI]
  utils-typing: dev → SubagentType.AI [AI] // Claude: "`council-batch --next` is tracking *global* council state (it surfaced `utils-test-conftest`/`utils-typing`, not my targets) — so it's not the right driver for a **scoped** recouncil."
issues.what_happened: When the user chose 'Run recouncil now' for the two features the build touched, Claude tried the documented council driver `council-batch --next`, but it surfaced two unrelated features from global council state with no flag to scope it. Claude abandoned the CLI driver and hand-orchestrated all three stages (manual council-log start/complete calls, manual `cp` snapshots of dev drafts) — error-prone plumbing the CLI was supposed to own.
issues.root_cause_guess: council-batch only iterates the global pending-council queue; it lacks a --feature/--only filter mirroring `--recouncil <feature>` semantics.

issues.title: CLI subcommands reject --help: per-subcommand help is unsupported
issues.evidence:
error: unknown argument(s) for `assign-files`: ['--help'] ... error: unknown argument(s) for `council-batch`: ['--help']
=== enrich-plan ===
error: unknown argument(s) for `enrich-plan`: ['--help']
issues.what_happened: Three times during the reconcile close-out Claude tried `dummyindex context <verb> --help` to learn flags (assign-files, council-batch, enrich-plan) and got an argparse-style rejection each time. It fell back to grepping the monolithic top-level help output, slowing discovery of the right reconcile verbs.
issues.root_cause_guess: The custom argument parser treats --help as a positional/unknown arg on subcommands instead of wiring per-subcommand help.

issues.title: build --next-wave keyword-matches a docs specialist to a code-implementation task
issues.evidence: "index": 0, "text": "`list_for_brand` gains `variant: str | None = None` + ... outer-query-unaliased comment added (`app/core/resources/assets/reads.py:42-116`)", "agent": "backend-docs-specialist" // Claude: "One override: the CLI keyword-matched item 0 to `backend-docs-specialist` (triggered by \"comment added\"), but it's a code-implementation task. I'll dispatch all three with `python-implementer` and note it."
issues.what_happened: Wave 1's first checklist item — a SQL/service code change — was deterministically routed to backend-docs-specialist because the item text contained the phrase 'comment added'. The conductor caught it and manually overrode to python-implementer; a less careful run would have dispatched a docs persona to write production SQL.
issues.root_cause_guess: The build verb's agent resolution uses naive keyword matching over checklist text against specialist capability keywords ('comment'/'doc' → docs), with no precedence for the file-path/code-shaped signal or honor for plan-time '— via' tags on that item.

issues.title: Reconcile gate conflates pre-existing drift with this session's drift and demands it all be reconciled now
issues.evidence: drifted features:     assets-resources, docs-app-bootstrap, llm-uploads, mcp-server, workspace-org-core // Claude: "Reconcile reports drift in `assets-resources` + `mcp-server` (the features I actually touched) plus three I didn't (`docs-app-bootstrap`, `llm-uploads`, `workspace-org-core` — pre-existing drift from before this build)" // Gate: "for each drifted feature below, re-run its council enrichment"
issues.what_happened: The Stop gate listed all five drifted features and instructed enrichment for each 'before this session ends', though three drifted before this session. Claude had to manually scope the recouncil to the two features the build touched and leave the rest, contradicting the gate's literal instruction.
issues.root_cause_guess: The anchor (Model B) records one global commit, so reconcile can't attribute drift per-session; the gate message doesn't acknowledge pre-existing drift or permit scoping.

issues.title: Gate offers no honest exit between full LLM council and --force; user dragged into the tradeoff mid-close
issues.evidence: AskUserQuestion: "The reconcile gate keeps firing for the curated enrichment of the features this build changed. `recouncil` is the full multi-agent council pass (not a quick CLI step). How should I settle it?" options: "Force-stamp, defer enrichment" / "Run recouncil now"
issues.what_happened: After the build, Claude did the deterministic reconcile (assign-files + rebuild --changed) and deliberately declined to stamp, but the Stop gate re-blocked. With recouncil being a heavy multi-agent pass and --force being a documented lie-to-the-anchor, Claude escalated the decision to the user. The user picked the expensive recouncil — which then STILL ended in a forced stamp because of the equipment files (issue #1).
issues.root_cause_guess: Gate state machine recognizes only fully-reconciled or force; no 'deterministic-reconciled, enrichment deferred' acknowledgement state.

issues.title: Force-stamp anchors to HEAD while built code is uncommitted, guaranteeing a false drift report next session
issues.evidence: WARNING: uncommitted source changes remain outside .context/. Since the anchor is now HEAD, that source will re-surface as drift next reconcile — commit it to settle. // Claude: "the build's **production code remains uncommitted** in the working tree, exactly as the \"no commit\" choice intended. The anchor advanced to that HEAD; the uncommitted code re-surfaces as drift next session until committed (the accepted caveat)."
issues.what_happened: The user chose 'Reconcile now, no commit'. The features were re-enriched against the working tree, but reconcile-stamp can only anchor to a commit; meanwhile the user had committed only the proposal+equipment files between turns (db2dda9). Result: the index is now enriched for code the anchor says doesn't exist yet — next session's gate will re-report already-reconciled work as drift.
issues.root_cause_guess: Commit-anchored (Model B) semantics have no representation for 'reconciled against working tree at tree-hash X'; stamping is all-or-nothing against HEAD.

issues.title: council-batch output leaks Python enum repr 'SubagentType.AI' as the agent name
issues.evidence:
council-batch: stage 1 — 2 parallel units (dispatch concurrently, barrier, then re-run --next):
  utils-test-conftest: dev → SubagentType.AI [AI]
  utils-typing: dev → SubagentType.AI [AI]
issues.what_happened: The dispatch plan printed `SubagentType.AI` instead of a resolvable subagent type string (e.g. 'AI Engineer'). Had Claude used council-batch as the driver, it would have had to guess the real Task subagent_type from an enum repr.
issues.root_cause_guess: str(enum_member) used in the formatter instead of .value / the resolved per-feature dev-pick name.

issues.title: Write to CLI-scaffolded plan.md rejected: 'File has not been read yet'
issues.evidence: <tool_use_error>File has not been read yet. Read it first before writing to it.</tool_use_error>
issues.what_happened: The plan skill says 'Flesh out plan.md' after `context propose` scaffolds it. Claude Wrote spec.md fine (it had Read it) but the first Write to plan.md failed because the harness requires reading scaffolded files first, costing a retry round-trip. One occurrence.
issues.root_cause_guess: SKILL.md doesn't tell the orchestrator to Read each scaffolded file before overwriting it; the CLI-created files are unknown to the harness's read-before-write tracker.

issues.title: Plan drafted wrong facts about existing code despite .context grounding; only the critique panel caught them
issues.evidence: Critic: "Several findings turn on facts the plan got wrong about the existing code ... `list_for_brand` (reads.py:42-48) currently has signature `brand_id, kind, requesting_user_id` — **no `*args`/`conn` param, and it acquires its own connection internally**. ... the byte-identical claim hinges on reproducing that exact whitespace."
issues.what_happened: The spec/plan, written while navigating .context features/conventions/symbols, asserted incorrect signatures and SQL-construction details for the code it planned to modify. The testability critic (reading raw source) corrected them and the plan was revised. The safety net worked, but the index-first drafting produced wrong load-bearing facts — suggesting symbols.json/feature docs lack signature-level fidelity or the skill under-emphasizes verifying against raw source before drafting.
issues.root_cause_guess: The deterministic map (symbols.json) records names/locations but not signatures/string-literal details, and the plan skill encourages drafting from the index without a verify-against-source step.

issues.title: Build wave dispatch broke an existing test the plan never scheduled for update
issues.evidence:
FAILED tests/core/mcp/test_tools_assets.py::test_get_brand_assets_variant_narrows_logo_results
1 failed, 20 passed // Claude: "This is the **existing** MCP test that asserted the old Python post-filter behavior; by moving the filter into SQL (item 4), the mock-based test no longer narrows. ... the test encodes removed behavior."
issues.what_happened: Wave 2's MCP item deleted the Python post-filter per spec, but neither the consistency scan nor the wave checklist included updating the existing mock-based test that encoded the removed behavior. The conductor discovered it at verify time and fixed the test outside any checklist item. One occurrence; recovered in-session.
issues.root_cause_guess: context propose's deterministic consistency scan matches features/conventions but doesn't surface existing tests asserting behavior a task removes, so the checklist had no test-maintenance item for it.

summary: Session in BOS-Mono/backend ran /dummyindex-plan then /dummyindex-build for a 'brand-hub illustrations' backend feature, closing with a scoped recouncil of two features. The happy path largely worked (proposal authored, 14-item checklist executed wave-by-wave with parallel specialists, 242 tests green, features re-enriched), but the close-the-loop machinery generated heavy friction: equip-generated agent files tripped reconcile-stamp into two forced --force stamps and a planning-only-session Stop-gate block; the plan skill's hardcoded critique-panel agent types failed to dispatch; council-batch couldn't be scoped and subcommand --help is unsupported, forcing manual council orchestration; and build's keyword routing assigned a docs specialist to a code task. No plugin discover/install was attempted this session, so the plugin-manager-specific struggles here are all equip-output ↔ reconcile integration rather than marketplace wiring. No explicit user frustration; the user was twice pulled into reconcile-gate tradeoff decisions the tooling created.

issues.title: Bare `dummyindex install` (run to probe usage) executed a full destructive install and re-clustered curated .context/ features into community-N
issues.evidence: ⚠️ Important: my `uv run dummyindex install` (which I expected to just print usage) actually **executed a full install** — it installed the skill to user scope, and critically it **rebuilt `.context/` (332 files, 189 indexed)**. ... Confirmed — my `install` invocation triggered a destructive re-cluster: it created `community-0` … `community-18` and rewrote `features/INDEX.json` to point at them, exactly the failure mode in memory ([[full-rebuild-destroys-curated-features]]).
issues.what_happened: While trying to discover install flags (because `--help` is rejected, see separate issue), Claude ran `uv run dummyindex install` with no args expecting a usage printout. Instead it performed a full user-scope install AND a full .context/ rebuild of the cwd repo, creating community-0..community-18 feature dirs and overwriting features/INDEX.json plus 16 tracked backbone files — destroying the curated taxonomy. Recovery was only possible because the git tree happened to be clean: `git checkout -- .context && git clean -fd .context`. Claude logged this as a memory lesson at session end.
issues.root_cause_guess: `install` has destructive defaults: with no args it silently targets cwd, installs to user scope, and runs a full ingest/re-cluster of .context/ with no confirmation, no dry-run, and no protection for an existing curated index. Combined with no `--help` support, probing the command IS running it.

issues.title: CLI subcommands reject --help and use terse mandatory-flag errors, forcing risky no-arg probing
issues.evidence:
=== install --help ===
error: unknown install argument '--help'
[...] === rebuild --help full ===
error: unknown argument(s) for `rebuild`: ['--help']
[...] error: council-batch requires --next
issues.what_happened: Three separate occurrences: `dummyindex install --help`, `dummyindex context rebuild --help`, and bare `dummyindex context council-batch` all errored instead of printing help. To learn install's flags Claude had to run the bare command — which triggered the destructive full install (critical issue above). Help text was otherwise only available via the top-level command list, whose long descriptions get truncated in grep/head pipelines.
issues.root_cause_guess: Hand-rolled argument parsing without per-subcommand -h/--help handling; `council-batch` makes `--next` mandatory rather than defaulting to it.

issues.title: `rebuild --changed` never adds NEW files to the backbone, and scaffold-feature/assign-files don't register them either — required hand-editing .context/cache/manifest.json
issues.evidence:
context rebuild: no source files changed; .context/ unchanged.
[...] `rebuild --changed` re-extracts *modified known* files but doesn't **add** new ones — the new files are absent from the backbone. [...workaround:] cp .context/cache/manifest.json /tmp/dummyindex_manifest_backup.json ... 'files': {k:v for k,v in m['files'].items() if k not in drop} ... manifest files: 191 -> 184
=== rebuild --changed === AST extraction: 189/189 files (100%)
issues.what_happened: During reconcile, 7 genuinely new source files (guideline_roles package, conversion.py, new tests) were reported as unassigned_new_files but no documented CLI path could get them AST-extracted into map/files.json: `rebuild --changed` said "no source files changed", and after `scaffold-feature`/`assign-files` the files were still OUT of the backbone (verified twice). Claude's workaround was to delete the 7 entries from the internal cache file .context/cache/manifest.json by hand so the next `rebuild --changed` would treat them as changed and extract them. It worked, but it's unsanctioned surgery on internal state that any user-facing flow would get wrong.
issues.root_cause_guess: Change detection is manifest-hash based and only diffs files already present in the manifest; new-file ingestion is only implemented in the full (destructive) rebuild path. Placement verbs update feature.json membership but never touch the AST backbone or manifest.

issues.title: council-batch frontier is council-log-completeness-based, so drifted features with complete logs are never re-enriched and there is no force/recouncil flag
issues.evidence: The batch returned `utils-*` features (never-enriched) plus `guideline-roles` — but **not** the 6 drifted features. The council-batch frontier keys off council-log *completeness*, and the drifted features already have complete logs, so they're considered done despite the content drift. I need the `--recouncil`/force path to re-enrich drifted features. [...] `council-batch` has no force flag — it's purely log-driven.
issues.what_happened: The whole point of the reconcile was to re-enrich 6 features whose source had drifted, but `council-batch --next` refused to surface them (complete logs = done). With no force/reset flag, Claude abandoned the CLI's batch loop entirely: it recomputed each feature's aggregate source hash in raw python, compared against stored `.hash` files to determine real staleness (which contradicted the batch's verdict — "Decisive result — and it overturns the mtime/batch signal: recomputing **content** hashes shows the 6 git-drifted features are genuinely **STALE**"), then hand-dispatched all 24 council agents (3 stages x 8 features) outside the sanctioned mechanism.
issues.root_cause_guess: council-batch only implements the resume/first-run frontier; hash-invalidation of completed features was deferred to 'the orchestrator' but no CLI verb exists to reset or force a feature's council state, so the resumption design contradicts the reconcile design.

issues.title: Reconcile delta is polluted by dummyindex's own .claude/ skill files and .context/ byproducts; the only exit is reconcile-stamp --force which warns the noise will recur forever
issues.evidence:
context reconcile-stamp: anchor advanced to 44314747f612
  WARNING: --force advanced the anchor past un-reconciled work, but did NOT resolve it (markers and untracked files persist). It WILL be re-reported next reconcile until you place/enrich it or stop tracking it:
  unassigned new files: .claude/skills/dummyindex-audit/SKILL.md, ... .claude/skills/dummyindex-equip/templates/db-specialist-agent.md.tmpl, ... .claude/skills/dummyindex/council/65-reconcile.md, supabase/migrations/20260610170000_asset_versions_feed_idx.sql, ...
issues.what_happened: With scope: "repo" in .context/config.json, the reconcile delta listed 22 unassigned files that are dummyindex's OWN tooling (19 .claude/skills/** files it had just installed, .context/_enrich_plan.json) plus supabase SQL migrations no Python feature can own. There is no ignore/"stop tracking" verb, so Claude had to stamp with --force, and the CLI itself warns the same 22 files will be re-reported on every future reconcile. Tooling noise also drowned the 7 real new source files in the delta output (recurred in every reconcile --json call, 4+ times).
issues.root_cause_guess: repo-scope indexing has no built-in exclusion for .claude/, .context/ byproducts, or non-AST file types, and reconcile offers no mechanism to mark files as intentionally unowned — the upgrade itself (which writes ~20 new skill files) guarantees drift noise after every version bump.

issues.title: Installed skill (0.19.2) silently lagged the upgraded CLI (0.22), and SKILL.md's own remedy points at the wrong scope and the destructive command
issues.evidence: > **Installed from dummyindex `0.19.2`.** Run `dummyindex --version` to confirm the CLI matches. If they diverge, re-run `dummyindex install --scope user`. [...later:] Skills are installed at **project scope** (`.claude/skills/`), not user scope — there's no `~/.claude/skills/dummyindex`.
issues.what_happened: The user upgraded the CLI to 0.22 but the repo's skill files stayed at 0.19.2 with no automatic sync. The SKILL.md banner's documented fix (`dummyindex install --scope user`) was doubly wrong for this repo: skills live at project scope here, and following the bare-install advice is exactly what triggered the destructive rebuild. The safe invocation Claude eventually found (`install --scope project --dir . --skill-only`) is documented nowhere in the banner.
issues.root_cause_guess: Version-skew remedy text is hardcoded to user scope and predates the --skill-only flag; no install-time record of which scope a repo uses, and no non-destructive 'sync skill files' default.

issues.title: `dummyindex` not on PATH — bare invocations fail in repos where it is only a venv dev dependency
issues.evidence: === status === /bin/bash: line 1: dummyindex: command not found
issues.what_happened: Both `which dummyindex` and `dummyindex context preflight .` failed (2 occurrences) because the CLI is a uv dev dependency resolved only via `uv run dummyindex`. SKILL.md and council docs all say bare `dummyindex ...`, so Claude burned turns rediscovering the invocation (and later had to inject 'NO bare dummyindex' warnings into all 24 subagent prompts). The Stop hook guards with `command -v dummyindex >/dev/null 2>&1 || exit 0`, so in such repos the memory-nudge hook can silently no-op depending on hook PATH.
issues.root_cause_guess: Install supports multiple CLI delivery modes (uv tool, venv dep) but the generated skill docs and hooks assume a global PATH binary; no wrapper/launcher detection is recorded per-repo.

issues.title: Placement verbs corrupt council bookkeeping: assign-files doesn't refresh .hash, and unassign-files re-drops the pending-enrichment marker of an already-enriched feature
issues.evidence: And `assign-files` did **not** refresh `assets-resources/.hash` (still 06-07), yet its `spec.md` doesn't mention the version-feed I just added — so it's genuinely stale. [...later:] `awaiting_enrichment: ['workspace-org-core']` — my earlier dead-file prune re-dropped its marker (per the reconcile spec). It *was* enriched, so mark it.
issues.what_happened: Two state-invariant breaks in one session: (1) adding files to assets-resources left its stale .hash in place, so the hash skip-signal claimed the feature was current when it wasn't; (2) pruning two dead files from workspace-org-core AFTER its enrichment re-set the awaiting_enrichment marker, requiring a manual `mark-enriched` just before stamping. Both required Claude to notice and repair by hand; either one missed would have left the index silently wrong.
issues.root_cause_guess: assign-files/unassign-files mutate membership without recomputing or invalidating the feature's aggregate hash and unconditionally set the pending-enrichment marker, ignoring whether enrichment already happened at current content.

issues.title: No CLI verb to write the .hash skip-signal — orchestrator had to reimplement dummyindex's hashing algorithm in raw python
issues.evidence: Now write the `.hash` skip-signal for each (my algorithm is byte-identical to dummyindex's — it matched the MATCH cases exactly earlier), set `guideline-roles` confidence to INFERRED, and `mark-enriched` the two placed features.
issues.what_happened: After enriching 8 features, the council-skip signal (.context/features/<id>/.hash) had to be updated so future runs skip them — but no CLI command writes it. Claude wrote all 8 hashes via an inline python sha256-aggregate reimplementation, validated only by the fact that it happened to match existing MATCH cases. It also flipped guideline-roles' confidence field to INFERRED by editing feature.json directly.
issues.root_cause_guess: The .hash mechanism is read by the resumption logic but its write side was only ever implemented inside the council pipeline internals; reconcile docs tell the orchestrator to 'write .hash' without providing a verb, so any algorithm drift between versions silently breaks staleness detection.

issues.title: section-write '--section security' fallback instruction caused stray security.md sibling files in half the critique agents
issues.evidence: Security critique complete. `## Security` written to `concerns.md` (via `--section concerns`, which worked; `--section security` also wrote a sibling `security.md`); raw findings snapshotted to `council/10-critiques.md`. [...orchestrator cleanup:] === remove stray security.md siblings (canonical is concerns.md) ===
issues.what_happened: The stage-3 critique prompts (derived from the council docs) told agents to use `--section concerns` with `--section security` as a fallback. At least 3–4 of the 8 security-critic agents executed the fallback even though the primary path succeeded, and the CLI happily created non-canonical `security.md` files next to concerns.md. The orchestrator had to sweep all 8 feature dirs and delete the strays before verification; one agent also self-reported removing one mid-run.
issues.root_cause_guess: Ambiguous fallback wording in the council/skill docs plus a section-write CLI that accepts arbitrary section names and creates new sibling files instead of erroring or canonicalising to concerns.md.

issues.title: Three mutually contradictory staleness oracles: SessionStart mtime drift report (12 features) vs git-anchored reconcile (6) vs content hashes (8), worsened by an anchor left unstamped by a prior session
issues.evidence: the SessionStart report's 12 was the looser *mtime* signal; the git-anchored 6 is authoritative [...] "No source files changed" — meaning the deterministic backbone was already refreshed previously; only the commit **anchor** lagged (a prior `rebuild --changed` without a `reconcile-stamp`). [...] Decisive result — and it overturns the mtime/batch signal: recomputing **content** hashes shows the 6 git-drifted features are genuinely **STALE**, plus `utils-config-fragment` STALE and `guideline-roles` NO-HASH.
issues.what_happened: The SessionStart hook reported 68 files across 12 drifted features (mtime-based); `reconcile --json` reported 6 (git-anchor-based); .hash mtimes plus council-batch implied 0 of those 6 needed work; only Claude's manual content-hash recomputation established the true set of 8 stale features. Claude flip-flopped twice ('the council already re-ran on them' → 'this overturns the earlier read decisively') before getting it right. A prior session's `rebuild --changed` without `reconcile-stamp` had left the anchor lagging, manufacturing part of the false drift.
issues.root_cause_guess: Each subsystem (hook, reconcile, council resume) uses a different staleness signal with no single authoritative source, and the two-step rebuild/stamp protocol is easy to leave half-done with nothing detecting the desync.

issues.title: Mid-session .claude/settings.json mutation (extraKnownMarketplaces + enabledPlugins) conflated with dummyindex's changes during verification
issues.what_happened: During final verification of dummyindex's .claude/ changes, git showed settings.json modified with a marketplace + plugin enablement that nothing in the session had knowingly written. Claude spent a verification round attributing it (concluding the Claude Code harness added it at session start) and left it uncommitted-but-flagged. Because dummyindex's install also writes into .claude/, any third-party settings.json mutation lands in the same diff and muddies the question of what dummyindex wired.
issues.root_cause_guess: Not a dummyindex bug per se, but dummyindex has no manifest/baseline of exactly which .claude/ paths and settings.json keys it owns, so its changes can't be cleanly distinguished from harness/plugin writes at review time.

summary: User asked Claude to update .context/ and .claude/ after upgrading the dummyindex CLI from 0.19.2 to 0.22 in a repo with new commits. The end state was correct (skill refreshed, 8 features re-enriched, anchor stamped to HEAD), but virtually every step of the documented path failed: a bare `install` probe destructively re-clustered the curated index (recovered only via git), `rebuild --changed` could not ingest new files (fixed by hand-editing the internal manifest cache), `council-batch` refused to surface drifted features (Claude reimplemented the hash algorithm and dispatched 24 agents manually), and the reconcile delta was polluted by dummyindex's own skill files, forcing a `reconcile-stamp --force` that warns the noise will recur every reconcile. No plugin discover/install/wiring was exercised this session — the only settings.json plugin change came from the harness, not dummyindex — so the owner's plugin-manager concerns are not directly evidenced here; the dominant pain is in install defaults, the reconcile/update loop, and CLI ergonomics.

issues.title: build --next-wave keyword matching mis-assigns checklist items to wrong specialists, skills, and hooks
issues.evidence: "index": 2, "text": "`features/brand-hub/lib/illustration-upload.ts` — `uploadIllustration` ...", "agent": "frontend-verify", "subagent_type": "general-purpose", "fallback": false ... "index": 3 ... "agent": "prettier-format", "subagent_type": "general-purpose" ... "index": 4 ... `IllustrationCard.tsx` ... "agent": "frontend-reviewer"  ||  Assistant: "the CLI's keyword matching mis-assigned them ... the CLI mis-matched it to `frontend-reviewer` (a *reviewer*, not an implementer) ... the `general-purpose` fallbacks came from keyword mis-matches to capability specialists."
issues.what_happened: First `dummyindex context build --next-wave --json` call: of 5 Wave-1 items, only 1 was correctly assigned. A design-export prep step was matched to typescript-implementer, two pure TS lib helpers were matched to `frontend-verify` (a generated SKILL) and `prettier-format` (a PostToolUse HOOK) — both un-dispatchable, downgraded to general-purpose with `fallback: false` (misleading metadata) — and a component-implementation item was matched to a reviewer agent. Claude detected the mis-assignments itself and overrode all of them, dispatching typescript-implementer manually. Discussed/repeated at L276 and L316.
issues.root_cause_guess: The wave item→agent matcher is naive keyword matching over the whole equipment manifest (including skills and hooks, not just Task-dispatchable agents), with no role awareness (implementer vs reviewer vs verifier) and a `fallback` flag that doesn't reflect the general-purpose downgrade.

issues.title: Build loop cannot execute plugin-routed/interactive checklist items (canvas-to-code, impeccable); waves block and Claude must hand-orchestrate
issues.evidence: "Waves **5–6** are interactive skills (`/canvas-to-code:start`, `/impeccable …`) that `/dummyindex-build` can't drive — it dispatches Task agents, which can't invoke those plugins the way you can. ... `/dummyindex-build` works the checklist top-to-bottom and will try to dispatch the Wave 5–6 design-tooling items to general agents — when it reaches those, stop it and run the `/canvas-to-code` and `/impeccable` skills yourself."  ...  "Wave 1 can't complete — and Wave 2 can't start — until these resolve, and they're the precise A-vs-B fork."
issues.what_happened: The plan deliberately routed visual surfaces through installed plugins (canvas-to-code bridge + impeccable polish), but the build CLI has no representation of plugin-routed tasks: it assigned them to coding agents (item 0 'Export BCO-0 ... (canvas-to-code input)' → typescript-implementer). Wave 1 mixed plugin tasks with codeable tasks, so the strict wave-ordering invariant broke — the agent built 3 of 5 items, stopped, asked the user (AskUserQuestion) which path to take, then ran /canvas-to-code:start and /impeccable manually outside the build loop, ticking boxes after the fact. Recurred at the Wave 5–6 boundary too.
issues.root_cause_guess: The '— via <tool>' plugin annotation (shipped same-day in v0.24+) isn't in the installed 0.22.0 skill / 0.23.0 CLI, and the plan skill groups plugin-driven items into the same waves as agent-codeable items; build CLI has no 'interactive/plugin' item type, so it keyword-matches them to agents.

issues.title: reconcile-stamp refuses on intentionally-unowned files; only escape is --force with a warning promising perpetual re-reporting
issues.evidence:
context reconcile-stamp: REFUSED — un-reconciled work remains (advancing the anchor would silently forget it):
  unassigned new files: .claude-design/brand-hub/illustrations/.../jsx/illustrations-recommended-step5.tsx, ... source-meta.yaml, .claude/agents/frontend-performance-specialist.md
... re-run with --force to anchor anyway.  ||  WARNING: --force advanced the anchor past un-reconciled work, but did NOT resolve it (markers and untracked files persist). It WILL be re-reported next reconcile until you place/enrich it or stop tracking it
issues.what_happened: After committing the feature and assigning all real source/test files to features, the only remaining 'unassigned new files' were design-export artifacts (.claude-design/**) and an equip-generated agent — files that legitimately belong to no feature. There is no 'mark as intentionally unowned / ignore' verb, so the agent had to use --force, which emitted a warning that the same files will keep being re-reported. The agent rationalized this as 'the established pattern', i.e. it's a known recurring annoyance.
issues.root_cause_guess: The reconcile model assumes every new tracked file must be owned by a feature; there is no ignore-list/unowned classification for tooling and design artifacts, forcing routine --force usage that erodes the gate's meaning.

issues.title: dummyindex equip's own generated agent file trips dummyindex's own reconcile gate
issues.evidence:
equip: ... write   frontend-performance-specialist  ->  .claude/agents/frontend-performance-specialist.md ... wrote 7 item(s)   ||   context reconcile: anchor 92c96bb510da
  unassigned new files: ... .claude/agents/frontend-performance-specialist.md
issues.what_happened: `dummyindex context equip apply --for-proposal` wrote .claude/agents/frontend-performance-specialist.md; later, `dummyindex context reconcile` listed that very file as un-reconciled work, contributing to the reconcile-stamp REFUSED and the need for --force. The tool's own output is counted as drift it cannot classify. (Also note: equip printed only 5 'write' lines but reported 'wrote 7 item(s)' — inconsistent count reporting.)
issues.root_cause_guess: Reconcile's new-file scan doesn't exclude dummyindex/equip-generated paths (.claude/agents, .claude/skills, .claude-design) even though equipment.json records exactly which files equip wrote.

issues.title: .pending-enrichment markers persist after genuine enrichment; agent resorted to rm -f on internal state files
issues.evidence:
?? .context/features/brand-hub/.pending-enrichment
?? .context/features/tests/.pending-enrichment  ||  Assistant: "Since I've genuinely completed the enrichment ... those markers no longer reflect reality. I'll clear them and commit the reconcile:" → `rm -f .context/features/brand-hub/.pending-enrichment .context/features/tests/.pending-enrichment` → "context reconcile: ... in sync — nothing to reconcile."
issues.what_happened: After assign-files, reconcile flagged 'awaiting enrichment: brand-hub, tests'. The agent enriched both feature specs by hand, but the markers (and the 'awaiting enrichment' report, even post --force stamp) persisted. No CLI verb to declare enrichment complete was discovered (a `mark-enriched` verb exists in newer source but never surfaced — the agent's --help grep for assign/stamp/reconcile/scaffold wouldn't match it), so the agent deleted the marker files directly with rm -f, bypassing the safety mechanism.
issues.root_cause_guess: mark-enriched is undiscoverable (not suggested in reconcile output, not greppable next to the other reconcile verbs, no per-subcommand --help), so the documented place→enrich→stamp loop dead-ends and agents mutate .context state files manually.

issues.title: CLI subcommands reject --help with an error instead of printing usage
issues.evidence:
error: unknown argument(s) for `assign-files`: ['--help']
=== ALSO check reconcile-stamp help ===
error: unknown argument(s) for `reconcile-stamp`: ['--help']
issues.what_happened: When the agent needed assign-files/reconcile-stamp usage, `--help` on the subcommand errored. It had to grep the monolithic `dummyindex context --help` (whose grepped output was wrapped/garbled fragments like 'concerns.md. Already-assigned files are') and then run `assign-files` bare to extract the usage from its error message ('error: --feature <feature_id> and at least one --file <path> are required').
issues.root_cause_guess: Hand-rolled argument parsing that treats --help as an unknown positional on subcommands; help text only exists at the `context` group level with line-wrapped descriptions that don't survive grep.

issues.title: reconcile/rebuild output repeatedly prescribes '/dummyindex --recouncil', contradicting the commit-anchored reconcile-stamp workflow the skills teach
issues.evidence:
context reconcile: anchor 92c96bb510da
  ... drifted features:     tests
  Run the council reconcile procedure (`/dummyindex --recouncil`).  ||  context rebuild: enriched index preserved; refreshed 7 deterministic artefact(s) (no re-cluster).
  drifted features: brand-hub
  enriched index preserved; run `/dummyindex --recouncil` to reconcile enrichment for the drift above.
issues.what_happened: Five separate CLI outputs (after the feature commit, after assign-files, after --force stamp, after the polish commit, and after rebuild --changed) all told the agent to run the full council via '/dummyindex --recouncil'. The agent ignored the instruction every time and instead followed the skill-documented reconcile → assign/enrich → reconcile-stamp loop, including stamping past a 'drifted features: brand-hub' state after a style-only commit by judgment call. Dual, conflicting guidance forces the agent to decide which docs to trust.
issues.root_cause_guess: CLI hint strings predate the commit-anchored reconcile/reconcile-stamp flow (v0.15.3+) and were never updated to point at the lighter procedure; drift messaging also doesn't distinguish style-only drift from contract drift.

issues.title: Plan critique panel dispatched nonexistent subagent type 'Code Reviewer'
issues.evidence: Agent type 'Code Reviewer' not found. Available agents: Accessibility Auditor, ... Backend Architect, canvas-to-code:canvas-to-code-auditor, ... frontend-reviewer, general-purpose, ... typescript-implementer, typescript-tester, ...
issues.what_happened: During /dummyindex-plan's lightweight critique panel, the agent dispatched three critics in parallel; the risk critic used subagent_type 'Code Reviewer', which doesn't exist in the installed agent roster (the other two guesses, 'Backend Architect' and 'Test Results Analyzer', happened to exist). The dispatch failed and had to be re-run with general-purpose ('the skill says fall back to general-purpose'), costing a round-trip.
issues.root_cause_guess: The dummyindex-plan SKILL.md names critic personas without instructing the model to validate against the actual installed agent list first; equipped-repo specialists (frontend-reviewer) weren't suggested for the critic roles either.

issues.title: Installed skill family (0.22.0) diverges from CLI (0.23.0); divergence detected but never remediated
issues.evidence: Skill header: "> **Installed from dummyindex `0.22.0`.** Run `dummyindex --version` to confirm the CLI matches. If they diverge, re-run `dummyindex install --scope user`."  ||  `dummyindex --version` output: "0.23.0"
issues.what_happened: All three dummyindex skills used in the session (plan, build) self-identify as installed from 0.22.0 while the CLI reported 0.23.0. The skill's own instruction says to re-run `dummyindex install --scope user` on divergence, but the agent ran the version check, saw 0.23.0, and proceeded without ever flagging or fixing the skew — so the session drove a 0.23.0 CLI with 0.22.0-era skill instructions (plausibly explaining the missing '— via <tool>' handling and stale reconcile guidance).
issues.root_cause_guess: The version-check instruction is passive prose the model skims past; nothing in the CLI (e.g., a version handshake in --json output or SessionStart hook) enforces or even reports skill/CLI version mismatch.

issues.title: Wired-in impeccable plugin's mandated setup script fails from project cwd (MODULE_NOT_FOUND)
issues.evidence:
Error: Cannot find module '/mnt/windows-ssd/Projects/opensession/BOS-Mono/frontend/.claude/skills/impeccable/scripts/context.mjs'
... code: 'MODULE_NOT_FOUND'  ||  Skill setup text: "1. Run `node .claude/skills/impeccable/scripts/context.mjs` once per session."
issues.what_happened: The dummyindex plan/checklist routes Wave 6 through the impeccable plugin (recorded in the generated conventions/design-tooling.md). When the agent invoked the skill, its mandatory setup command assumes a project-local `.claude/skills/impeccable/` install, but the plugin actually lives in ~/.claude/plugins/cache/impeccable/impeccable/3.5.0/. The literal command failed; the agent improvised by resolving the cache path from the skill's 'Base directory' line. The wiring (plan annotation + convention doc) never validated the plugin's invocation contract against where it is actually installed.
issues.root_cause_guess: Plugin wiring records the plugin as 'available' without smoke-testing its setup commands or normalizing project-local vs plugin-cache install locations; the plugin's relative-path assumption goes unchecked at wire time.

issues.title: impeccable wired into the plan without its prerequisites; agent bypassed the plugin's mandated NO_PRODUCT_MD init flow
issues.evidence: NO_PRODUCT_MD: This project has no PRODUCT.md yet. Stop the current task, load reference/init.md, and follow its instructions to write PRODUCT.md before resuming.  ||  Assistant: "Rather than a full interview-driven init (the user's away), I'll synthesize the anchor from existing context." ... "I'll proceed without creating impeccable's scaffolding files"
issues.what_happened: Once the setup script ran (from the cache path), it hard-blocked on a missing PRODUCT.md. The plan/equip step had annotated checklist items as impeccable-driven without ever checking or provisioning the plugin's project prerequisites. The agent then explicitly violated the plugin's contract — skipping init and synthesizing context from .context/ docs — so the 'polish' items were ticked via a freehand typescript-implementer pass rather than the plugin's actual workflow.
issues.root_cause_guess: dummyindex's plugin wiring/annotation treats an installed plugin as ready-to-use; there is no prerequisite/init probe at equip or plan time, so the first real use lands on an interactive blocker and the model routes around the plugin instead of through it.

issues.title: /dummyindex-build invoked three times in a row with three different argument shapes before execution started
issues.evidence: L255: <command-name>/dummyindex-build</command-name>  (no args) → L259: <command-args>.context/proposals/brand-hub-illustrations</command-args> → L267: <command-args>brand-hub-illustrations</command-args>
issues.what_happened: The user issued /dummyindex-build with no args, then with the proposal directory path, then with the bare slug; only the third invocation produced an assistant run (no assistant output exists between the first two). Whether the earlier attempts were user-cancelled or stalled, the retries suggest the expected argument form (slug vs path vs nothing) isn't obvious, and the skill gave no visible feedback on the first two submissions.
issues.root_cause_guess: The build skill documents '<slug>' loosely ('if they didn't name it, list .context/proposals/ and ask') and doesn't normalize path-style arguments to slugs, leaving the invocation contract ambiguous to users.

summary: The session drove the full dummyindex pipeline on the BOS-Mono frontend: /dummyindex-plan turned a Paper design URL into a brand-hub-illustrations proposal (with critique panel + auto-equip), then /dummyindex-build executed the wave checklist via canvas-to-code, typescript-implementer/tester agents, an impeccable polish pass, and a commit-anchored reconcile, finishing at 26/28 items with three clean commits. The deterministic backbone (propose, equip apply, assign-files, --check ticking, rebuild --changed) mostly worked, but Claude succeeded largely by overriding the tool: the build CLI's keyword matcher assigned skills/hooks/reviewers as implementers, plugin-routed tasks (canvas-to-code, impeccable) were undispatchable and blocked waves, reconcile-stamp had to be --forced past unowned files (including equip's own generated agent) and its .pending-enrichment markers were cleared with rm -f, and the wired-in impeccable plugin failed its own setup contract (wrong path + missing PRODUCT.md). The user showed no overt frustration, but every dummyindex friction point was absorbed by the agent improvising manual workarounds.

issues.title: reconcile-stamp advances anchor with no content validation; false-clean report masked real drift and recovery required hand-editing meta.json
issues.evidence: "The procedure hinges on the `reconcile --json` delta — but my premature stamp moved the anchor to HEAD, so that report now reads tautologically clean (line 32: \"has_drift == false → nothing to do; Stop\")... are the 36 added files actually assigned to features, or did the anchor get advanced past unplaced files?" ... [TOOL_USE Edit] meta.json: "indexed_commit": "39104814..." → "a7be5dbdeb60571909e7c2ca0908bed624f13443"
issues.what_happened: Trusting `reconcile --json` (has_drift: false), Claude ran `reconcile-stamp` and the anchor advanced to HEAD — but ~36 added + 26 modified files from merged PR #13 had never been absorbed (an earlier session had also stamped past #13, so the report was already false-clean). When /dummyindex --recouncil later exposed the real drift via `context check`, the only way to get an honest delta was to manually Edit `.context/meta.json` and roll `indexed_commit` back to the last genuinely-reconciled commit (a7be5db, PR #12). A concurrent process also committed the premature stamp (fba707a 'update indexed_commit and updated_at in meta.json'), adding confusion.
issues.root_cause_guess: reconcile-stamp only refuses when unassigned/awaiting markers exist, and those markers come from the same anchor-based delta the stamp is rewriting — circular validation. Once an anchor is stamped past unreconciled commits, every anchor-based signal reads clean forever. No `--to <commit>`/rollback verb exists, forcing raw JSON editing.

issues.title: Conflicting drift signals produced a tool-backed false 'no drift' all-clear to the user
issues.evidence: Claude (turn ~215): "Confirmed — **no drift, and `.context/` is fully up to speed.** Every signal is clean... has_drift: false — 0 drifted, 0 unassigned, 0 removed, 0 awaiting enrichment" — then later (turn ~255): "context check: drift detected — 36 added, 26 modified, 0 removed  added: - app/core/ai/art_direction.py - app/core/auth/service.py - app/core/integrations/connectivity.py ..."
issues.what_happened: User asked 'confimr there is no drift in .context and make sure eveything up to speed' three times (lines 132/134/210). Claude checked anchor-based reconcile, mtime plan-update, and the gate — all clean — and declared no drift. Only when the user escalated to /dummyindex --recouncil did `context check` (manifest hash comparison) reveal 36 added + 26 modified files and 18 source files unassigned to any feature, all from already-merged PR #13. The earlier all-clear was wrong.
issues.root_cause_guess: dummyindex has 4 independent drift detectors (mtime report, git-anchor reconcile, manifest `context check`, per-feature `.hash`) that disagree by design — Claude's own memory note says 'Don't trust one alone.' There is no single command that cross-checks them, so an agent that consults the 'authoritative' ones reports clean while another signal holds the truth.

issues.title: Stop-hook reconcile gate blocks session exit demanding a 14-feature recouncil after a git-only session that changed no code
issues.evidence: Stop hook: "dummyindex reconcile gate: `.context/` is stale after a substantial session... re-run its council enrichment (`/dummyindex --recouncil <feature>`)... Do NOT skip silently... Drifted features: ai-chat-streaming, api-routers, app-infra-tools, art-direction, ... workspace-org-core." Claude: "**this drift was not caused by this session** — I only did git operations (rebase, branch cleanup, syncing development)."
issues.what_happened: After a pure git housekeeping session (rebase, branch deletion, fast-forward), the always-on Stop reconcile-gate blocked exit and demanded council enrichment of 14 features whose 'drift' came from previously merged PRs (and was largely mtime noise from the git operations themselves). Claude had to argue with the hook across multiple turns and ask the user how to proceed instead of just finishing.
issues.root_cause_guess: The gate keys on 'substantial session' (output tokens) + repo-wide mtime staleness, with no check of whether the session actually edited source files, so any long session in a repo with pre-existing drift gets gated.

issues.title: mtime-based drift heuristic mass false-positives after git operations; cleared only by touch-ing every .context doc
issues.evidence: "Both files share the **exact same mtime** (`19:48:24`) — `git pull` wrote source and `.context` docs in the same instant, and the heuristic flags equal-or-newer source as 'drift.'" Workaround executed: "find .context/features -type f -exec touch {} +\nfind .context/conventions -type f -exec touch {} +" — SessionStart reports claimed "51 source files across 13 features" then "63 source files across 14 features" drifted while git status was clean.
issues.what_happened: SessionStart drift reports flagged 51→63 files across 13-14 features as edited-since-docs, though content was identical to the index (PRs had reconciled in-branch). git pull/rebase/checkout rewrote working-tree mtimes, and the heuristic counts equal-or-newer source as drift. The only way to clear it was a bulk `touch` of all feature/convention docs — an improvised hack the report's own wording effectively sanctions. Recurred at every SessionStart/Stop in the session.
issues.root_cause_guess: Drift heuristic compares raw file mtimes with >= semantics; git checkouts give source and docs identical timestamps, making every pull/rebase look like repo-wide drift. Needs content hashes or git-aware comparison instead of mtime.

issues.title: reconcile-gate keeps blocking when every content signal is clean, and violates its own documented block-ONCE behavior
issues.evidence: "Confirmed: the gate still blocks **even though the commit-anchored reconcile is fully clean** (anchor now at HEAD, `has_drift: false`). This proves the gate is driven purely by the **mtime heuristic**, not by content." CLI help: "Prints a `decision: block` payload — blocking the session's exit ONCE... Silent (allows stop)... on the re-entrant stop" — yet hook_blocking_error fired at 14:19:00.527Z and again at 14:19:36.692Z with the identical reason.
issues.what_happened: After Claude advanced the anchor to HEAD and `reconcile --json` reported fully clean, the gate still returned decision:block (mtime-driven). Separately, the gate blocked two consecutive Stop attempts ~36 seconds apart even though its help text promises it blocks once and is silent on the re-entrant stop. The session could only stop after the mass-touch workaround.
issues.root_cause_guess: Gate's staleness predicate uses the mtime report, not the commit-anchored reconcile it tells the agent to run — so completing the prescribed procedure doesn't clear the gate. The block-once/re-entrant detection (likely a per-stop marker) failed or resets when the agent emits a new message between stops.

issues.title: rebuild --changed won't ADD new files to the backbone; gitignored manifest gates it; the workaround lives only in a hand-written session memory note
issues.evidence: Memory note read mid-procedure: "**`rebuild --changed` re-extracts MODIFIED known files but does NOT add NEW files** to the backbone (`map/files.json`, `symbols.json`, `tree.json`)... if a path is in the manifest, `--changed` skips it even when the tracked backbone lacks it. **Fix to force new files into the backbone non-destructively:** drop their entries from `manifest.json`, then `rebuild --changed`... A `git checkout -- .context` can desync the tracked backbone (old) from the gitignored manifest (current)."
issues.what_happened: To execute the reconcile correctly, Claude had to consult its own prior-session memory note ('Hard-won mechanics') documenting that the advertised non-destructive incremental rebuild silently skips new files because the gitignored cache/manifest.json gates --changed, plus a manual manifest-realign trick. In this run the backbone happened to already contain the files (manifest stale at 184 vs 222 actual), but the documented CLI path alone cannot fold new files in.
issues.root_cause_guess: --changed change-detection is driven by a local gitignored cache rather than the committed backbone or git itself, so cache/backbone desync (e.g., after git checkout of .context) makes incremental rebuilds skip exactly the files that need indexing. None of this is in the official skill docs.

issues.title: context check keeps reporting 'drift detected — 36 added, 26 modified' even after a complete, verified reconcile
issues.evidence: Post-reconcile verification: "=== content drift vs manifest (context check) === context check: drift detected — 36 added, 26 modified, 0 removed" — Claude: "**`context check`** reads the **gitignored `cache/manifest.json`** (184 entries, local-only cache), which lags disk... A future `rebuild --changed` after commit resolves it."
issues.what_happened: After the full reconcile (all files placed, 12 features re-enriched, anchor stamped, gate clean), `context check` still reported the same 36 added/26 modified drift it reported before the work, because it compares source against the stale gitignored manifest. The signal that originally exposed the real drift is now permanently crying wolf until some future rebuild — indistinguishable from genuine drift.
issues.root_cause_guess: `context check` hashes against cache/manifest.json, but nothing in the reconcile procedure (assign-files, mark-enriched, refresh-indexes, reconcile-stamp) updates that manifest, so check and reconcile can never converge within one procedure.

issues.title: council-batch --next reports already-complete features as pending, misses content-stale ones, and leaks a Python enum repr in JSON output
issues.evidence: council-batch --next output: '{"complete": false, "stage": 1, ... "feature_id": "utils-test-conftest", "stage": 1, "role": "dev", "subagent_type": "SubagentType.AI", "framework": "AI"}' — Claude: "The two 'incomplete' ones (`utils-test-conftest`, `utils-typing`) are trivial single-file features completed via the **stage-0 consolidation** pass — done, not pending." Memory note: "a source-changed feature with a complete log will NOT appear in the batch".
issues.what_happened: When scoping --recouncil work, council-batch --next surfaced two trivial features that were already finished (their stage-0 consolidation isn't recorded the way the batch reader expects) while showing none of the 12 genuinely content-stale features (it is log-based, not hash-based). Claude had to ignore the batch output and compute the stale set itself. The JSON also serializes subagent_type as the raw enum repr 'SubagentType.AI' instead of a usable agent name.
issues.root_cause_guess: council-batch derives pending work solely from _council-log.json completeness; stage-0 consolidation writes a log shape it misreads, and hash-invalidation is left to the orchestrator. The subagent_type field is str(enum) instead of enum.value.

issues.title: No CLI verb to compute/write per-feature .hash — orchestrator reimplemented the hash algorithm inline; mcp-server's .hash was missing entirely
issues.evidence: Claude wrote .hash files via raw Python: "agg=hashlib.sha256(''.join(sorted(digs)).encode()).hexdigest(); open(f'.context/features/{fid}/.hash','w').write(agg)" for 13 features. Earlier scan: "mcp-server ... .hash=NO" — "`mcp-server` is fully councilled (stages 1-3 complete) but is the lone feature **missing its `.hash` stamp**."
issues.what_happened: The --recouncil cache semantics depend on features/<id>/.hash, but the CLI offers no command to compute or stamp it. After enrichment Claude had to replicate the exact algorithm (sha256 of sorted per-file sha256 hex digests, verified byte-identical in a prior session) in inline Python for all 13 features. The tool itself had also failed to write mcp-server's .hash during its original council run, leaving cache state inconsistent.
issues.root_cause_guess: Hash stamping is done internally during full council runs but was never exposed as a CLI verb for the reconcile/enrichment path, and at least one code path (mcp-server's council) skips writing it.

issues.title: Gate/skill instructions prescribe the wrong procedure for the actual drift; correct execution depended on a prior session's hand-written notes
issues.evidence: Gate message: "for each drifted feature below, re-run its council enrichment (`/dummyindex --recouncil <feature>`); place any new files; then `dummyindex context reconcile-stamp`...
