Drifted features: ... art-direction, ... mcp-server ..." vs Claude's later memory-note-driven finding: "Key mechanics: `rebuild --changed` won't add NEW files without a manifest-realign trick; `reconcile --json` is git-anchor based (so my premature stamp masks the delta); `.claude/**` + `supabase/*.sql` are perpetually-unassigned noise."
issues.what_happened: The gate's drifted-feature list (mtime-based, 14 features) included features that were not content-stale (art-direction, mcp-server) and the real work — placing 18 unassigned PR-#13 files via assign-files and enriching a different 12-feature set — appears nowhere in the gate text. Following the gate text earlier (stamp when reconcile reads clean) is precisely what masked the drift. The successful path came from .claude project memory ('Hard-won mechanics... dummyindex v0.22'), not from SKILL.md/council docs.
issues.root_cause_guess: Hook guidance is generated from the noisy mtime list and assumes the anchor delta is trustworthy; the official docs omit the manifest gating, anchor-masking, and placement-first ordering that real reconciles need.

issues.title: .context/meta.json version stamp stuck at 0.15.0 while CLI and backbone are at 0.24.0
issues.evidence: "=== installed CLI === 0.24.0 === .context stamp === \"dummyindex_version\": \"0.15.0\", \"schema_version\": 1, === pyproject dev dep === \"dummyindex>=0.24\"," — Claude: "the backbone was refreshed under 0.24 (per the #16 commit) but the version stamp in `meta.json` wasn't bumped."
issues.what_happened: Despite a dedicated PR (#16) updating dummyindex to 0.24.0 and refreshing the .context backbone, and multiple rebuild/refresh-indexes runs in this session, meta.json still records dummyindex_version 0.15.0 — making 'which version built this index' unanswerable from the index itself.
issues.root_cause_guess: rebuild --changed / refresh-indexes / install paths update artefacts but never rewrite the dummyindex_version field, which is only set at init/full rebuild.

issues.title: reconcile --json returns has_drift:true while every displayed category is zero (uncommitted removed_files)
issues.evidence: "=== commit-anchored reconcile === has_drift: True | drifted: 0 | unassigned: 0 | awaiting: 0" — Claude: "**`has_drift: True`** is solely from `removed_files` — the **4 flow files** ... These are *uncommitted* `.context` deletions; they clear the moment the `.context` changes are committed."
issues.what_happened: At final verification the reconcile report contradicted itself from the agent's summary view: has_drift true with 0 drifted/0 unassigned/0 awaiting, triggering another investigation round. The flag was driven by deletions of .context's own flow files (pruned as part of the reconcile itself) that can't clear until a git commit — so finishing the reconcile necessarily leaves has_drift true.
issues.root_cause_guess: has_drift ORs in removed_files, and removal detection compares the anchor commit's tree against the working tree, so legitimate in-progress .context cleanups count as drift until committed; .context's own files probably shouldn't feed the drift flag.

issues.title: Deterministic flow detection generated bogus flows (flow-011/012) that a council agent had to prune as misdetected
issues.evidence: reconcile --json removed_files: ".context/features/integrations-oauth/flows/flow-011.json ... flow-012.md" — Claude: "the **4 flow files** (`flow-011`, `flow-012` × json/md) the integrations-oauth agent correctly pruned as misdetected."
issues.what_happened: During re-enrichment, the integrations-oauth council agent found two flows in the curated index that did not correspond to real code paths and deleted all four files. The misdetected flows had been sitting in the index serving wrong answers since the original build.
issues.root_cause_guess: The deterministic flow extractor over-detects entry-point chains; nothing validates generated flows against source until a human/agent enrichment pass happens to look.

issues.title: Council enrichment expects a Context7 MCP server that was not wired/exposed in the session
issues.evidence: "I have the output contract. No Context7 server is exposed, so agents reason single-shot from source (same artifact shape)." and later: "=== confirm Context7 absent (agents reason single-shot) === (no *context7* MCP server in this session)"
issues.what_happened: The dummyindex council docs/agent contract reference Context7-backed doc lookup for enrichment authors, but no Context7 MCP server was available in this repo session, so all 12 enrichment subagents silently degraded to single-shot reasoning from source. No discover/install path was suggested to fill the gap.
issues.root_cause_guess: The skill assumes a globally wired context7 plugin without checking or wiring it per-repo (equip/plugin-manager never ran here), and the degraded mode is silent rather than surfacing a wiring fix.

summary: Session in the BOS-Backend repo (a BOS-Mono submodule): the user asked to rebase a 'chore/dummyindex-0.24' PR onto development and make sure .context was in line; it grew into branch cleanup, three rounds of 'confirm there is no drift', and a full /dummyindex --recouncil reconcile. dummyindex performed poorly on its update loop: four mutually contradictory drift signals led Claude to issue a tool-backed false 'no drift' all-clear, a premature reconcile-stamp masked 36 added + 26 modified unabsorbed files from a merged PR, and recovery required hand-editing meta.json, reimplementing the .hash algorithm in inline Python, and mass-touch-ing .context docs to silence the mtime-based hooks — guidance came mostly from a prior session's hand-written memory note rather than the official docs. The Stop reconcile-gate also blocked a git-only session repeatedly (violating its documented block-once behavior). No plugin discover/install activity occurred in this session; the only plugin-adjacent finding is council agents silently degrading when the expected Context7 MCP server wasn't exposed.

issues.title: Full `context rebuild` re-clusters the feature layer, orphaning all 14 curated council-authored features
issues.evidence: "`dummyindex context rebuild` (full, no `--changed`) regenerates `map/` + `tree.json` (good — picks up new modules) BUT re-runs graph community-detection from scratch, overwriting `features/INDEX.json` with raw `community-N` clusters and dropping the architect's Phase-2 structural regrouping (`_structural-plan.json` is deleted). The prior curated semantic features ... survive only as **orphaned folders on disk + in git** — they're no longer in INDEX.json." ... away_summary: "I recovered the curated `.context` features after a full rebuild scrambled them"
issues.what_happened: After `rebuild --changed` proved blind to a new 18-file module, the user picked "Full /dummyindex pipeline". The full rebuild wrote 295 files, replaced the 14 curated semantic features in INDEX.json with 17 generic community-0..16 clusters, deleted _structural-plan.json, and also modified curated prose (.context/PROJECT.md, architecture/overview.md, conventions/naming.md). Claude spent most of a 44-minute turn (214 messages) on manual recovery: git checkout of INDEX.json, rm -rf community-*, then hand-rebuilding the feature. Claude wrote a memory note titled full-rebuild-destroys-curated-features.
issues.root_cause_guess: Full rebuild unconditionally re-runs community detection and overwrites features/INDEX.json with raw clusters; it has no merge/preserve path for an existing curated feature roster, no pre-rebuild snapshot, and no warning that the feature layer will be reset.

issues.title: `rebuild --changed` is a no-op while an entire new 18-file module is missing from the map (manifest/map desync)
issues.evidence: "context rebuild: no source files changed; .context/ unchanged." ... "The **manifest** (`.context/cache/manifest.json`) already tracks all 158 `.py` files *including* the new `app/core/mcp/*` module — but `map/files.json` / `map/symbols.json` (dated **Jun 8**) contain **zero** `core/mcp` references. That's why `rebuild --changed` is a no-op: it compares source hashes against the manifest, the manifest already matches, so it concludes \"nothing changed\" — but it never notices the *map itself* is missing the entire MCP module."
issues.what_happened: User ran `/dummyindex rebuild --changed` to index the new app/core/mcp/ MCP-server module. The command reported nothing changed even though the module (18 files) had zero entries in map/files.json. The manifest had somehow been updated (Jun 8 06:36) without the map being regenerated (map dated Jun 8 13:37 but missing the module), so the hash comparison passed. This is the exact command CLAUDE.md and the skill recommend for catching the index up, and it cannot.
issues.root_cause_guess: --changed anchors change detection solely on hash-compare against cache/manifest.json and never validates that manifest-tracked files actually exist in map/files.json; some earlier operation updated the manifest without rebuilding the map, leaving a permanently invisible gap.

issues.title: Full rebuild's file discovery is manifest-seeded: silently skips files newer than the prior manifest (3-file extraction gap survives a full rebuild)
issues.evidence: "app/**/*.py with defs>0 but 0 extracted symbols: 3 ... app/core/integrations/credentials.py src_defs=11 in_files.json=False ... tokens.py: ast.parse OK -> dummyindex extraction bug, not bad syntax" ... "it's a **dummyindex `rebuild` bug**: a full rebuild doesn't index files that are newer than the prior manifest (its file discovery is manifest-seeded, not a fresh walk). The committed map was already missing them; my rebuild didn't fix it and `--changed` can't."
issues.what_happened: Even after the destructive FULL rebuild (176 files / 875 symbols), three Python files with real defs (credentials.py 11 defs, tokens.py 1, _token_formatters.py 3) had zero extracted symbols and were absent from files.json. All three parse cleanly with ast.parse and are exactly the three files missing from the pre-rebuild manifest. This later caused two reality-check false positives ("file not found") against accurate docs.
issues.root_cause_guess: rebuild seeds its file walk from the existing cache/manifest.json instead of doing a fresh filesystem walk, so files absent from the prior manifest are never discovered/extracted even on a full rebuild.

issues.title: reality-check produced 100% false-positive 'contradicted' claims: duplicate-basename misresolution, gap files, and non-source files
issues.evidence: "_36 concrete claim(s) ... **31 verified**, **5 contradicted**_ ... - ``brand.py:429`` (plan.md) — file has 409 line(s), claim cites line 429 - ``tools/_token_formatters.py:97`` (plan.md) — file not found - ``enums.py:42`` (plan.md) — file has 12 line(s), claim cites line 42 - ``tokens.py:26`` (plan.md) — file not found - ``pyproject.toml:41`` (concerns.md) — file not found" ... assistant: "all 5 \"contradictions\" are reality-check false positives, zero authoring errors"
issues.what_happened: After councilling the mcp-server feature, `dummyindex context reality-check` flagged 5 contradicted claims; Claude verified every one was a false positive: bare basenames (brand.py, enums.py) resolved to the wrong duplicate-basename file; tokens.py/_token_formatters.py were victims of the extraction-gap bug; pyproject.toml isn't indexed at all. After a citation fix and a re-run it still reported 4 contradicted, again all false positives (recurred twice). The report instructs "The original persona should revise or remove them on the next council pass", which would corrupt accurate docs.
issues.root_cause_guess: reality-check resolves doc citations against the AST map by bare basename (first match wins among duplicates), only indexes Python source so config-file citations fail as 'file not found', and inherits the map's extraction gaps — no confidence scoring or path disambiguation before declaring a claim contradicted.

issues.title: preflight wrongly warns 'not a git repo — writes won't be reversible via git' inside a git submodule
issues.evidence: "## ⚠ Warnings - not a git repo — writes won't be reversible via git. ... git: not a git repo" — assistant: "(The \"not a git repo\" warning is a false-ish positive: `backend/` is a submodule, so its `.git` is a file pointer, not a dir — your changes are still git-tracked.)"
issues.what_happened: `dummyindex context preflight` on BOS-Mono/backend (a git submodule where .git is a gitfile pointer, not a directory) reported the tree as not a git repo and warned writes were irreversible. Git was in fact fully functional — the later disaster recovery depended on `git checkout HEAD -- .context/features/INDEX.json`. A user trusting this warning could have made the wrong recovery decision.
issues.root_cause_guess: Git detection checks for a `.git` directory instead of using `git rev-parse --is-inside-work-tree` (or handling the gitfile pointer that submodules/worktrees use).

issues.title: No status verb: `dummyindex --status` and `dummyindex context status` both nonexistent; Claude had to guess three times
issues.evidence: "error: unknown command '--status'\nUsage: dummyindex <command> [args]" (from `dummyindex --status 2>&1 || dummyindex context status 2>&1 || true`; only `dummyindex context hooks status` eventually worked)
issues.what_happened: After running --refresh, the skill flow led Claude to show drift/hook health. It tried `dummyindex --status`, then `dummyindex context status` — neither exists — before landing on `context hooks status`, which only covers the hook. There is no single command summarizing index health/drift/version, so the orchestrator burns turns guessing.
issues.root_cause_guess: CLI surface lacks a top-level `status` command even though the skill narrative implies status visibility; SKILL.md doesn't enumerate the exact health-check invocations.

issues.title: Drift report, `--refresh`, and `rebuild --changed` measure three different things; the documented remediation doesn't clear the reported drift
issues.evidence: "## Heads-up: live drift not cleared by refresh ... If you want the `.context/` docs brought in sync with the MCP-server changes, the right command is `dummyindex context rebuild --changed` (or a targeted `--recouncil` ...)" — followed next turn by: "context rebuild: no source files changed; .context/ unchanged." Drift report: "_Drift clears naturally: once you edit a feature doc, its mtime updates and these entries drop off._"
issues.what_happened: The SessionStart drift report (mtime-based, doc vs source) flagged 4 drifted features. `--refresh` (refresh-indexes) doesn't touch drift; Claude then recommended `rebuild --changed` as 'the right command' to clear it — but that is manifest-hash based and reported no changes, leaving the drift untouched. At session end the 4 features were still drifted. Three overlapping freshness mechanisms with incompatible semantics confused even the orchestrating skill.
issues.root_cause_guess: Drift (doc-mtime vs source-mtime), refresh-indexes (regenerate derived indexes), and rebuild --changed (source-hash vs manifest) are independent mechanisms with no shared model; the skill docs conflate them, so the recommended command can never resolve the hook's complaint.

issues.title: No CLI verb to add a single feature to a curated index — Claude hand-built feature.json with ad-hoc Python
issues.evidence: "No single community is a clean MCP feature. So \"rename one community\" is dead, and re-clustering everything (full orchestrator) would discard your 14 curated council docs. The right path is the surgical one: **keep the good map update, restore the 14 curated features from git, and add a properly-derived `mcp-server` feature**"
issues.what_happened: To index the new module without losing curated features, the only supported paths were `features-rename` (rename an existing community) or full re-cluster (destructive). Neither worked, so Claude wrote a custom Python heredoc that derived members/entry-points from map/symbols.json and wrote .context/features/mcp-server/feature.json + INDEX.json entries by hand — reverse-engineering the schema from an existing feature folder first. Fragile and unrepeatable.
issues.root_cause_guess: The CLI assumes features only come from clustering; there is no `context feature add --from-path app/core/mcp` style verb for surgically scaffolding a feature on an already-curated index.

issues.title: Community detection fragments a cohesive new package across 6+ clusters, mixing source and tests
issues.evidence: "The clustering is genuinely fragmented — `community-3` has 8 MCP files (but buried among 26 files dominated by `core/ai/*`), `community-6` has 7 (mostly MCP *tests*), the rest scattered 1–2 each. No single community is a clean MCP feature."
issues.what_happened: Even setting aside the destructiveness, the full rebuild's clustering scattered the 18-file app/core/mcp/ package across at least 6 community clusters (community-0/3/6/10/11...), with tests and unrelated core/ai files interleaved, so no cluster could be renamed into the mcp-server feature. This made the rebuild output unusable as a starting point.
issues.root_cause_guess: Graph community detection over the symbol/import graph ignores package/directory boundaries and test-vs-source separation as clustering priors.

issues.title: Skill/design doc claims rebuilds preserve authored markdown, setting wrong expectations before the destructive rebuild
issues.evidence: "dummyindex's design preserves authored markdown across rebuilds (the whole drift-clears-when-you-edit-the-doc model depends on it), so a full rebuild *should* leave those intact and only regenerate the deterministic map + scaffold the new MCP feature — but you're on a branch where you're actively curating `.context/` with staged files, so I'd rather confirm than assume."
issues.what_happened: Operating from the skill's documented contract, Claude told the user a full rebuild should leave curated council docs intact and only scaffold the new feature, then offered 'Full rebuild (backbone only)' / 'Full /dummyindex pipeline' options in AskUserQuestion with no destruction warning. The user chose the full pipeline and the feature layer was reset. The markdown folders technically survived on disk but were orphaned from INDEX.json — functionally lost to navigation/query. Docs promise a guarantee the code doesn't honor.
issues.root_cause_guess: SKILL.md/council docs describe the intended preservation model but the rebuild implementation only preserves files it doesn't manage — it does not carry the curated feature roster (INDEX.json, _structural-plan.json) forward; the doc-vs-behavior gap propagated straight into user-facing advice.

summary: Session (2026-06-09, dummyindex 0.15.0, BOS-Mono/backend submodule) ran two slash commands: /dummyindex --refresh (succeeded, but couldn't clear drift) and /dummyindex rebuild --changed (intended to index a new 18-file app/core/mcp module). The --changed path was a no-op due to a manifest/map desync, the escalation to a full rebuild scrambled the curated feature layer into 17 generic community-N clusters, and Claude spent a 44-minute turn doing manual git-based recovery, hand-building the mcp-server feature.json, then running the council manually — which surfaced further bugs: a manifest-seeded extraction gap (3 files with zero symbols even after full rebuild) and a reality-check whose 5 (then 4) 'contradicted' claims were 100% false positives. No plugin discover/install/wire activity occurred in this session (predates the plugin manager); all struggles are in the context build/update/reconcile layer, plus a wrong 'not a git repo' submodule warning and a nonexistent --status command. The end state was recovered and coherent, but only via improvised manual surgery the tool should have made unnecessary.

issues.title: Proposal shipped with a factually wrong rationale that dummyindex grounding never caught
issues.evidence: My earlier framing — and the **Python port's own module docstring** (`app/core/mcp/tools/brand.py:30`, "the static token tree comes from `design_tokens()`… not the TS Supabase/proxy layer") — implied the TS *MCP* read tokens from Supabase and the port regressed that. **That's wrong.** The TS MCP tools always read the static file; there was never a DB-backed MCP token path.
issues.what_happened: During the /dummyindex-plan run, Claude wrote spec.md/plan.md framing the change as 'restoring' a DB-backed token path the TS app supposedly had (stated explicitly at the investigation stage: 'The TS MCP server read design tokens from Supabase (brand_themes); the Python port replaced that with the static tokens.json'). The 'consistency-checked' propose flow, the .context mcp-server feature docs, and the deterministic scan all failed to flag this; the wrong premise sat in the durable proposal until the USER asked an unrelated follow-up ('how was the main app ... handling brand-theme?'), after which an Explore agent disproved it and Claude had to patch spec.md and add a 'Prior art' correction. Occurred once but the wrong claim persisted across two turns and a written artifact.
issues.root_cause_guess: dummyindex-plan's consistency scan only greps the local .context index — it has no step to verify factual claims inherited from source docstrings or cross-repo history, so the index/proposal happily encodes an inaccurate 'port regression' narrative seeded by a wrong docstring that the original ingest/council also never flagged in concerns.md.

issues.title: Generated checklist violates the skill's own 'flat list' contract (improvised Phase headings + GATE semantics)
issues.evidence: Skill: "| `checklist.md` | You | A flat `- [ ]` list derived from the plan tasks **plus** the spec's Acceptance items. |" ... "**Derive `checklist.md`** — flatten the plan tasks and the spec's Acceptance items into one top-to-bottom `- [ ]` list." — but the written artifact begins: "> Flat, top-to-bottom list ... **GATE:** items 1–2 must be done before any code/schema change.\n\n## Phase 0 — Resolve blockers (GATE)"
issues.what_happened: Claude wrote checklist.md with `## Phase 0`–`## Phase 4` section headings and a prose 'GATE' rule, while claiming in the header comment it is a 'flat, top-to-bottom list'. The 0.15.0 dummyindex-build skill in this repo 'loops the flat checklist top-to-bottom' and has no documented heading or gate semantics; newer builds only parse `## Wave N` headings — `## Phase N` matches neither format, so a later /dummyindex-build run's behavior on this artifact is undefined.
issues.root_cause_guess: SKILL.md gives no machine-checkable schema for checklist.md and the CLI never validates it, so the model freely invents structure (phases, gates) that downstream wave/serial parsers don't recognize.

issues.title: Durable checklist closes the loop with rebuild-only, never reconcile/stamp
issues.evidence: - [ ] Pre-flight passes: `ruff check`, `ruff format --check`, `mypy`, `pyright`, `pytest`, route-count smoke; then `dummyindex context rebuild --changed`.
issues.what_happened: The final item of the proposal's checklist — a durable artifact future sessions will execute — instructs only a deterministic `rebuild --changed` after implementation, with no `context reconcile` / `reconcile-stamp` step. Under the current product doctrine (build must end with the reconcile procedure, and the anchor only advances on reconcile-stamp) this bakes a stale workflow into the repo: executing this checklist will leave the curated taxonomy un-reconciled and the drift anchor un-advanced.
issues.root_cause_guess: The repo's wiring is dummyindex 0.15.0, which predates the commit-anchored reconcile loop; the plan skill at that version teaches rebuild as the close-the-loop step, and nothing migrates or lints old proposals/checklists when the doctrine changes.

issues.title: Version self-check only detects CLI/skill divergence, not staleness — 0.15.0 wiring passed the check and silently skipped every newer plan feature
issues.evidence: > **Installed from dummyindex `0.15.0`.** Run `dummyindex --version` to confirm the CLI matches. If they diverge, re-run `dummyindex install --scope user`. [...CLI output:] 0.15.0
issues.what_happened: The skill's only freshness guard passed because the repo's CLI and skill were equally old (both 0.15.0). As a result the whole plan run had none of the newer flow: no critique panel, no wave-grouped checklist, no `equip apply --for-proposal`, and crucially no plugin-manager steps (`— via <tool>` task annotation, `equip discover` for capability gaps, install) — directly relevant to the owner's plugin-wiring concern: the session could not exercise plugin discovery/wiring at all, and nothing surfaced that the install was months behind.
issues.root_cause_guess: The installed-version banner compares skill version to local CLI version only; there is no check against the latest release (and no nudge to /dummyindex-update), so equally-stale CLI+skill pairs look healthy forever and per-repo wiring quietly diverges from the shipped product.

issues.title: Plan flow has no quality gate of its own — a self-contradictory plan.md task only got caught by the external harness advisor
issues.evidence: **1. Fix the garbled sentence in `plan.md` task 5 — blocks (it's a defect in the artifact).** You wrote: > "Strip the `_deprecated`/`$schema`/`name`/`version`/`description`/`transitions` metadata keys are **kept** in storage..." That's self-contradictory ("Strip the … keys are kept").
issues.what_happened: Claude wrote a garbled, both-strip-and-keep instruction into plan.md and was about to declare the proposal done. The catch came from Claude Code's built-in `advisor` server tool ('Three good catches'), not from anything dummyindex provides — the 0.15.0 plan skill has no critique panel and the CLI does no artifact validation, so on a harness without the advisor this defect would have shipped into the execution surface that build agents follow literally.
issues.root_cause_guess: dummyindex-plan 0.15.0 ends at 'write the three docs and report' with no review round; the critique panel added in later versions exists precisely for this, but this repo's wiring never got it (see staleness issue).

issues.title: No guidance on updating canonical .context docs during plan-only work — gap-noting deferred, then drift-avoidance used to justify not touching feature docs
issues.evidence: Advisor: "**2. The standing principle ('if not supported, note it') isn't actually honored yet — act now, don't defer.** You noted the gap thoroughly *inside the proposal*, but propagation to `BACKLOG.md` / `DECISIONS.md` is Phase 4, task 12" — Claude: "the canonical reversal ... is **deferred to execution** — changing a feature's source-of-truth doc before the reversal is approved would itself create `.context` drift."
issues.what_happened: The user's explicit standing instruction ('if something is currently not supoorted it needs to be noted') was initially satisfied only inside the pending proposal folder; the BACKLOG.md note happened only after the advisor overrode the deferral. Claude then reasoned that editing `.context/features/mcp-server/spec.md` now would 'create drift', exposing that the skill gives no rule for when curated docs (BACKLOG/DECISIONS/feature specs) should be updated during a plan-only proposal versus deferred — the model had to improvise the line itself.
issues.root_cause_guess: Neither dummyindex-plan's SKILL.md nor the .context HOW_TO_USE defines the contract between proposals and canonical docs (what may be edited pre-approval, what counts as drift), so each session re-litigates it; the drift model also conflates 'doc edited ahead of code' with staleness.

issues.title: Consistency scan's related_features are mostly noise for the actual feature
issues.evidence: context propose: /mnt/windows-ssd/Projects/opensession/BOS-Mono/backend/.context/proposals/mcp-design-tokens-from-db (4 files) related features: mcp-server, docs-app-bootstrap, assets-resources, integrations-oauth, artifacts-providers
issues.what_happened: For a 'design tokens from Supabase for the MCP server' proposal, the deterministic scan returned 5 related features of which 3 (docs-app-bootstrap, integrations-oauth, artifacts-providers) are unrelated to design tokens; Claude implicitly confirmed the noise by reading and grounding only on mcp-server and assets-resources. Harmless here but it dilutes the grounding signal that proposal.json/spec.md Consistency blocks carry into build agents.
issues.root_cause_guess: Keyword/symbol-overlap retrieval in `context propose` — the title term 'tokens' likely matched OAuth token handling (integrations-oauth) and shared config/bootstrap symbols, with no relevance ranking or threshold before the hits are written into proposal.json.

summary: Plan-only session in the BOS-Mono backend: the user wanted the MCP server's design tokens sourced from Supabase instead of a bundled tokens.json; Claude investigated, invoked /dummyindex-plan (repo wired at dummyindex 0.15.0, matching the local CLI), ran one successful `dummyindex context propose`, and authored a thorough proposal plus a BACKLOG gap note. dummyindex itself performed cleanly — the SessionStart drift hook reported correctly, the single CLI call succeeded, and no tracebacks or usage errors occurred; notably there was zero plugin discover/install/wire activity because the 0.15.0 wiring predates the plugin manager, so the owner's plugin-struggle hypothesis gets no direct evidence here. The real findings are softer: a factually wrong premise baked into the proposal that the grounding flow never caught (corrected only after a user follow-up), stale 0.15.0 wiring passing the skill's version self-check and silently skipping all newer plan/equip/plugin steps, a checklist that violates the skill's flat-list contract with improvised Phase/GATE structure, a rebuild-only (no reconcile/stamp) close-the-loop instruction written into the durable checklist, and artifact/principle defects caught by the harness's external advisor rather than any dummyindex quality gate.

issues.title: Bare `dummyindex install --scope user` destructively rebuilt the repo's curated .context/ (re-clustered into 824 community-N dirs) when user only asked to resync skill files
issues.evidence:
.context/        ->  built (3051 files, 1154 indexed, 1699 symbols)
  CLAUDE.md (proj) ->  managed block written … "This is the re-clustering hazard my core memory explicitly warned about: the `install` ran a **full `.context/` rebuild that re-shattered the repo into raw `community-N` clusters**, destroying the curated 13-feature taxonomy and modifying my just-committed `.context/` files plus `.claude/settings.json`." … "the re-cluster created **824 empty `community-N` skeleton dirs**"
issues.what_happened: User said "resync the skill files" after a version question. Claude ran `dummyindex install --scope user`; despite `--scope user`, the install also did a full AST extraction + re-cluster of the cwd repo's .context/, rewriting ~20 tracked files (INDEX.md, PROJECT.md, graph.json, playbooks, meta.json...), creating 824 empty community-N feature dirs, modifying .claude/settings.json (added reconcile-gate hook) and writing a managed CLAUDE.md block — minutes after a carefully hand-merged rebase commit. Recovery took ~15 tool calls (git checkout HEAD -- .context/, rm -rf community-*, revert settings.json). Claude wrote a memory note because this is a known recurring hazard ("core memory explicitly warned about").
issues.root_cause_guess: `install` unconditionally runs a full .context build (fresh-onboarding path) on the cwd repo even when a curated/enriched index exists and even when only user-scope skill files were requested; no detection of an existing curated taxonomy, no --skills-only flag honored by default.

issues.title: SKILL.md's documented remediation for version skew IS the destructive command (`dummyindex install --scope user`)
issues.evidence: > **Installed from dummyindex `0.24.0`.** Run `dummyindex --version` to confirm the CLI matches. If they diverge, re-run `dummyindex install --scope user`.
issues.what_happened: The /dummyindex skill header (and Claude's own suggestion at the version question: "run `dummyindex install --scope user` if you want them resynced") points users at the exact command that re-clusters a curated repo (issue above). The safe path (/dummyindex-update, which does a NON-DESTRUCTIVE backbone refresh) exists but neither the skill text nor Claude routed to it; Claude only recorded "use /dummyindex-update to resync skills instead" in memory AFTER the damage.
issues.root_cause_guess: Skill documentation was written before install became destructive on curated repos (or vice versa); docs never updated to route resyncs through dummyindex-update / a skills-only install mode.

issues.title: council-batch stage 2 wants to re-run architect over ALL 14 features — pre-0.24 enrichment stage completions not recognized, forcing Claude to abandon the --recouncil loop
issues.evidence: "Stage 2 returns **all 14 features** — not the 3 I refreshed. That's the cache problem resurfacing: the council log has no stage-2/stage-3 completion records for any feature (the original enrichment's stage completions aren't recognized by 0.24.0's council-batch), so the frontier wants to run the architect + critic across everything. Continuing would **clobber every feature's `plan.md`** (including brand-hub's hand-merged plan and home-v3's just-authored one) via ~28 more dispatches — runaway churn driven by missing logs, not real need. I'm stopping the loop here."
issues.what_happened: During the user-requested /dummyindex --recouncil, stage 1 correctly scoped to 3 changed features, but advancing the loop (council-batch --next) returned architect units for all 14 features (brand-hub, chat, brain, ... home-v3). Claude aborted the documented council loop mid-pipeline to avoid clobbering curated plan.md files, leaving stages 2-3 permanently unrun. The recouncil therefore never completed as designed.
issues.root_cause_guess: Council-log/stage-completion records written by an older CLI version (index stamped 0.19.2) are not migrated or recognized by 0.24.0's council-batch frontier, so per-stage dedup fails for everything enriched before the upgrade.

issues.title: Commit-anchored reconcile model (meta.indexed_commit) breaks under rebase/squash workflows — anchor repeatedly orphaned
issues.evidence:
=== is anchor still an ancestor of HEAD? ===
NO — orphaned by rebase … earlier: "246a6e7390f7c23af164ced06ae6dc5e01203fc0 NOT ancestor of dev" — "`246a6e73` (\"reconcile art-direction into curated index\") is **not an ancestor of `origin/development` either** — development itself carries this non-ancestor anchor"
issues.what_happened: Three occurrences in one session: (1) origin/development's own committed anchor was not an ancestor of development (squash-merged PRs), so reconcile diffed against a phantom commit and reported other PRs' drift; (2) after Claude's rebase the anchor pointed at pre-rebase SHAs; (3) after the user's own later rebase ("i just rebased the branch") the anchor d53dc920 was again "orphaned by rebase" and had to be manually re-stamped via reconcile-stamp. Each time required manual forensic git archaeology to separate real drift from anchor breakage.
issues.root_cause_guess: Anchor is a raw commit SHA; rebases/squash merges rewrite history so the anchor stops being an ancestor of HEAD. No tree-hash or content-based fallback, no auto-repair, and no warning from reconcile when the anchor is unreachable from HEAD.

issues.title: Disjoint drift signals (council-batch frontier vs reconcile --json) led Claude to misdiagnose 'corrupted hash cache', skip the requested recouncil, and user re-issued the command
issues.evidence: "The council-batch frontier returned `projects, home, design-system-base` — features **not** in the reconcile drift list. That's a tell that the hash cache was invalidated by the earlier install/revert churn, so `council-batch` now thinks *everything* needs re-councilling. Blindly following it would rewrite all 13 features' docs… The reliable signal is `reconcile --json`."
issues.what_happened: First /dummyindex --recouncil: reconcile --json said drifted_features = [brain, brand-hub, chat, shared-ui-custom, tests] while council-batch flagged [projects, home, design-system-base(, home-v3)] — disjoint sets. Claude distrusted council-batch, declared the cache corrupted (later self-corrected: "The hash cache is actually working"), and substituted its own scoped reconcile instead of the recouncil. The user then typed /dummyindex --recouncil a second time to get the actual recouncil run.
issues.root_cause_guess: Two independent staleness signals (content-hash council cache vs commit-anchored reconcile delta) with no documentation of how/why they diverge; the skill gives no rule for arbitrating them, so the orchestrating model improvises and the user has to repeat the command.

issues.title: mtime-based drift report disagrees with commit-anchored reconcile; only clearable by manually `touch`-ing .context docs (workaround used twice)
issues.evidence: "The commit-anchored reconcile is clean (`has_drift: False`) — that's the authoritative signal. The remaining mtime hints (brain/chat/shared-lib/shared-ui-custom) are the trivial barrel/nav edits I already verified are spec-irrelevant, surfacing only because git checkouts left source mtimes newer tha[n]…" — followed by: "# Touch the reviewed feature docs to clear mtime drift … for f in brain chat shared-lib shared-ui-custom; do touch .context/features/$f/spec.md .context/features/$f/plan.md"
issues.what_happened: After reconcile-stamp reported has_drift: False, the SessionStart-hook view (plan-update) still flagged 6 files across 4 features because git checkout/rebase reset source mtimes. Claude had to manually `touch` spec.md/plan.md files to silence it — done again later for brand-hub after the second rebase. False-positive drift that survives a clean stamp and demands a filesystem hack.
issues.root_cause_guess: plan-update compares filesystem mtimes, which git operations churn arbitrarily; it isn't reconciled with (or suppressed by) the authoritative commit-anchored stamp, and there is no sanctioned 'mark reviewed' verb — touch is the only escape.

issues.title: `rebuild --changed` leaves features/INDEX.json + INDEX.md counts stale; Claude hand-edited generated index files
issues.evidence: "`members` already agree at 348, but `file_count` disagrees: `feature.json` correctly lists 199 files (development's 185 + my 14 illustrations), while `INDEX.json`/`INDEX.md` still show development's stale 185. `feature.json` is the source of truth. Let me sync the two index summaries to match:"
issues.what_happened: After resolving rebase conflicts and running `dummyindex context rebuild --changed`, the per-feature roll-up counts in .context/features/INDEX.json and INDEX.md were not regenerated to match feature.json, so Claude manually Edit-patched two generated files (file_count 185→199). Only much later in the session did it discover/use `refresh-indexes`, which does regenerate them — the rebuild path and the documented reconcile procedure didn't include it at that point.
issues.root_cause_guess: rebuild --changed refreshes only a subset of deterministic artifacts (map/, symbol-graph, meta, source-docs) and does not re-derive feature INDEX summaries from feature.json; procedure docs don't say refresh-indexes is required after manual feature.json edits.

issues.title: SessionStart drift hook reported nonsense ('103 source files across 92 features') after the install re-cluster
issues.evidence: "hookName": "SessionStart:resume" … "## .context/ drift report\n\n103 source files across 92 features have been edited since the matching `.context/features/<id>/` docs were last touched."
issues.what_happened: While the repo was polluted with the install's 824 community-N skeleton dirs, a session resume fired the drift hook and it counted 92 'features' — the shattered community clusters — producing a misleading report injected into the session context (the pre-disaster report correctly said 11 files across 5 features).
issues.root_cause_guess: The drift report enumerates every .context/features/* directory with no sanity check for empty/skeleton community-N dirs, so any re-cluster (even an accidental one) floods the hook output.

issues.title: Tracked generated .context artifacts (graph.json etc.) make every rebase conflict-heavy — 84 conflict markers in one file
issues.evidence:
3 markers : .context/features/INDEX.json
3 markers : .context/features/INDEX.md
3 markers : .context/features/brand-hub/feature.json
6 markers : .context/features/brand-hub/spec.md
84 markers : .context/features/graph.json
3 markers : .context/meta.json
issues.what_happened: The PR rebase onto development conflicted in two separate commits almost entirely inside .context/ (meta.json, INDEX.json/md, graph.json, symbol-graph.json, naming.md, source-docs INDEX, spec.md, feature.json). Claude spent the bulk of the session resolving machine-generated JSON conflicts (strategy: take --ours then regenerate), and curated spec.md/feature.json needed careful manual three-way merging. This is recurring overhead for any branch that touches code (drift commits are committed per the workflow).
issues.root_cause_guess: Large deterministic JSON artifacts are committed to git with no merge driver (.gitattributes union/ours strategy) or 'regenerate on merge' tooling, so dummyindex's own commit-the-index discipline guarantees rebase conflicts.

issues.title: council-batch --json leaks Python enum repr: "subagent_type": "SubagentType.FRONTEND"
issues.evidence: "units": [ { "feature_id": "projects", "stage": 1, "role": "dev", "subagent_type": "SubagentType.FRONTEND", "framework": "React" }, …
issues.what_happened: The machine-readable batch output emits the unserialized enum repr instead of the resolved subagent name (`dev-pick` separately returns "Frontend Developer"). Claude's first parse also printed feature names as None (used 'feature' vs actual 'feature_id'), and it had to call dev-pick per feature anyway to get a dispatchable subagent_type — the --json contract isn't directly usable for Task dispatch.
issues.root_cause_guess: str(enum) serialization bug in the council-batch JSON encoder plus undocumented/unstable field names in the JSON schema.

issues.title: Index version stamp stale (0.19.2) while CLI is 0.24.0 — rebuilds and reconcile-stamps don't advance it
issues.evidence:
=== version stamp / schema ===
dummyindex_version: 0.19.2 | schema: 1 | anchor: d53dc9208b7a  … (earlier) === CLI version ===
0.24.0
issues.what_happened: After multiple rebuild --changed runs, refresh-indexes, and a reconcile-stamp under CLI 0.24.0, meta.json still recorded dummyindex_version 0.19.2. Combined with skill files lagging the CLI and memory recording 0.22.0, version diagnosis required cross-checking three layers — this skew is exactly what prompted the user's "what the dummyindex version ?" and the fateful resync.
issues.root_cause_guess: Only full builds (or install) write dummyindex_version into meta.json; incremental update verbs preserve the old stamp, so an actively-maintained index permanently reports the version that first built it.

issues.title: git index.lock contention in the submodule superproject during post-install cleanup
issues.evidence:
fatal: Unable to create '/mnt/windows-ssd/Projects/opensession/BOS-Mono/.git/modules/frontend/index.lock': File exists.

Another git process seems to be running in this repository, e.g.
an editor opened by 'git commit'.
issues.what_happened: Immediately after the destructive install (which runs git operations and installs hooks), both `git checkout HEAD -- .context/` and the settings.json revert failed on a stale index.lock under the superproject's .git/modules/frontend (the repo is a git submodule of BOS-Mono). No git process was running; Claude had to rm -f the lock manually to proceed with recovery.
issues.root_cause_guess: A dummyindex-spawned git process (install/hook auto-refresh) crashed or overlapped in the submodule's gitdir, leaving the lock; dummyindex doesn't serialize its git access or clean up locks in submodule layouts.

issues.title: Equip-generated agent file (.claude/agents/frontend-performance-specialist.md) surfaces as 'unassigned new files' drift requiring manual placement
issues.evidence: unassigned new files: … .claude/agents/frontend-performance-specialist.md, app/(dashboard)/brand-hub/guidelines/[docId]/page.tsx, app/(dashboard)/home-v3/page.tsx, …
issues.what_happened: The reconcile delta repeatedly listed an equip-generated specialist agent file among unassigned source files; Claude had to assign-files it into the 'tooling' feature to clear the drift report. dummyindex's own generated tooling pollutes its own reconcile workflow.
issues.root_cause_guess: Reconcile's new-file scan doesn't exclude (or auto-assign) dummyindex/equip-managed paths like .claude/agents/**, so every equip render creates placement chores.

summary: Session (2026-06-12, BOS-Mono/frontend, CLI 0.24.0) rebased the brand-hub-illustrations PR onto origin/development while keeping .context/ in line, then handled a skill resync, drift verification, two /dummyindex --recouncil invocations, and a second user-initiated rebase. dummyindex's core read paths worked, but the session surfaced one near-catastrophe — `dummyindex install --scope user` (the SKILL.md-documented resync command) destructively re-clustered the curated 13-feature index into 824 community-N dirs and rewrote settings.json/CLAUDE.md — plus systemic friction: the commit-anchor reconcile model orphaned by every rebase/squash, council-batch stage records unrecognized after the version upgrade (recouncil loop aborted to avoid clobbering all 14 plans), disagreeing drift signals (council hash cache vs reconcile vs mtime report, the last cleared only by touch-hacks), stale generated INDEX counts hand-patched, and heavy rebase conflicts inside committed generated JSON. No plugin discover/install/wiring activity occurred in this session beyond an equip-generated agent file polluting the reconcile drift report.

issues.title: context rebuild --changed destroys curated feature taxonomy, re-shattering it into ~100 community-N stubs
issues.evidence: "One self-inflicted incident, fully reverted: I ran `dummyindex context rebuild --changed` per the plan and it re-shattered the curated `.context` index into ~100 auto `community-N` stubs (the known 0.25.0 bug). I hard-reset that commit, restored the curated index, scrubbed the rebuild step from the proposal, and saved a memory note so it never gets run again" — plus git stat showing `.context/features/community-0/feature.json | 1301 +` and stub text "# Feature: community-99 _Deterministic stub (`confidence: ConfidenceLevel.EXTRACTED`)..."
issues.what_happened: The plan's own Wave-1 step prescribed `dummyindex context rebuild --changed` to fix a stale index. Running it (L407-414) replaced the curated features (brand-hub, chat, ...) with ~100 auto-clustered community-N stubs ("0 added, 6 modified, 16 removed" was misleading — the commit added thousands of stub lines). The assistant had to `git reset --hard HEAD~1` ("Drop the index-shattering rebuild commit"), edit the proposal/checklist to say "Do NOT run rebuild --changed", commit a warning, and write a memory note. The memory note states the frontend had ALREADY reverted its auto-rebuild hooks for this exact bug (commit 4477cef6), and that CLAUDE.md guidance recommending rebuild --changed is stale — so this is a recurring, documented-but-unfixed regression that the tool's own docs and generated plans keep prescribing.
issues.root_cause_guess: 0.25.0 deterministic backbone rebuild no longer respects/preserves the curated (reconciled) taxonomy — it re-runs community clustering from scratch over the AST instead of mapping changed files into existing curated features; shipped CLAUDE.md/skill docs and the plan generator still recommend the destructive command.

issues.title: canvas-to-code plugin installed and wired but never actually invoked — build hand-simulated all 11 gates and ticked the '— via canvas-to-code:start' item anyway
issues.evidence: "Wave 2 ticked. Wave 3 is the bridge — the plugin is cached locally. Loading its `start` command:" (then reads /home/ahmed/.claude/plugins/cache/canvas-to-code-marketplace/canvas-to-code/0.5.0/commands/start.md and agents/*.md as plain files) ... later: "build check: [x] #4 Fresh bridge run from iter-02 emitting the route page + `Illustration*` components ... — via canvas-to-code:start"
issues.what_happened: The checklist item carried the plan's `— via canvas-to-code:start` plugin tag, but the plugin slash command was never executed. Claude instead read the cached plugin .md files out of ~/.claude/plugins/cache, re-enacted the eleven gates itself, dispatched every plugin agent (auditor, mapper, data-binder, planner, implementer slices 1-5) as `subagent_type: general-purpose` with hand-pasted prompts, and mutated the plugin's `.canvas-to-code/state/brand-hub/status.json` directly via inline python. The CLI then ticked the item as done-via-plugin. Recurred for the whole bridge (gates 0-8, 5 slices, reconciliation pass).
issues.root_cause_guess: equip wires plugins into the frontend submodule's .claude/settings.json, but the session was rooted at the BOS-Mono parent, so the plugin's commands/agents were never registered in the harness; the build skill has no availability check and no failure mode — it silently degrades to manual emulation and still marks the via-tagged item complete.

issues.title: Prior dummyindex-build run bridged the page with NO design source — session opens with user reporting a broken result
issues.evidence: User (session opener): "the desigin that was created is no where close to the design in paper, its very broken you need to follow these steps \"/paper-design artboard <your-paper-share-url> /canvas-to-code:start\"" — and the assistant's later confirmation: "the bridge state (`source_iter: null`) shows the bridge never ingested it. Either way, the page was built ungrounded from the canvas — your diagnosis stands."
issues.what_happened: The whole session exists to clean up a previous dummyindex-driven build that emitted /brand-hub/illustrations without ever ingesting a Paper design source (`.canvas-to-code/state/brand-hub/status.json` had `source_iter: null`). The remediation required deleting the entire emitted UI (route page, nine components, six tests), archiving the stale bridge state that blocked the new run, and re-doing the page from a fresh capture. The user had to spell out the correct tool sequence (/paper-design then /canvas-to-code:start) manually.
issues.root_cause_guess: dummyindex-build treated the installed canvas-to-code plugin as optional: nothing in the build loop validates that a via-tagged consumer plugin actually ran with its required input (a design iter), so the bridge ran/was emulated source-less and produced UI from nothing.

issues.title: User had to demand hardening: '— via' tags were hints, not directives — Claude hand-patched the shipped SKILL.md in two places
issues.evidence: User: "i need you to make sure this doesnt happen again dont commit anything and make sure \"dummyindex-build\" command also uses the canvas-to-code" — assistant: "I'll harden the build skill so `— via` tags are binding routing (the bridge can never be skipped or substituted again)" ... "Mirroring to the user-level copy so any session (not just frontend ones) gets the same rules"
issues.what_happened: After the no-source-bridge disaster, the user explicitly demanded the build command be forced to use canvas-to-code. Claude edited frontend/.claude/skills/dummyindex-build/SKILL.md (3 edits) AND ~/.claude/skills/dummyindex-build/SKILL.md to make via-tags 'a directive, not a hint'. These are local patches to dummyindex-shipped skill files — the next `dummyindex install`/`/dummyindex-update` will clobber them, and the fix never reached the product.
issues.root_cause_guess: Shipped dummyindex-build SKILL.md wording is too soft about honoring plan-assigned plugin routing; no enforcement mechanism in the CLI (e.g. refuse `--check` on a via-tagged item without evidence the tool ran).

issues.title: Skill tool invocation of plan-tagged '/design-handoff' failed: Unknown skill — gate manually emulated
issues.evidence: Assistant: "Wave 2 is the handoff gate, tagged `— via /design-handoff` — invoking it as the executor:" → Skill {"skill": "design-handoff"} → "<tool_use_error>Unknown skill: design-handoff</tool_use_error>" — yet the item was later ticked: "build check: [x] #3 Handoff readiness: all four `/design-handoff` decision rules pass on iter-02 ... — via /design-handoff"
issues.what_happened: dummyindex-plan annotated the wave with `— via /design-handoff` (a project skill at frontend/.claude/skills/design-handoff/, which equip's equipment docs reference as the paper-design↔canvas-to-code mediator). The Skill tool rejected it as unknown, even though the sibling skill paper-design from the same directory loaded fine earlier in the session. Claude fell back to Read-ing SKILL.md and applying its four decision rules by hand, then ticked the checklist item as via-skill anyway.
issues.root_cause_guess: Skill registration mismatch — design-handoff was either not registered at session start (multi-line `description: |` frontmatter, or added/changed by a mid-session branch switch) while the via-tag pipeline (plan→checklist→build) never verifies a tagged skill is actually invokable before committing the plan to it.

issues.title: dummyindex-plan produced a fully built, equipped, critiqued proposal for the WRONG page (art-direction vs illustrations)
issues.evidence: "Now committing the iter and fixing the branch name (it referenced art-direction — wrong target):" ... Bash: "rm -rf .context/proposals/art-direction-paper-rebuild && dummyindex context propose --slug illustrations-paper-rebuild --title 'Rebuild /brand-hub/illustrations ... — page was bridged with no design source'" (description: "Replace mis-targeted proposal with illustrations one")
issues.what_happened: The full /dummyindex-plan pipeline ran to completion against /brand-hub/art-direction — spec, plan, 3-agent critique panel, wave checklist, `equip apply --for-proposal`, branch `design/brand-hub-art-direction-iter-03` — before anyone opened the Paper URL. When /paper-design finally opened artboard BCO-0 it was 'Illustrations · Recommended (Step 5)', a different page. Everything had to be redone: rm -rf the proposal, re-propose, rename the branch, cherry-pick across branches (causing conflicts), and re-equip. The user also had to correct mid-stream: "the paper command never ran it takes a screenshot and the code from paper" — the plan had deferred the only grounding step (the capture) into the build phase.
issues.root_cause_guess: dummyindex-plan grounds only in the repo's .context/ and never resolves external design inputs (the Paper artboard) the user supplied, scheduling the capture as a build task instead of a planning input — so the proposal's target was a guess.

issues.title: Stale .context index: propose found '(none detected)' related features, and the only documented refresh path is the destructive rebuild
issues.evidence: CLI: "context propose: .../art-direction-paper-rebuild (4 files) related features: (none detected)" — assistant summary: "Related features: the deterministic scan found none — because the `.context/` index predates the as-built art-direction page (none of its components are in `symbols.json`). The real related feature is `brand-hub`; the plan's Wave 1 includes `dummyindex context rebuild --changed` to fix the stale index."
issues.what_happened: The deterministic related-feature retrieval that grounds proposals returned nothing for a page that plainly belongs to the existing brand-hub feature, because symbols.json predated the as-built code. The prescribed remedy — rebuild --changed — is the command that destroys the curated index (issue 1), and reconcile only runs at build close, leaving no safe way to refresh the backbone mid-flight. The second propose (after some commits) did detect brand-hub et al.
issues.root_cause_guess: Index staleness + a catch-22 in the update model: backbone refresh is destructive and curation-preserving reconcile is only wired into the build-close procedure, so plan-time grounding silently degrades on drifted repos.

issues.title: equip apply output (equipment.json + generated agents) repeatedly caused git checkout/stash/cherry-pick conflicts
issues.evidence: "error: Your local changes to the following files would be overwritten by checkout: .context/equipment.json Please commit your changes or stash them before you switch branches. Aborting" ... then "Auto-merging .context/equipment.json CONFLICT (content): Merge conflict in .context/equipment.json" ... later "AA .claude/agents/frontend-security-specialist.md UU .context/equipment.json error: Committing is not possible because you have unmerged files."
issues.what_happened: Three separate git operations in one session were blocked or conflicted by equip artifacts: branch checkout aborted on a dirty equipment.json, the stash-pop of the equip output conflicted, and a cherry-pick of the proposal commits hit AA/UU conflicts on equipment.json + frontend-security-specialist.md. The assistant's resolution each time was checkout --ours/--theirs and re-running `equip apply` to regenerate ("equip: wrote 8 item(s)" — it rewrites all items unconditionally).
issues.root_cause_guess: equip writes machine-generated, branch-divergent content (hashes/manifest entries) into tracked files with no merge-friendly format and leaves the tree dirty immediately after apply; rewriting all 8 items on every apply maximizes diff churn.

issues.title: Equip-generated specialist agents never dispatched natively — build inlined their rules into general-purpose prompts
issues.evidence: "Emitting all 5 slices via subagents, sequential (each depends on the previous), zero commits. Reading the project-tuned implementer's definition to inline its rules:" — every Agent dispatch in the session (critique panel, gates 3/5b/6/7, slices 1-5, Wave-4 reconciliation) used subagent_type general-purpose (one used the unrelated 'Test Results Analyzer'); prompts begin "You are the **typescript-implementer**..."
issues.what_happened: equip generated .claude/agents/typescript-implementer.md, frontend-reviewer.md, specialists, etc. in frontend/, and dummyindex-build promises to 'dispatch tuned agents'. In practice none were available as subagent types: Claude read each agent file with head/Read and pasted a paraphrase into general-purpose Task prompts, losing the agents' tool restrictions and model pinning. ~12 dispatches in the session, all degraded this way.
issues.root_cause_guess: Agents written into a submodule's .claude/agents aren't registered when the session roots at the mono parent; the build skill neither checks agent availability nor instructs how to dispatch a named project agent, so the equip toolkit's core value silently degrades.

issues.title: Monorepo/submodule root ambiguity: tooling wired in frontend/ but session rooted at BOS-Mono — user told to start a different session
issues.evidence: "Yes, almost — exactly that, with three things to know: 1. **Run it from inside `frontend/`** (not the mono root): start a session in `frontend/` and run `/dummyindex-build illustrations-paper-rebuild`. The design tooling resolves all its paths against the frontend root."
issues.what_happened: The repo is a mono root with the dummyindex-equipped frontend as a submodule. The plan/build skills, equipment, plugins, and .context all live under frontend/, but the session ran at the mono root. Claude had to warn the user to start a new session inside frontend/; the user instead ran /dummyindex-build in the same mono-root session, which 'worked' only because Bash had been cd'd into frontend — while harness-level wiring (plugins, project agents, the design-handoff skill) still resolved from the mono root, directly causing issues 2, 5 and 9.
issues.root_cause_guess: dummyindex has no concept of an equipped subdirectory inside a larger session root — CLI calls follow cwd but harness registration (skills/agents/plugins in settings.json) follows the session root, and nothing detects or reconciles the mismatch.

issues.title: Unrequested auto-commits of .context artifacts — user had to say 'dont commit anything'; the destructive rebuild was committed before inspection
issues.evidence: User: "i need you to make sure this doesnt happen again dont commit anything..." — earlier the assistant had committed the rebuild output and then had to run `git reset --hard HEAD~1` ("Drop the index-shattering rebuild commit")
issues.what_happened: Following dummyindex's 'commit the index as dedicated commits' doctrine, the session auto-committed proposal/equip/index artifacts (~5 commits), including committing the catastrophic rebuild output before checking what it contained, requiring a hard reset. The user then explicitly forbade committing, and all subsequent slices were left uncommitted.
issues.root_cause_guess: Skill guidance to commit .context changes as dedicated commits encourages commit-before-verify and conflicts with this user's no-auto-commit preference; no verification step between rebuild and commit.

issues.title: Plugin wiring location inconsistent/undiscoverable: enabledPlugins in settings.local.json while equipment docs claim settings.json
issues.evidence: grep result: "{ \"enabledPlugins\": { \"impeccable@impeccable\": true }, \"extraKnownMarketplaces\": { \"impeccable\": { \"source\": { \"source\": \"github\", \"repo\": \"pbakaus/impeccable\", \"ref\": \"3.5.0\" }" — found only in frontend/.claude/settings.local.json, while the canvas-to-code equipment doc states "Scope: project (enabled in `.claude/settings.json`)"
issues.what_happened: While verifying the impeccable plugin during planning, Claude found no reference in settings.json and had to grep settings.local.json to confirm the wiring. The equipment record (.context/equipment/*.md) documents the wrong file for at least one plugin, and a .local.json wiring is per-machine/uncommitted — so the equip manifest's claim of where plugins are wired doesn't match reality.
issues.root_cause_guess: equip install writes (or recorded) plugin enablement inconsistently between settings.json and settings.local.json, and the generated equipment docs aren't validated against the actual settings files.

issues.title: Proposal scaffold files trip Write-before-Read errors in the flesh-out workflow
issues.evidence: Write to .context/proposals/art-direction-paper-rebuild/plan.md → "<tool_use_error>File has not been read yet. Read it first before writing to it.</tool_use_error>"
issues.what_happened: `dummyindex context propose` scaffolds spec.md/plan.md/checklist.md which the skill then tells Claude to overwrite. The first Write failed because the harness requires reading existing files first; Claude had to Read each scaffold before rewriting (recurred for plan.md here; same pattern hit 4 more times on paper-design notes files).
issues.root_cause_guess: Skill instructions say to flesh out scaffolded files but don't tell Claude to Read them first (or have the CLI emit empty/absent files instead), so every proposal costs extra failed tool calls.

summary: Session in the BOS-Mono monorepo: the user invoked /dummyindex-plan to fix a /brand-hub page that a prior canvas-to-code bridge run had built with no design source, then drove /dummyindex-build through Paper capture, handoff gate, and the bridge to re-emit the page. The deterministic plumbing (propose, equip apply, build --next-wave/--check) executed without CLI errors, but the plugin/skill wiring layer failed pervasively: the plan targeted the wrong page before the design was ever opened, the via-tagged design-handoff skill was un-invokable ('Unknown skill'), the canvas-to-code plugin was never actually invoked (Claude hand-simulated all eleven gates with general-purpose agents and ticked the via-tagged items anyway), and equip-generated agents were never dispatched natively. The worst single event: `dummyindex context rebuild --changed` — prescribed by the plan itself — re-shattered the curated feature index into ~100 community-N stubs and had to be hard-reset, after which the user demanded SKILL.md hardening ('make sure dummyindex-build command also uses the canvas-to-code', 'dont commit anything') that Claude applied as local patches to the shipped skill files.

issues.title: dummyindex CLI vanished from ~/.local/bin mid-session, breaking reconcile
issues.evidence:
=== raw reconcile output ===
/bin/bash: line 14: dummyindex: command not found
[...next attempt with full path...]
/bin/bash: line 16: /home/ahmed/.local/bin/dummyindex: No such file or directory
[...earlier piped attempt...]
json.decoder.JSONDecodeError: Expecting value: line 1 column 1 (char 0)
issues.what_happened: The CLI worked at session start (resolved at /home/ahmed/.local/bin/dummyindex). In the second half of the session every `dummyindex context reconcile` invocation failed — first surfacing as a confusing JSONDecodeError (empty stdout piped to python), then 'command not found', then 'No such file or directory' even with the absolute path. Claude burned several turns doing a filesystem-wide `find /` and finally found a 0.22.0 copy inside the BACKEND repo's venv (/mnt/.../BOS-Mono/backend/.venv/bin/dummyindex) and used that as a workaround. The ~/.local/bin editable 0.19.2 install had been moved to Trash (user reinstalling concurrently); it reappeared as 0.22.0 minutes later. 3+ failed invocations before recovery.
issues.root_cause_guess: dummyindex is a single uv-tool/editable copy at ~/.local/bin with no fallback resolution (no `python -m dummyindex`, no pinned venv shim). A concurrent upgrade/reinstall deletes the launcher for a window, and every skill/hook/doc assumes `dummyindex` on PATH. The CLI's --json mode also emits nothing on launcher failure, so downstream JSON parsing fails with an opaque error instead of a clear 'CLI missing' message.

issues.title: Drift report and reconcile-stamp permanently polluted by dummyindex's OWN generated files; no ignore mechanism exists
issues.evidence:
context reconcile-stamp: REFUSED — un-reconciled work remains (advancing the anchor would silently forget it):
  unassigned new files: .claude-design/.../guidelines-recommended.tsx, ... .claude/CLAUDE.md, .claude/agents/frontend-reviewer.md, ... .claude/skills/dummyindex-audit/SKILL.md, ... .claude/skills/dummyindex/.dummyindex_version, .claude/skills/dummyindex/SKILL.md, .claude/skills/dummyindex/agents/architect.md, ... [55 files]
[...]
error: unknown context subcommand 'ignore'
issues.what_happened: 55 'unassigned new files' — mostly dummyindex's own installed skill/council/agent files under .claude/skills/dummyindex*/, equip-generated agents, plus .claude-design/ inputs and docs/spikes — blocked `reconcile-stamp` and filled the SessionStart drift report wall-of-text. Claude explicitly searched the CLI for an ignore/exclude mechanism (`dummyindex context ignore --help` → exit 2, grep of all help text → nothing) and concluded 'No ignore mechanism exists — force-stamp is the sanctioned path'. The same 55 files had already been force-stamped past in a prior session and resurfaced after the rebase; `--force` is required every single cycle. Recurred across both halves of the session.
issues.root_cause_guess: Reconcile treats every new file outside a feature's file list as un-reconciled work, with no ignore list / .dummyindexignore / config exclusion globs, and crucially does not exempt its own install footprint (.claude/skills/dummyindex**, equip outputs) from drift accounting — the tool flags itself as drift forever.

issues.title: Commit-anchored reconcile invalidated by rebase — anchor became a dangling commit and the whole reconcile had to be redone
issues.evidence:
=== HEAD vs anchor ===
HEAD:   92c96bb510da84e00a8d807af8591798e3a5d7fa
anchor: 52f4fa6c670a4cbf73610c58409c39c4f8238f23
[...]
=== is 52f4fa6c still in history? ===
NO — history rewritten (rebase)
issues.what_happened: Claude completed a full reconcile cycle and stamped the anchor to HEAD (52f4fa6c). The user then rebased again and re-sent the identical prompt ('i have rebased the repo i need you to 1. update the .context...'). The stamped anchor now pointed at a dangling commit; the CLI gave no rebase warning — Claude had to manually run `git merge-base --is-ancestor` to diagnose that history was rewritten, then redo the reconcile/enrich/stamp cycle against the new equivalent commit 92c96bb5. The user's repeat of the exact same request shows the first pass's output didn't survive their workflow.
issues.root_cause_guess: The Model-B anchor stores a raw commit SHA with no rebase detection (no ancestor check, no fallback to the equivalent rewritten commit by patch-id/message). Works only while the SHA stays reachable; after history rewrite it silently compares against a dangling object and the operator must diagnose and re-drive everything manually.

issues.title: `rebuild --changed` re-runs AST extraction over all 1086 files when only 11 changed
issues.evidence:
=== run rebuild --changed (refresh backbone for 11 changed files) ===
  AST extraction: 100/1086 files (9%)
  AST extraction: 200/1086 files (18%)
  ...
  AST extraction: 1086/1086 files (100%)
context rebuild: enriched index preserved; refreshed 7 deterministic artefact(s) (no re-cluster).
issues.what_happened: Claude invoked `rebuild --changed` expecting an incremental refresh for the 11 files changed since the anchor, but the progress output shows a full-repo AST extraction pass (1086/1086). The result was correct (only 6 .context files changed, enrichment preserved), but the --changed flag does not limit extraction work, making the 'incremental' path scale with repo size.
issues.root_cause_guess: rebuild --changed only scopes which artefacts are rewritten, not the AST extraction phase — there's no extraction cache or changed-file filter feeding the extractor, so --changed is incremental in name only.

issues.title: Skill files, repo wiring, and index stamp lag the CLI after upgrade (0.19.2 vs 0.22.0)
issues.evidence:
=== CLI vs skill version ===
skill: 0.19.2
CLI (venv): 0.22.0
[assistant:] **Skill files lag the CLI** — `.claude/skills/dummyindex/**` is still `0.19.2` while the CLI is now `0.22.0`. Everything worked ... but to fully finish the upgrade you'd run `dummyindex install --scope user` (rewrites the ~50 skill procedure files).
issues.what_happened: After the mid-session CLI reinstall (0.19.2 editable → 0.22.0), the repo-vendored skill family (.claude/skills/dummyindex/.dummyindex_version = 0.19.2), meta.json's dummyindex_version (0.19.2), and the ~50 council/procedure files all remained at the old version. Claude also had to defensively warn it would 'check the diff scale before trusting' rebuild under 0.22.0 because of the version skew. The upgrade required a separate manual `dummyindex install --scope user` that was offered but never run in-session, leaving the three layers permanently skewed.
issues.root_cause_guess: CLI package upgrade and skill/wiring refresh are separate manual steps (the /dummyindex-update skill exists for this but wasn't what the user did); nothing detects or warns about CLI-vs-skill version skew at invocation time.

issues.title: mark-enriched clears the marker but drift state stays fully dirty until a force-stamp — confusing multi-step choreography
issues.evidence:
=== mark-enriched brand-hub ===
context mark-enriched: cleared pending-enrichment for brand-hub
=== re-read delta ===
awaiting_enrichment: []
drifted_features: ['app-shell', 'brain', 'brand-hub', 'shared-lib', 'shared-ui-custom']
unassigned count: 55
removed_files: []
has_drift: True
issues.what_happened: After enriching all five drifted features and running mark-enriched, the reconcile delta still reported all five as drifted with has_drift: True — only reconcile-stamp (which then REFUSED without --force, see the unassigned-files issue) clears it. The full happy path is reconcile → enrich each feature → mark-enriched → stamp (refused) → stamp --force: five manual steps Claude had to choreograph twice in one session. A prior session had also abandoned mid-choreography (backbone rebuilt but 'left uncommitted and un-reconciled: the anchor was stuck at the old commit, brand-hub carried a .pending-enrichment marker').
issues.root_cause_guess: Drift is defined purely as anchor-vs-HEAD file delta, so per-feature enrichment work is invisible to the drift report until the anchor moves; combined with the no-ignore issue, the only terminal state is a forced stamp. The multi-verb workflow has no single 'finish the reconcile' command and is easy to abandon half-done.

issues.title: Inconsistent plugin wiring scope: canvas-to-code wired team-wide, impeccable wired local-only
issues.evidence:
=== plugins enabled in settings ===
settings.json: ['canvas-to-code@canvas-to-code-marketplace']
settings.local.json: ['impeccable@impeccable']
=== marketplaces registered ===
settings.json mkts: ['canvas-to-code-marketplace']
settings.local.json mkts: ['impeccable']
issues.what_happened: The two plugins the user asked about ended up wired at different scopes: canvas-to-code in committed .claude/settings.json (team-wide) but impeccable only in .claude/settings.local.json (gitignored, this machine only), despite both being installed through the same dummyindex equip flow. Nobody chose this deliberately in-session; Claude just reported it as fact ('your local'), so teammates cloning the repo get canvas-to-code but not impeccable even though equipment.json and the committed playbooks reference both.
issues.root_cause_guess: equip install writes enabledPlugins/extraKnownMarketplaces to whichever settings file it targets without a consistent scope policy or a disclosure step ('commit team-wide vs local?'), producing silently divergent wiring for plugins installed at different times.

issues.title: Skill-only plugins (impeccable) cannot be dispatched by /dummyindex-build — gap papered over with playbook prose
issues.evidence: **impeccable is a skill, not an agent** — `/dummyindex-build` dispatches *agents*, so an impeccable step must be authored as a **general-purpose** checklist item that invokes `/impeccable …`. This is documented in `playbooks/design-workflow.md` so it routes correctly at plan time.
issues.what_happened: The user's explicit goal was 'make sure the new plugins ... work through dummyindex'. For impeccable the honest answer was that the build loop can't dispatch it at all (subagent_type: None in equipment.json) — it only 'works through dummyindex' if a human/planner remembers to author checklist items as general-purpose steps that manually invoke /impeccable, a convention enforced only by a markdown playbook. Half the user's request is satisfied by documentation rather than mechanism.
issues.root_cause_guess: The equip/build integration model assumes plugins expose agents; skill-only and command-only plugins have no execution adapter in the build dispatcher, so equipment entries for them are inert metadata.

issues.title: Plugin equipment entries missing lifecycle metadata (grounded_in was empty; version/origin_hash null)
issues.evidence:
-      "grounded_in": [],
+      "grounded_in": [
+        ".context/playbooks/impeccable.md",
+        ".context/playbooks/design-workflow.md"
+      ],
       "subagent_type": null,
       "version": null,
       "origin_hash": null,
issues.what_happened: The equipment.json entries created when the plugins were installed shipped with empty grounded_in (a prior session had to hand-patch the playbook links in) and version: null / origin_hash: null for marketplace plugins whose versions are known (3.5.0, 0.5.0). The hash-baselined never-clobber lifecycle and the 'grounded in the index' promise depend on exactly these fields being populated at install time.
issues.root_cause_guess: equip's plugin-install path writes a minimal schema-v3 equipment item and never backfills origin version/hash from the marketplace ref or links the generated playbooks it creates alongside, leaving lifecycle tracking fields permanently null.

issues.title: No built-in 'verify plugin wiring' verb — verification hand-rolled three times and the user still had to ask again
issues.evidence: [user, after two completed verification reports:] is canvas to code and impeccable designed wire correctly and read to be used ?
issues.what_happened: To answer 'do the plugins work through dummyindex', Claude wrote bespoke multi-line python/bash verification scripts three separate times (equipment grounded_in existence checks, settings.json/local parsing, plugin cache dir listing, .canvas-to-code config presence). Even after two 'fully wired' summaries the user came back and asked the same question in plain words, indicating the tool gives no single trustworthy readiness signal. There is no `dummyindex equip status --plugins`-style check that validates the whole chain (settings → marketplace → cache on disk → equipment entry → playbooks).
issues.root_cause_guess: equip has status/refresh for generated files but no end-to-end doctor/verify command for installed plugins, so wiring confidence depends on ad-hoc scripting each time.

issues.title: SessionStart/Stop hooks silently no-op when the CLI is missing, and the Stop hook is misleadingly named AUTO_REFRESH
issues.evidence:
"command": "# DUMMYINDEX_AUTO_REFRESH
command -v dummyindex >/dev/null 2>&1 || exit 0
dummyindex context memory nudge --root "$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR" 2>/dev/null || true
exit 0
"
issues.what_happened: The installed hooks guard with `command -v dummyindex || exit 0` and `2>/dev/null || true`. During the window when the CLI vanished from ~/.local/bin, every drift report / memory nudge would silently disappear with no signal that the dummyindex layer was broken — Claude only discovered the missing binary because a manual reconcile failed. Separately, the hook block is labeled DUMMYINDEX_AUTO_REFRESH but actually runs the session-memory nudge, which would mislead anyone auditing settings.json (and matches a project rule that NO hook may refresh/stamp).
issues.root_cause_guess: Deliberate fail-open hook design with no degraded-mode notice ('dummyindex CLI not found — drift reporting disabled'), plus a stale comment/label left over from an earlier auto-refresh design.

issues.title: Reconcile-stamp run unconditionally twice (plain then --force) as defensive scripting because refusal is the norm
issues.evidence:
=== stamp (force: intentionally-unowned tooling/design/docs may resurface) ===
context reconcile-stamp: anchor advanced to 92c96bb510da
--- if refused, force ---
context reconcile-stamp: anchor advanced to 92c96bb510da
issues.what_happened: Because earlier stamps were refused over the perpetual unowned files, Claude scripted stamp-then-force-stamp as a pattern; in this run the plain stamp already succeeded, so the --force stamp ran redundantly anyway. Shows the operator model has shifted to 'always force', which defeats the safety check the refusal is supposed to provide.
issues.root_cause_guess: Consequence of the missing ignore mechanism: when refusal fires on known-irrelevant files every cycle, agents learn to blanket-force, eroding the guardrail.
